24.44: if she tells me to drink and also draws for the camels, may she be the woman that the Lord has chosen for my master's son.43
24.45: "Before I had finished praying, along came Rebekah with her jug on her shoulder! She went to the spring and drew some water. I asked her to please let me have a drink.
24.46: She quickly lowered her jug from her shoulder and told me, 'Have a drink while I also water your camels.' So I drank, and she also gave my camels water to drink.45
24.47: "That's when I asked, 'Whose daughter are you?' "She replied, 'I'm the daughter of Bethuel, Nahor's son, whom Milcah bore to him.' "So I gave her a ring for her nose and bracelets for her wrists.
24.48: I bowed down and worshipped the Lord, and I praised the Lord God of my master Abraham, who led me on the true way to request the daughter of my master's brother for his son.
24.49: So now, if you wish to show gracious love and truth toward my master, tell me so. But if not, tell me, so that I may go elsewhere."
24.50: "Since this has come from the Lord," Laban and Bethuel both replied, "we cannot speak one way or another.
24.51: So here's Rebekah - she's right in front of you. Take her and go, so she can become a wife for your master's son, just as the Lord has decreed."
24.52: When Abraham's servant heard what they had said, he bowed down to the ground before the Lord.
24.53: Then the servant brought out some silver and gold items, along with some clothing, and gave them to Rebekah. He also gave gifts to her brother and to her mother.
24.54: He and the men with him ate and drank, and then they spent the night. When they got up the next morning, the servant requested, "Send me off to my master."
24.55: But her brother and mother said, "Let the young lady stay with us a few days - at least ten - and after that she may go."
24.56: "Please don't delay me," the servant answered them. "The Lord has made my journey successful. Send me off so I can return to my master."
24.57: But they said, "We'll call the young lady and see what she has to say about this."
24.58: So they called Rebekah and asked her, "Do you want to go with this man?" "I will go," she replied.
24.59: So they sent off their sister Rebekah, along with her personal assistant, Abraham's servant, and his men.
24.60: As they were leaving, they all blessed Rebekah, saying,       "Our sister, may you become the mother of tens of millions! May your descendants take over the city gates of those who hate them."
24.61: Then Rebekah and her young servant women got up, mounted their camels, and followed Abraham's servant, who took Rebekah and went on his way.
24.62: Later on, as Isaac was returning one evening from Beer-lahai-roi (he had been living in the Negev region),
24.63: went out walking in a field. He looked up, and all of a sudden there were some camels coming.
24.64: Rebekah looked up, and when she saw Isaac she quickly dismounted from the camel
24.65: and asked the servant, "Who is that man coming in the field to meet us?" "That's my master," the servant told her. So she reached for a veil and covered herself.
24.66: Then the servant informed Isaac about everything he had done.
24.67: Later, Isaac brought Rebekah into the tent that had belonged to his mother Sarah and married her. Isaac loved her, and that's how he was comforted following the loss of his mother.

25.1: Abraham had taken another wife whose name was Keturah.
25.2: She bore him Zimran, Jokshan, Medan, Midian, Ishbak, and Shuah.
25.3: Jokshan was the father of Sheba and Dedan. Dedan's sons were the Asshurites, Letushites, and Leummites.
25.4: Midian's sons were Ephah, Epher, Hanoch, Abida, and Eldaah. All of these were Keturah's descendants.
25.5: Abraham gave everything he owned to Isaac.
25.6: While he was still alive, Abraham gave gifts to his concubines and sent them to the east country in order to keep them away from his son Isaac.
25.7: Abraham lived for a total of 175 years.
25.8: Then Abraham passed away, dying at a ripe old age, having lived a full life, and was joined to his people.
25.9: His sons Isaac and Ishmael buried him in the cave of Machpelah near Mamre, in the field that used to belong to Zohar the Hethite's son Ephron.
25.10: This was the same field that Abraham had bought from the son of Heth, where Abraham and his wife Sarah were buried.
25.11: After Abraham's death, God blessed his son Isaac, who continued to live near Beer-Lahai-Roi.10
25.12: Now this is what happened to Ishmael, whom Sarah's Egyptian servant Hagar bore for Abraham.
25.13: Here's a list of the names of Ishmael's sons, recorded by their names and descendants： Nebaioth was the first born, followed by Kedar, Adbeel, Mibsam,
25.15: Hadad, Tema, Jetur, Naphish, and Kedemah.
25.16: These were Ishmael's children, listed by their names according to their villages and their camps. There were a total of twelve tribal chiefs, according to their clans.
25.17: Ishmael lived for 137 years, then he took his last breath, died, and joined his people.
25.18: His descendants settled from Havilah to Shur (that's near Egypt), all the way to Assyria, in defiance of all of his relatives.16
25.19: This is the account of Isaac, Abraham's son. Abraham fathered Isaac.
25.20: Isaac was forty years old when he married Rebekah, the daughter of Bethuel, the Syrian from Paddan-Aram and sister of Laban the Syrian.
25.21: Later, Isaac prayed to the Lord on behalf of his wife, since she was unable to conceive children, and the Lord responded to him - his wife Rebekah became pregnant.
25.22: But when the infants kept on wrestling each other inside her womb, she asked herself, "Why is this happening?" So she sought the Lord for an explanation.
25.23: "Two nations are in your womb," the Lord responded, "and two separate people will emerge. One people will be the stronger, and the older one will serve the younger."
25.24: Sure enough, when her due date to deliver arrived, she delivered twins.
25.25: The first came out reddish - his entire body being covered with hair - so they named him Esau.
25.26: After that, his brother came out with his hand clutching Esau's heel, so the named him Jacob. Isaac was 60 years old when they were born.
25.27: As the boys were growing up, Esau became skilled at hunting and was a man of the outdoors, but Jacob was the quiet type who tended to stay indoors.
25.28: Isaac loved Esau, because he loved to hunt, while Rebekah loved Jacob.
25.29: One day, while Jacob was cooking some stew, Esau happened to come in from being outdoors, and he was feeling famished.
25.30: Esau said to Jacob, "Let me gobble down some of this red stuff, since I'm starving. (That's how Esau got his nickname "Edom".)
25.31: But Jacob responded, "Sell me your birthright. Do it now."
25.32: "Look! I'm about to die." Esau replied. "What good is this birthright to me?"
25.33: But Jacob insisted, "Swear it by an oath right now." So he swore an oath to him and sold his birthright to Jacob.
25.34: Then Jacob gave Esau some of his food and boiled stew, and Esau ate, drank, got up, and left, after having belittled his own birthright.

26.1: Later on, a famine swept through the land. This was a different famine from the previous one that occurred during Abraham's lifetime. So Isaac went to Abimelech, king of the Philistines, at Gerar.
26.2: That's when the Lord appeared to him. "You are not to go down to Egypt," he said. "Instead, you are to settle down in the land in the area where I'll tell you.
26.3: Remain in this land, and I'll be with and will bless you by giving all these lands to you and to your descendants in fulfillment of my solemn promise that I made to your father Abraham.
26.4: "I'll multiply your descendants as the star of the heavens, and I'll certainly give all these lands to your descendants. Later on, through your descendants all the nations of the earth will bless one another.
26.5: I'm going to do this because Abraham did what I told him to do. He kept my instructions, commands, statutes, and laws."
26.7: Later on, the men of the place asked about his wife, so he replied, "She's my sister," because he was afraid to call her "My wife." He kept thinking, "…otherwise, the men around here will kill me on account of Rebekah, since she is very beautiful."
26.8: After he had been there a while, King Abimelech of the Philistines looked out through a window and saw Isaac caressing his wife Rebekah.
26.9: So Abimelech called Isaac and confronted him. "She is definitely your wife!" he accused him, "so why did you claim, 'She's my sister?'" Isaac responded to him, "Because I had thought '…otherwise, I'll die on account of her.'"
26.10: "What have you done to us?"Abimelech asked. "Any minute now one of the people could have had sex with your wife and you would have brought guilt on all of us."
26.11: So he issued this order to all the people： "Whoever touches this man or his wife is to be executed." 10
26.12: Isaac received a 100-fold return on what he planted that year in the land he received, because the Lord blessed him.
26.13: The man became very wealthy and lived a life of wealth, becoming more and more wealthy.
26.14: He owned so many sheep, cattle, and servants that the Philistines eventually became envious of him.
26.15: They filled in with sand all of the wells that his father Abraham's servants had dug during his lifetime.12
26.16: Then Abimelech ordered Isaac, "Move away from us! You've become more powerful than we are."
26.17: So Isaac moved from there and encamped in the Gerar Valley, where he settled.
26.18: Isaac re-excavated some water wells that his father had first dug during his lifetime, because the Philistines had blocked them after Abraham's death. Isaac renamed them with the same names that his father had called them.
26.19: While Isaac's servants were digging in the valley, they discovered a well with flowing water.
26.20: But the herdsmen who lived in Gerar quarreled with Isaac's herdsmen. "The water is ours," they said. As a result, Isaac named the well Esek, for they had fiercely disputed with him about it.
26.21: When his workers started digging another well, they quarreled about that one, too, so he named it Sitnah.
26.22: Then he left that area and dug still another well. Because they did not quarrel over that one, he named it Rehoboth, because he used to say, "The Lord has enlarged the territory for us. We will prosper in the land."
26.23: Later on, he left there and went to Beersheba,
26.24: where one night the Lord appeared to him. "I'm the God of your father Abraham," he told him. "Don't be afraid, because I'm with you. I'm going to bless you and multiply your descendants on account of my servant Abraham."
26.25: In response, Isaac built an altar there and called on the name of the Lord. He also pitched his tents there and his servants dug a well.
26.26: Later, Abimelech traveled to visit Isaac from Gerar. He arrived with Ahuzzath, his staff advisor, and Phicol, the commanding officer of his army. "Why have you come to see me," Isaac asked them, "since you hate me so muc h that you sent me away from you?"
26.28: "We've seen that the Lord is with you," they responded, "so we're proposing an agreement between us - between us and you. Allow us to make a treaty with you
26.29: by which you'll agree not to do us any harm, just as we haven't harmed you, since we've done nothing but good for you after we sent you away in peace. As a result, you've been tremendously blessed by the Lord."
26.30: So Isaac held a feast for them and they ate and drank.
26.31: They woke up early the next morning and made the treaty. After this, Isaac sent them off and they left on peaceful terms.
26.32: That very same day, Isaac's servants arrived and reported to him about a well that they had just completed digging. "We've found water!" they said.
26.33: So Isaac named the well Shebah, which is why the city is named Beersheba to this day.
26.34: When Esau was 40 years old, he married Judith, the daughter of Beeri the Hittite and Basemath, the daughter of Elon the Hittite.
26.35: This brought extreme grief to Isaac and Rebekah.

27.1: Eventually, Isaac grew so old that he could not see. One day, he called his eldest son Esau. "My son," he called out to him. "I'm here," he responded.
27.2: "Look how old I am! I could die any day now,
27.3: so go find your weapons, take your bow and arrows, go outside, and hunt some game for me.
27.4: Then prepare some food, just the way I like it, and bring it to me so that I can eat and bless you before I die."
27.5: Now Rebekah overheard Isaac while he was speaking to his son Esau. When Esau had gone out to the field to hunt and bring in some game,
27.6: Rebekah gave these instructions to her son Jacob： "Quick! Pay attention!" she said. "I heard your father talking to your brother Esau.
27.7: He told him, 'Bring me some game and then prepare some food for me so I can eat and bless you in the presence of the Lord before I die.'
27.8: "So now, my son, listen to what I have to say and pay attention to what I'm about to tell you.
27.9: Go to the flock and bring me two healthy young goats. I'll prepare some delicious food for your father, just the way he loves it.
27.10: Then you are to take it to your father so that he can eat and bless you before he dies."
27.11: "But look!" Jacob pointed out to his mother Rebekah, "My brother Esau is a hairy man, but I'm smooth skinned.
27.12: My father might touch me and he'll realize that I'm deceiving him. Then, I'll bring a curse on myself instead of a blessing."
27.13: "My son," she replied, "let any curse against you fall on me. Just listen to me, then go and get them for me."
27.14: So out he went, got them, and brought them to his mother, who then prepared some delicious food according to his father's liking.
27.15: Then Rebekah took some garments that belonged to her elder son Esau - the best ones available - and put them on her younger son Jacob.
27.16: She put some goat skins over his hands and on the smooth part of his neck.
27.17: Then she handed the delicious food and bread that she had prepared over to her son Jacob,
27.18: who went in to his father and said, "My father…" "It's me," he replied. "Which one are you, my son?"
27.19: "I'm Esau, your first born," Jacob said to his father, "I've done what you asked, so please sit up and eat what I caught so you can bless me."
27.20: "How did you catch it so quickly, my son?" Isaac asked. Jacob responded, "…because the Lord your God made me successful."
27.21: So Isaac said to Jacob, "Come here, my son, so I can feel you and know for sure whether or not you're my son Esau."
27.22: So Jacob approached his father, who felt him and said, "It's Jacob's voice, but Esau's hands."
27.23: He didn't recognize Jacob, because his hands were hairy like those of his brother Esau, so Isaac blessed him.
27.24: He asked, "Are you really my son Esau?" "I am," Jacob replied.
27.25: "Come closer to me," Isaac replied, "so I can eat some of the game, my son, and then bless you." So Isaac came closer, and Isaac ate. He also brought wine so he could drink.
27.26: After this, Jacob's father Isaac told him, "Come closer and kiss me, my son."
27.27: So he drew closer to kiss him. When he smelled the scent of his clothes, he blessed him and said, "How my son's scent is the fragrance of the field, That the Lord has blessed.
27.28: May the Lord grant you dew from the skies, and from the fertile land may he grant you abundant grain and fresh wine.
27.29: May people serve and bow before you; may you be master over your brothers; may your mother's sons bow before you; May anyone who curses you be cursed; and may anyone who blesses you be blessed.
27.30: Just after Isaac had finished blessing Jacob and Jacob had left his father Isaac's presence, his brother Esau returned from hunting,
27.31: prepared some delicious food, brought it to his father, and told him, "Can you get up now, father, so you may eat some of your son's game and then bless me?"
27.32: But Isaac's father asked him, "Who are you?" "I'm Esau, your first born son," he answered
27.33: At this, Isaac began to tremble violently.
27.34: "Who then," he asked, "hunted some game and brought it to me to eat before you  arrived, so that I've blessed him -  Indeed, he is blessed." When Esau realized what his father Isaac was saying, he began to wail out loud bitterly. "Bless me," he cried, "even me, too, my father!"
27.35: Isaac replied, "Your brother came here deceitfully and stole your blessing."
27.36: Then he said, "Isn't his name rightly called Jacob?' Esau asked. "He has circumvented me this second time. First, he took away my birthright, and now, look how he also stole my blessing." Then he added, "Haven't you reserved a blessing for me -
27.37: In response, Isaac told Esau, "Look! I've predicted that he's going to become your master, and I've assigned all his brothers to be his servants. What then can I do for you, my son?"
27.38: Then Esau implored his father, "Don't you have even one blessing for me, my father -  Bless me, even me too, my father!" Then Esau lifted his voice and wept bitterly.
27.39: In response, his father Isaac replied to him, "Look! Away from the fertile land will be your dwellings; away from the dew of the skies above.
27.40: By your sword you'll live; but you'll serve your brother. But when you've become restless, you'll break off his yoke from your neck."
27.41: So Esau harbored animosity toward Jacob because of the way his father had blessed him. Esau kept saying in his heart, "The time to mourn for my father is very near. That's when I'm going to kill my brother Jacob."
27.42: Eventually, what Rebekah's older son Esau had been saying was reported to her, so she sent for her younger son Jacob and warned him, "Look! Your brother is planning to get even by killing you.
27.43: Son, you'd better do what I say! Get up, run off to my brother Laban in Haran,
27.44: and stay there with him a few days until your brother's fury sub sides.
27.45: After that happens and he has forgotten what you've done to him, I'll send for you and take you from there. Why should I be bereaved of you both in one day?"27
27.46: Rebekah also told herself, "Heth's daughters are making me tired of living. If Jacob marries one of Heth's daughters, and she turns out to be just like these other local women, what kind of life would there be left for me?"

28.1: Later, Isaac called Jacob and blessed him, instructing him, "Don't marry a wife from the local Canaanite women.
28.2: Instead, get up, travel to Paddan-Aram, and visit the household of Bethuel, your mother's father. Marry one of Laban's daughters, since he's your mother's brother.
28.3: May God Almighty bless you and make you fruitful so that you become a whole group of people.
28.4: May he give you and your descendants the blessings that he gave Abraham. May you possess the land where you have lived that God gave to Abraham."
28.5: So Isaac sent Jacob off toward Paddan-Aram to visit Laban, Bethuel's son, the Syrian and brother of Rebekah, the mother of Jacob and Esau.
28.6: Esau noticed that after Isaac had blessed Jacob as he sent him off to Paddan-Aram to marry a wife from there, he instructed Jacob, "Don't marry a Canaanite woman."
28.7: After Jacob had obeyed his father and mother's instructions to set out for Paddan-Aram,
28.8: Esau realized that Canaan women didn't please his father Isaac,
28.9: so he went to Abraham's son Ishmael and married Ishmael's daughter Mahalath, who was the sister of Nebaioth.
28.10: Meanwhile, Jacob had left Beersheba and was on his way to Haran.
28.11: He reached a certain place and spent the night there, because the sun was setting. He found a stone there, used it for a pillow, and slept in that place for the night.
28.12: That night he had a dream! He saw a raised highway that had been built with its ending point on earth and its beginning point in heaven. God's angels were ascending and descending on it.
28.13: And there was the Lord, standing above it and telling Jacob, "I am the Lord God of your grandfather Abraham. I'm Isaac's God, too. I'm giving you and your descendants the ground on which you're sleeping.
28.14: Your descendants are going to become like the dust of the earth and spread out to the west, east, north, and south. All the families of the earth will be blessed through you and your descendants.
28.15: Now pay attention! I'm here with you, and I'm going to be watching over you wherever you go. I'm going to bring you back to this land, because I won't ever leave you until I've accomplished what I've promised about you."
28.16: Then Jacob woke up during the night and said to himself, "Surely, the Lord is in this place and I never knew it!"
28.17: In mounting terror, he cried out, "How scary this place is! This is nothing less than God's house and the gateway to heaven!"
28.18: When Jacob got up early the next morning, he took the stone that he had used for  his pillow, set it up as a pillar, drenched it in oil,
28.19: and named the place Beth-el, although previously the city had been named Luz.
28.20: Then he made this solemn vow： "If God remains with me, watches over me throughout this journey that I'm taking, gives me food to eat and clothes to wear,
28.21: and returns me safely to my father's house, then the Lord will be my God,
28.22: this stone that I've erected in the form of a pillar will be God's house, I'll give you a tenth of everything that you give to me."

29.1: Jacob journeyed on and reached the territory that belonged to the people who lived in the east.
29.2: As he was observing a well that had been dug out on the open range, all of a sudden he noticed three flocks of sheep lying there, because shepherds watered their flocks from that well. There was a very large stone that covered the opening of the well,
29.3: and when all the flocks had been gathered there, they would roll away the stone from the opening of the well, water their flocks, and then return the stone to its place covering the opening of the well.
29.4: Jacob asked them, "My brothers, where are you from?" "We're from Haran," they answered.
29.5: "Do you happen to know Nahor's son Laban?" he inquired. "We do," they replied.
29.6: So he asked them, "How's he doing?" "Very well," they answered. "As a matter of fact, look over there! That's his daughter Rachel, coming here with his sheep."
29.7: "Look!" Jacob replied. "The sun is still high. It's not yet time for the flocks to be gathered. Let's water the sheep, then let them graze."
29.8: But they responded, "We can't do that until all the sheep have been gathered and the stone has been rolled away from the opening of the well. Only then can we cause the flock to drink."
29.9: While he was still talking with them, Rachel arrived with his father's sheep, since she was a shepherdess.
29.10: When Jacob saw Rachel, the daughter of Laban, his mother's brother, accompanied by Laban's sheep, Jacob approached the well, rolled the stone from the opening of the well, and then watered his mother's brother Laban's flock.
29.11: Then Jacob kissed Rachel and began to cry out loud.
29.12: Jacob told Rachel that he was related to her father, since he was Rebekah's son, so she ran and told her father.
29.13: When Laban heard the news about his sister's son Jacob, he ran out to meet him. He embraced him, kissed him, and brought him back to his house. Then Jacob told Laban about everything that had happened.
29.14: Laban responded, "You certainly are my flesh and blood!" So Jacob stayed with him for about a month.
29.15: Later, Laban asked Jacob, "Should you serve me for free, just because you're my nephew -  Let's talk about what your wages should be."
29.16: Now Laban happened to have two daughters. The older one was named Leah and the younger was named Rachel.
29.17: Leah looked rather plain, but Rachel was lovely in form and appearance.
29.18: Jacob loved Rachel, so he made this offer to Laban： "I'll serve you for seven years for Rachel, your younger daughter."
29.19: "It's better that I give her to you than to another man," Laban replied, "so stay with me."
29.20: Jacob served seven years for Rachel, but it seemed like only a few days because of his love for her.
29.21: Eventually, Jacob told Laban, "Bring me my wife, now that my time of service has been completed, so I can go be with her."
29.22: So Laban gathered all the men who lived in that place and held a wedding feast.
29.23: That night Laban took his daughter Leah and brought her to Jacob. He had marital relations with her.
29.24: Laban also gave his servant woman Zilpah to Leah to be her maidservant.
29.25: The next morning, Jacob realized that it was Leah! "What have you done to me?" he demanded of Laban. "Didn't  I serve you for seven years in order to marry Rachel -  Why did you deceive me?"
29.26: But Laban responded, "It's not the practice of our place to give the younger one in marriage before the first born.
29.27: Fulfill the week for this one, then we'll give you the other one in exchange for serving me another seven years."
29.28: So Jacob completed another seven years' work, and then Laban gave him his daughter Rachel to be his wife.
29.29: Laban also gave his woman servant Bilhah to his daughter Rachel to be her maidservant.
29.30: Jacob also married Rachel, since he loved her. He served Laban another full seven years' work for Rachel.
29.31: Later, the Lord noticed that Leah was being neglected, so he opened her womb, but Rachel remained childless.
29.32: Leah conceived, bore a son, and named him Reuben, because she was saying, "The Lord had looked on my affliction. So now, my husband will love me."
29.33: Later, she conceived again, bore a son, and declared, "Because the Lord heard that I'm neglected, he gave me this one, too." So she named him Simeon.
29.34: Later, she conceived again and said, "This time my husband will join himself with me, because I've borne him three sons." So he named him Levi.
29.35: Then she conceived yet again, bore a son, and said, "This time I'll praise the Lord." So she named him Judah. Then she stopped bearing children.

30.1: Rachel noticed that she was not bearing children for Jacob, so out of envy of her sister Leah, she said to Jacob, "If you don't give me sons, I'm going to die!"
30.2: That made Jacob angry with Rachel, so he asked in response, "Can I take God's place, who has withheld from you fruit of the womb?"
30.3: But she responded, "Here's my handmaid Bilhah. Go have sex with her. She can bear them on my knees so I can have children through her."
30.4: So she gave Jacob her woman servant Bilhah to be his mistress, and Jacob had sex with her.
30.5: Bilhah conceived and bore a son for Jacob.
30.6: Then Rachel said, "God has judged me and has heard my voice. He gave me a son." Therefore, she named him Dan.
30.7: Rachel's servant conceived again and bore a second son for Jacob,
30.8: so Rachel said, "I've been through a mighty struggle with my sister and won." She named him Naphtali.
30.9: When Leah saw that she had stopped bearing children, she took her woman servant Zilpah and gave her to Jacob as a mistress.
30.10: Leah's servant Zilpah bore a son to Jacob,
30.11: and Leah remarked, "How fortunate!" So she named him Gad.
30.12: Later, Leah's servant Zilpah bore a second son to Jacob.
30.13: She said, "How happy I am, because women will call me happy." So she named him Asher.
30.14: Some time later, during the wheat harvest season, Reuben went out and found some mandrakes in the field and brought them back for his mother Leah. Then Rachel said to Leah, "Please give me your son's mandrakes."
30.15: In response, Leah asked her, "Wasn't it enough that you've taken away my husband -  Now you also want to take my son's mandrakes!" But Rachel replied, "Okay, let's let Jacob sleep with you tonight in exchange for your son's mandrakes."
30.16: When Jacob came from the field that evening, Leah went to meet him. Then she said, "You're having sex with me tonight. I traded my son's mandrakes for you!" So he slept with her that night.
30.17: God heard what Leah had said, so she conceived and bore a fifth son for Jacob.
30.18: Then Leah said, "God has paid me for giving my servant to my husband as a mistress." So she named him Issachar.
30.19: Later, Leah conceived again and bore a sixth son for Jacob.
30.20: Then Leah said, "God has given me a good gift. This time my husband will exalt me, because I've borne him six sons." So she named him Zebulun.
30.21: After that, Leah conceived, bore a daughter, and named her Dinah.
30.22: Then God remembered Rachel. He listened to her and opened her womb,
30.23: so she conceived, bore a son, and remarked, "God has removed my shame."
30.24: Because she had been asking, "May God add another son for me," she named him Joseph.
30.25: After Rachel had given birth to Joseph, Jacob said to Laban, "Send me off so that I can go back to my place and country.
30.26: Give me my wives and children for whom I've served you. Then I'll leave, since you're aware of my service with which I've served you."
30.27: Then Laban responded, "If I've found favor in your sight, please stay with me, because I've learned through divination that the Lord has blessed me because of you."
30.28: Name your wage," Laban added, "and I'll give it to you."
30.29: But Jacob replied to Laban, "You know how I've served you and how your cattle thrived under my care.
30.30: What you had previously was only a few head, but the herd has now multiplied, because the Lord has blessed you through my efforts. But now, when am I going to be able to provide for my own household?"
30.31: "What do I have to give you?" Laban asked. Jacob responded, "You don't have to give me anything. Just do this for me： Let me tend your flock again and watch over it.
30.32: Let me walk among your flocks today and remove every speckled or spotted sheep, every black lamb, and let me do the same with the speckled and spotted goats. These will be my wages.
30.33: In times to come, you'll be able to verify my honesty because, when you come to check what I've earned, if you find a goat that's not speckled or spotted or a sheep that's not black, then it will have been stolen by me."
30.34: "Okay," Laban replied. "We'll do it the way you've asked."
30.35: That very day, Laban removed the male goats that were striped or spotted, all the female goats that were speckled or spotted - that is, every one that had white on them - and all the black lambs and placed them into the care of his sons.
30.36: He sent them as far away from Jacob as a three day's journey could take them. Meanwhile, Jacob kept tending the rest of Laban's flock.
30.37: Jacob took branches from white poplar trees, freshly cut almond trees, and some other trees, stripped off their bark to make white streaks, and uncovered the white part inside the branches.
30.38: Then he placed the branches that he had stripped bare in all the watering troughs where the flocks came to drink. He placed the branches in front of the flock, and they went into heat as they came to drink.
30.39: When the flocks mated in front of the branches, they would bring forth offspring that were striped, speckled, or spotted.
30.40: Jacob kept the lambs separate, facing the striped and entirely black ones that belonged to Laban's flock. He set his own herd by itself and would not let them be with Laban's flock.
30.41: Whenever the more vigorous of the flock came into heat, Jacob would place the branches in the troughs in front of the flock to make them mate by the branches.
30.42: But he didn't put the branches in front of any of the feeble members of the flock. As a result, the feeble ones belonged to Laban, but the stronger ones belonged to Jacob.
30.43: Therefore the man prospered so much that he had large flocks, female and male servants, as well as camels and donkeys.

31.1: Now Jacob used to listen while Laban's sons kept on complaining, "Jacob has taken over everything our father owns! He made himself wealthy from what belongs to our father!"
31.2: Jacob also noticed that the way Laban had been looking at him wasn't as nice as it had been just three days before.
31.3: Then the Lord ordered Jacob, "Go back to your father's territory and to your relatives. I'll be with you."
31.4: Jacob sent for Rachel and Leah to come out to the field where his flock was
31.5: and informed them, "I've noticed that the way your father has been looking at us hasn't been as nice as it was just a few days ago. But my father's God has been with me.
31.6: You know I've been serving your father with all my heart.
31.7: Even so, your father has cheated me. He broke our wage agreement ten times. However, God didn't allow him to harm me.
31.8: "When Laban said, 'The speckled ones will be your wages,' then all the flock gave birth to speckled ones. Then when he said, 'The streaked ones will be your wages,' all the flock gave birth to streaked offspring.
31.9: "So God has taken away your father's livestock and has given them to me.
31.10: As it was, when it was time for the livestock to breed, I once looked up in a dream, and the male goats that were mating with the flock were producing streaked, speckled, and spotted offspring.
31.11: "Then the angel of God spoke to me in a dream, 'Jacob. "'Here I am,' I replied
31.12: "'Look around!' he said. 'Go ahead, look! All the male goats have been mating with the flock, producing offspring that are streaked, speckled, and spotted, because I've been watching everything that Laban has done to you.
31.13: I am the God of Bethel, the place where you consecrated that stone and made a vow to me. Now get up, leave this territory, and return to your native land.'"
31.14: Then Rachel and Leah asked him, "Do we have anything left of inheritance remaining in our father's house -
31.15: He's treating us like foreigners. He sold us and spent all of the money that rightfully belonged to us.
31.16: Furthermore, all of the wealth that God has stripped away from our father belongs to us now and to our children. So do everything that God tells you to do."
31.17: So Jacob got up, seated his children and wives on camels,
31.18: and drove all his livestock ahead of him, with everything that belonged to him, including the livestock that he had bought and accumulated in Paddan-Aram, intending to deliver them to his father Isaac in the land of Canaan.
31.19: Meanwhile, Laban had been out shearing his sheep. While he was away, Rachel stole her father's teraphim.
31.20: Moreover, Jacob had deceived Laban the Syrian, because he had never told him that he was intending to leave.
31.21: Jacob fled, taking everything that he owned. He got up, crossed the Jordan river, and headed toward the hill country of Gilead.
31.22: Three days later, somebody reported to Laban that Jacob had fled,
31.23: so he took his relatives with him and pursued Jacob. Laban was on the road for seven days when he finally caught up with Jacob in the hill country of Gilead.
31.24: That night, God appeared to Laban the Syrian in a dream and warned him, "Be careful what you say to Jacob, whether it's one word good or bad."
31.25: Meanwhile, Jacob had pitched his tent on the mountain, where Laban had caught up with him. Laban and his relatives encamped on that same mountain in the hill country of Gilead, too.
31.26: Then Laban asked Jacob, "What did you do -  You deceived me, carried off my daughters like you would war captives,
31.27: ran away from me secretly, and stole from me by not letting me know. Otherwise, I could have sent you off with a party and singing, accompanied by a band playing tambourines and harps.
31.28: As it is, you didn't even allow me to kiss my grandchildren and daughters goodbye! You've acted foolishly.
31.29: It's actually in my power to do some serious evil to you, but last night, the God of your father said to me, 'Be careful of what you say to Jacob whether good or evil.'
31.30: Now, you can go if you must go, because you certainly are longing to go to your father's house. But why did you steal my gods?"
31.31: "I was afraid," Jacob replied. "I thought you might separate your daughters from me.
31.32: Now as to your gods, if you find someone has them in their possession, he's a dead man. Go with our relatives, search through our belongings, and take whatever belongs to you that's with me." But Jacob didn't know that Rachel had stolen them.
31.33: So Laban entered Jacob's tent, Leah's tent, and the tents of the two maid servants, but he didn't find them. Then he left Leah's tent and entered Rachel's tent.
31.34: Meanwhile, Rachel had taken the teraphim, placed them inside the camel's saddle, and sat on them. Laban searched through the whole tent, but found nothing.
31.35: Then Rachel said to her father, "Sir, please don't be angry that I cannot stand up in your presence. It's that time of the month." So he searched for - but never did find - the teraphim.
31.36: Then Jacob got angry and started an argument with Laban. "What have I done?" he demanded. "What's my crime that you would cause you to come pursue me so violently -
31.37: Now that you've searched all my properties, what did you find that belong to your house -  Set it here before our relatives and we'll let them judge between us!
31.38: "Meanwhile, these past 20 years that I've been with you, your sheep and goats never had miscarriages, I never once ate any of the rams from your flock,
31.39: and whatever was torn by beasts, I never bothered to bring to you. Instead, I bore the losses myself. Even so, you demanded that I provide restitution for anything what was stolen, whether during the day or the night.
31.40: "As it was, I was attacked by drought during the day and by cold at night. I never got any decent rest.
31.41: I've been in your house these 20 years - serving fourteen years for your two daughters and another six years for your flocks. During all that time you changed my wages ten times.
31.42: If the God of my father - the God of Abraham, the God whom Isaac feared - had not been with me, you would have sent me away empty handed. But God saw my misery and the labor of my hands and rebuked you last night."
31.43: But Laban answered Jacob. "These women are my daughters. These children are my children. The flocks are mine. In fact, everything that you see belongs to me. But what would I do today to my daughters and the children they have borne -
31.44: Come, let's make a covenant. Just between you and me. And let it serve as a witness between you and me."
31.45: So Jacob took a stone and raised it as a pillar.
31.46: Then Jacob said to his relatives, "Go gather some stones." So they picked up stones and stacked them one on the other to form a stack. Then they had a meal together there by the stack of stones.
31.47: Laban named the place Jegar-sahadutha, but Jacob named it Galeed.
31.48: Then Laban said, "This stack will serve a witness between you and me today." That's how the place came to be named Galeed.
31.49: It was also called Mizpah, because he said, "May the Lord watch between you and me, when we are estranged from each other.
31.50: If you mistreat my daughters or if you take other wives besides them, though no one is with us, keep in mind that God stands as a witness between you and me."
31.51: "Look!" Laban added, "Here is the stack of stones and here is the pillar that I've set up between you and me.
31.52: This stack is a witness, and so is this pillar, reminding me not to cross beyond this stack of stones, and reminding you not to pass by this stack in my direction, intending to cause harm.
31.53: May Abraham's God and Nahor's god judge between us." So Jacob made an oath by his father's Fear,
31.54: offered sacrifices there on the mountain, and called on his relatives to eat some food. So they ate the food and spent the night on the mountain.
31.55: Early the next morning, Laban woke up, kissed his grandchildren and daughters, blessed them, and then left for home.

32.1: As Jacob went on his way, angels from God met him.
32.2: As he was watching them, Jacob said, "This must be God's camp," so he named that place Mahanaim.
32.3: Then Jacob sent messengers ahead of him into the land of Seir (that is, into the territory of Edom) to meet his brother Esau.
32.4: He instructed them, "This is what you are to say to my master Esau： 'Your servant Jacob said to tell you, "I've journeyed to stay with Laban and I've remained there until now.
32.5: I now have cattle, donkeys, flocks, and male and female servants. I'm sending this message to my lord, so that you'll show favor to me."'"
32.6: Later, the messengers returned to Jacob and reported, "We went to your brother Esau. He's now coming to meet you. He has 400 men with him!"
32.7: In mounting terror and distress, Jacob divided the people who were with him into two companies, doing the same with the flocks, the cattle, and the camels.
32.8: Jacob was thinking, "If Esau comes to one company and attacks it, then the remaining company may escape."
32.9: Then Jacob prayed, "O God of my father Abraham, O God of my father Isaac, O Lord, you who said to me, 'Return to your country and to your relatives and I'll cause things to go well for you.'
32.10: I'm unworthy of all your gracious love, your faithfulness, and everything that you've done for your servant. When I first crossed over this Jordan River, I had only my staff. But now I've become two companies.
32.11: Deliver me from my brother's control, from Esau, because I'm terrified of him, I fear that he's coming to attack me, the mothers, and their children.
32.12: Now, you promised me that 'I'm certainly going to cause things to go well with you, and I'm going to make your offspring like the sand of the sea, which cannot be numbered.'"
32.13: Jacob spent the night there. Out of everything that he had brought with him, he chose a gift for his brother Esau -
32.14: 200 female goats, 20 male goats, 200 ewes, 20 rams,
32.15: 30 milking camels with their young, 40 cows with ten bulls, and 20 female donkeys with ten male donkeys.
32.16: He entrusted them into the care of his servants, one herd at a time. Then he said to his servants, "Go in front of me, making sure there's set a space between herds."13
32.17: To the first group he said, "When you meet my brother Esau, if he asks, 'To whom do you belong -  Where are you going -  And to whom do these herds belong?'
32.18: then you are to reply, "To your servant Jacob. They are a gift. He's sending them to my master, Esau. Look! There he is, coming along behind us."
32.19: He issued similar instructions to the second and third group, as well as to all the others who drove the herds who followed： "This is how you are to speak to Esau when you find him.
32.20: You are to tell him, 'Look! Your servant Jacob is coming along behind us.'" Jacob was thinking, "I'll pacify him with the presents that are being sent ahead of me. Then, when I meet him, perhaps he'll accept me.'"
32.21: So the presents went ahead of him, while he spent that night in the camp.
32.22: Later that night, he woke up, quickly took his two wives, two women servants, and his eleven children, and forded the river at Jabbok.
32.23: He took them across the river, along with all his possessions.
32.24: And so Jacob was left alone, and he struggled with a man with him as daybreak approached.
32.25: When the man realized that he hadn't yet won the struggle, he injured the socket of Jacob's thigh, dislocating it as he wrestled with him,
32.26: and said, "Let me go, because the dawn has come." "I won't let you go," he replied, "unless you bless me."
32.27: Then he asked him, "What's your name?" "Jacob," he responded
32.28: But in response, he said, "Your name is not to be called Jacob anymore, but Israel, because you exerted yourself against both God and men, and you've emerged victorious."
32.29: "Please," Jacob inquired. "Tell me your name." But in reply he asked, "For what purpose are you asking about my name?" And he blessed Jacob there.
32.30: Jacob would later call that place Peniel, because "I saw God face to face, but my life was spared."
32.31: The sun was rising above him as he crossed over from Peniel, limping due to his wounded thigh.
32.32: Therefore, to this day the Israelites do not eat the hip tendon that connects to the thigh socket, because he had injured the socket of the thigh where the tendon connected to Jacob's hip.

33.1: When Jacob looked off in the distance, there was Esau! And he was coming toward him, accompanied by 400 men! So Jacob divided the children of Leah, Rachel, and the children of the two servants into separate groups.
33.2: Then he positioned the women servants and their children first, then Leah and her children next, and then Rachel and Joseph after them.
33.3: Then he went out to meet Esau, passing in front of all of them, and prostrated himself to the ground seven times as he approached his brother.
33.4: Esau ran to meet Jacob and embraced him. Then he fell on his neck and kissed him. And they wept.
33.5: When Esau eventually looked around, he saw the women and the children. So he asked, "Who are these people with you?" "The children, whom God has graciously given your servant," he answered.
33.6: Then the women servants approached, accompanied by their children, and bowed low.
33.7: Leah also approached, ands he and her children bowed low. Afterthis, Joseph and Rachel approached and bowed low.
33.8: Then Esau asked, "What are all these livestock for?" "To solicit favor from your eyes, my lord," Jacob answered.
33.9: But Esau replied, "I already have so much, my brother, so keep what belongs to you."
33.10: "Please," Jacob implored him, "don't refuse. If I'm to receive favor from you, then receive this gift from me, because seeing your face is like seeing the face of God, since you have favorably accepted me.
33.11: So receive my blessing, which has been sent to you, since God has been gracious to me. Besides, I have enough."Because Jacob kept pressing him, Esau received the gifts.
33.12: Then Esau suggested, "Let's set out and travel together, but let me go in front of you.
33.13: "My lord knows that the children are tender," Jacob added, "and the ewe and the cows with me are nursing their young. If they're driven even for a day, the entire flock will die.
33.14: So let my lord go ahead of his servant while I travel gently, letting the herds set their own pace with the children until I arrive to see my lord in Seir." 33
33.15: Esau said, "Let me leave with you some of the people who are with me." "Why do that?" Jacob asked. "I've already found favor in your sight, my lord."
33.16: So Esau set out that very day back on his way to Seir,
33.17: but Jacob set out for Succoth, built a house there, and constructed some booths for his cattle. He named the place Succoth.
33.18: After he had arrived safely from Paddan-Aram, Jacob entered the city of Shechem, which was located in the territory of Canaan, and encamped facing that city.
33.19: Then he bought a parcel of land for 100 pieces of silver from the descendants of Hamor, She chem's father. He pitched his tent there,
33.20: set up an altar, and named it El-Elohe-Israel. 33

34.1: 7But if you won't listen to us, then we're going to take our daughter and leave."1What they said pleased Hamor and his son Shechem,
34.2: When Hamor the Hivite's son Shechem, the regional leader, saw her, he grabbed her and raped her, humiliating her.
34.3: He was attached to Dinah, Jacob's daughter, since he loved the young woman and spoke tenderly to her.
34.4: Then he told his father Hamor, "Get for me this young woman for my wife."
34.5: Because Jacob learned that Shechem had dishonored his daughter Dinah while his sons were still out with their cattle on the open range, he remained silent until they returned.
34.6: Meanwhile, She chem's father Hamor arrived to talk to Jacob.
34.7: Just then Jacob's sons arrived from the field. When they heard what had happened, they were distraught with grief and livid with anger toward the man, because he has committed a disgraceful deed in Israel by forcing Jacob's daughter to have sex, an act that never should have happened.
34.8: But Hamor said this： "My son is deeply attached to your daughter. Please give her to him as his wife.
34.9: Intermarry with us. Give your daughters to us and take our sons for yourselves.
34.19: so the young man did not delay the matter any further, since he was delighted with Jacob's daughter. Moreover, he was the most important person in his father's house hold.
34.20: So Hamor and his son Shechem entered the gate of their city and addressed the men of their city.
34.21: "These men are a tpeace with us," they announced. "Therefore, let them live in the land and trade in it. Look! The land is large enough for them. Let's take their daughters as wives for ourselves and let's give our sons to them.
34.22: "However," they added, "only on this condition will the men consent to live with us and be united as a single people with us： every male among us will have to be circumcised just as they are.
34.23: Shouldn't all their cattle, acquisitions, and animals belong to us -  So, let's give our consent to them, and then they'll live with us."
34.24: All of the males who heard Hamor and his son Shechem, who had gone out to the city gate, were circumcised.
34.25: Three days later, while they were still in pain, Jacob's sons Simeon and Levi, two of Dinah's brothers, each grabbed a sword and entered the city unannounced, intending to kill all the males.
34.26: They killed Hamor and his son Shechem with theirs words, took back Dinah from Shechem's house, and left.
34.27: Jacob's other sons came along afterward and plundered the city where their sister had been defiled,
34.28: seizing all o ftheir flocks, herds, asses, and whatever else was in the city or had been left ou tin the field.
34.29: They carried off all their wealth, their children, and their wives as captives, plundering everything that remained in the houses.
34.30: Then Jacob said to Simeon and Levi, "You have certainly stirred up trouble for me! You've made me stink in the opinion of the Canaanites and the Perrizites who live in this territory. Because I have only a few men with me, they're going to gather themselves together and attack me until I am totally destroyed, along with my entire household!"
34.31: "Should he have treated our sister like a whore?" they asked in response.

35.1: Later, God told Jacob, "Get up, move to Bethel, and live there. Build an altar to the God who appeared to you when you were fleeing from your brother Esau."
35.2: Jacob announced to his household and to everyone with him, "Throw away the foreign gods that you've kept among you, purify yourselves, and change your clothes.
35.3: Then let's get up and go to Bethel, where I'll build an altar to the God who answered me when I was in distress and who was with me on the road, wherever I went."
35.4: So they handed over to Jacob all their foreign gods on which they had been depending, along with the rings that they were wearing on their ears. Jacob buried them under the oak that grew near Shechem.
35.5: As they set out on their journey, because the cities around them feared God, they did not pursue Jacob's sons.
35.6: Eventually, Jacob and everyone with him arrived at Luz (also called Beth-el) in the territory of Canaan.
35.7: He built an altar there to God and named the place ElBeth-el, because God had revealed himself there when he was fleeing from his brother.
35.8: Rebekah's nurse Deborah died and was buried there, under the oak tree that was below Beth-el. That's why the place was named "Weeping Oak".
35.9: Later, God appeared to Jacob after he had arrived from Paddan-Aram and blessed him.
35.10: Then God said to him, "Your name is Jacob. No longer are you to be called Jacob. Instead, your name will be Israel." So God called his name Israel
35.11: and also said to him, "I am God Almighty. You are to be fruitful and multiply. You will become a nation - in fact, an assembly of nations! Kings will come from you - they'll emerge from your own loins!
35.12: Now as for the land that I gave to Abraham and Isaac, I'm giving it to you and to your descendants after you I'm giving the land to you!"
35.13: After this, God ascended from the place where he had been speaking to him.
35.14: Jacob erected a pillar of stone at that very place where God had spoken to him. He poured a drink offering over it, anointed it with oil,
35.15: and named the place where God had spoken to him Beth-el.
35.16: Later, they set out from Beth-el. While still a long way from Ephrathah, Rachel started to have trouble giving birth.
35.17: While she was suffering due to her difficult labor, the midwife told her, "Don't fear! You're going to have another son."
35.18: As her soul was beginning to leave as she was dying, she called his name Ben-oni, but his father named him Benjamin.
35.19: So Rachel died and was buried on the way to Ephrathah, also known as Bethlehem.
35.20: Jacob erected a pillar over her grave, and that pillar over Rachel's grave is there to this day.
35.21: Jacob continued his travels, and eventually pitched his tent facing Migdal Eder.
35.22: But while Israel lived in that land, Reuben went in and had sexual relations with his father's concubine Bilhah, and Israel heard about it. Now Jacob had twelve sons.
35.23: Leah's sons were Reuben (Jacob's first-born), Simeon, Levi, Judah, Issachar, and Zebulun.
35.24: Rachel's sons were Joseph and Benjamin.
35.25: Rachel's servant Bilhah's sons were Dan and Naphtali.
35.26: Leah's servant Zilpah's sons were Gad and Asher. These were Jacob's sons who were born to him while he lived in Paddan-Aram.
35.27: So Jacob reached his father Isaac at Mamre, in Kiriath-arba (also known as Hebron), where Abraham and Isaac had lived.
35.28: Isaac had lived a total of 180 years
35.29: when he died and was gathered to his ancestors at a ripe old age. His sons Esau and Jacob buried him.

36.1: This is a record of Esau's genealogy, that is, of Edom.
36.2: Esau had married Canaanite women, including： Elon the Hittite's daughter Adah, Oholibamah the daughter of Anah, who was Zibeon the Hivite's daughter, and
36.3: Ishamael's daughter Basemath, who was Nebaioth's sister.
36.4: Adah bore Eliphaz to Esau, Basemath bore Reuel, and
36.5: Oholibamah bore Jeush, Jalam, and Korah. These were Esau's sons, who were born to him in the territory of Canaan.
36.6: Later, Esau took his wives, his children, everyone in his household, his livestock, all his animals, and all his possessions that he had acquired in the territory of Canaan and moved far away from his brother Jacob,
36.7: because their holdings were too vast to allow them to stay together, since the land where they had settled was not able to support all of their livestock.
36.8: So Esau lived in Mount Seir. That same Esau is Edom.
36.9: This is a record of the family history of Esau, the ancestor of the Edomites of Mount Seir.
36.10: The names of Esau's sons were Eliphaz(the son of Esau's wife Adah) and Reuel(the son of Esau's wife Basemath).
36.11: Eliphaz's sons were Teman, Omar, Zepho, Gatam, and Kenaz.
36.12: Timnah was aconcubine of Esau's son Eliphaz. She bore Amalek to Eliphaz.
36.13: Reuel's sons were Nahath, Zerah, Shammah, and Mizzah. These were the sons of Esau's wife Basemath.
36.14: These were the sons of Esau's wife Oholibamah, the daughter of Anah, who was the daughter of Zibeon. She bore Jeush, Jalam, and Korah for Esau.
36.15: These were the tribal leaders of Esau's descendants, the sons of Eliphaz, who was Esau's firstborn： tribal leaders Teman, Omar, Zepho, Kenaz,
36.16: Korah, Gatam, and Amalek. These were the tribal leaders who descended from Eliphaz in the territory of Edom. These were Adah's sons.
36.17: These were the descendants of Esau's son Reuel： tribal leaders Nahath, Zerah, Shammah, and Mizzah. These were the tribal leaders who descended from Reuel in the territory of Edom. These were the sons of Esau's wife Basemath.
36.18: These were the descendants of Esau's wife Oholibamah： tribal leaders Jeush, Jalam, and Korah. These were the tribal leaders who descended from Oholibamah, the daughter of Esau's wife Anah.
36.19: These were the descendants of Esau(also known as Edom) and their tribal leaders.
36.20: These were the descendants of Seir the Horite, who lived in the territory： Lotan, Shobal, Zibeon, Anah,
36.21: Dishon, Ezer, and Dishan. These were the tribal leaders who descended from the Horites, the descendants of Seir in the territory of Edom.
36.22: Lotan's children were Hori and Hemam. Lotan's sister was Timna.
36.23: Shobal's children were Alvan, Manahath, Ebal, Shepho, and Onam.
36.24: Zibeon's children were Aiah and Anah. Anah discovered the hot springs in the wilderness while grazing his father Zibeon's donkeys.
36.25: Anah's children were Dishon and her daughter Oholibamah.
36.26: Dishon's children were Hemdan, Eshban, Ithran, and Keran.
36.27: Ezer's children were Bilhan, Zaavan, and Akan.
36.28: Dishan's children were Uz and Aran.
36.29: These were the tribal leaders who descended from the Horites： tribal leaders Lotan, Shobal, Zibeon, Anah,
36.30: Dishon, Ezer, and Dishan. These were the tribal leaders who descended from the Horites, according to their tribal leader sin the territory of Seir.
36.31: This is a list of the kings who ruled the territory of Edom before any king reigned over the Israelites.
36.32: Beor's son Bela ruled over Edom. His city's name was Dinhabah.
36.33: After Bela died, Zerah's son Jobab from Bozrah ruled in his place.
36.34: After Jobab died, Husham from the territory of the Temanites ruled in his place.
36.35: After Husham died, Bedad's son Hadad, who killed Midian in the field of Moab, ruled in his place. His city's name was Avith.
36.36: After Hadad died Samlah from Masrekah ruled in his place.
36.37: After Samlah died, Shaul from Rehoboth by the River ruled in his place.
36.38: After Shaul died, Achbor's son Baal-hanan ruled in his place.
36.39: After Achbor's son Baal-hanan died, Hadar ruled in his place. His city's name was Pau. And his wife's name was Mehetabel, who was the daughter of Matred, and granddaughter of Me-zahab.
36.40: These were the names of the chiefs who descended from Esau according to their clans, territories, and names： tribal leaders Timna, Alvah, Jetheth,
36.43: Magdiel, and Iram. These were the chiefs who descended from Edom, according to their territories in their own land. This was Esau's dynasty, who was the ancestor of the Edomites.

37.1: 7Then the man responded, "They've already left. I heard them saying that they were headed to Dothan." So Joseph followed his brothers to Dothan and found them there.
37.2: This is a record regarding Jacob's generation. At the age of seventeen years, Joseph was helping his brothers tend the flocks. He was a young man at that time, as were the children of Bilhah and Zilpah his father's wives. But Joseph brought evil reports concerning them to their father.
37.3: Now Israel loved Joseph more than all his brothers, since he was born to him in his old age, so he had made a richly-embroidered tunic for him.
37.4: When his brothers realized that their father loved him more than all of his brothers, they hated him so much that they were un able to speak politely to him.
37.5: Right about this time, Joseph had a dream and then told it to his brothers. As a result, his brothers hated him all the more!
37.6: "Listen to this dream that I dreamed!, he said.
37.7: "We were tying sheaves together out in the middle of the fields, when all of a sudden, my sheaf stood up erect! And then your sheaves gathered around it and bowed down to my sheaf!"
37.8: At this, his brothers replied, "Do you really think you're going to rule us or lord it over us?" So they hated him all the more because of his dreams and his interpretations.
37.9: But then he had one more dream that he also related to his brothers. "I had another dream," he said. "The sun, moon, and eleven of the stars were bowing down before me!"
37.18: Now as soon as they saw him approaching from a distance, before he arrived they plotted together to kill him.
37.20: Come on! Let's kill him and throw him in one of the cisterns. Then we'll report that some wild animal devoured him and wait to see what becomes of his dreams!
37.21: When Reuben heard it, he tried to save him from their plot. "Let's not do any killing,"
37.22: Reuben said to them. "And no blood shedding, either. Instead, let's toss him into this cistern that's way out here in the wilderness. But don't lay a hand on him…" Reuben intended to deliver Joseph from their control and return him to his father.
37.23: As it was, when Joseph arrived where his brothers were, they stripped off the tunic that Joseph had given him, that is, the richly-embroidered tunic that he was wearing.
37.24: Then they seized him and tossed him into the cistern. But the cistern was empty. There was no water in it.
37.25: After this, while they were seated, eating their food, they looked around and saw a caravan of Ishmaelites coming from Gilead with camels carrying spices, balm and myrrh for sale down in Egypt.
37.26: Then Judah suggested to his brothers, "Where's the profit in just killing our brother and spilling his blood -
37.27: Come on! Let's sell him to the Ishmaelites! That way, we won't have laid our hand son him. After all, he's our brother, our own flesh." So his brothers listened to him.
37.28: As the Midianite merchants were passing through, they extracted Joseph from the cistern and sold Joseph for 20 pieces of silver to the Ishmaelites, who then took Joseph down to Egypt.
37.29: Later, when Reuben returned to the cistern, Joseph wasn't in the cistern! In mounting panic, he tore his clothes,
37.30: returned to his brothers, and shouted, "He's not there! What now -  Where am I to go?"
37.31: So they took Joseph's coat, slaughtered a young goat, and dipped the coat in the blood.
37.32: Thentheystretchedouttherichly-embroideredtunic to dry, and brought it to their father. "We've found this," they reported. "Look now and see if this is or isn't your son's tunic."
37.33: Examining it, he cried out, "It's my son's tunic! A wild animal has no doubt torn Joseph to pieces."
37.34: So Jacob tore his clothes, dressed himself in sackcloth, and then mourned many days for his son.
37.35: Allhissonsanddaughtersshowed up to comfort him, but he refused to be comforted. He kept saying, "Leave me alone! I'll go down to the next world, still mourning for my son." So his father wept for him.
37.36: Meanwhile, down in Egypt, the Midianites sold Joseph to Potiphar, one of Pharaoh's court officials who was also commander-in-chief of the imperial guards.

38.1: Right about then, Judah left his brothers and went to live with an Adullamite man named Hirah.
38.2: There Judah met the daughter of a Canaanite man named Shua. He married her, had sexual relations with her,
38.3: and she conceived, bore a son, and named him Er.
38.4: Later, she conceived again, bore a son, and named him Onan.
38.5: Then she bore yet another son and named him Shelah. Judah was living in Kezib when she bore him.
38.6: Judah found a wife for his oldest son Er. Her name was Tamar.
38.7: But Er, Judah's oldest son was wicked in the sight of the Lord, so the Lord put him to death.
38.8: So Judah instructed Onan, "You are to have sexual relations with your dead brother's wife, performing the duty of a brother-in-law with her, and raise up offspring for your brother."
38.9: But Onan knew that the offspring wouldn't be his own heir, so whenever he had sexual relations with his brother's wife, he would spill his semen to the ground to avoid generating offspring for his brother.
38.10: What he did was evil in the sight of the Lord, so the Lord slew him, too.
38.11: After this, Judah told his daughter-in-law Tamar, "Come and live as a widow in your father's house until my son Shelah grows up." But he was really thinking, "…otherwise, he might die like his brothers." So Tamar left and lived in her father's house.
38.12: Some years later, Shua's daughter, that is, Judah's wife, died. As Judah was grieving, he visited the shearers of his flock in Timnah, accompanied by his Adullamite friend Hirah.
38.13: "Look!" somebody reported to Tamar, "Your father-in-law is going to Timnah to shear his sheep."
38.14: So she took off her mourning apparel, covered herself with a shawl, and concealed her outward appearance. Then she went out and sat at the entrance of Enaim, which is on the way to Timnah, because she knew that even though Shelah had grown up, she wasn't being given to him as his wife.13
38.15: When Judah saw her, he thought she was a prostitute, since she had concealed her face.
38.16: So on the way, he turned aside, approached her, and said,"Come on! Let's have some sex!" But he didn't realize that she was talking to his own daughter-in-law. "What will you give me," she asked, "in order to have sex with me?"
38.17: "I'll send you a young goat from the flock," he responded. But she pressed him, asking, "What security will you put up until you've sent it?"
38.18: Then he asked, "What pledge do you want me to give you?" "Your signet ring, cord, and the staff in your hand," she countered. So he gave them to her, had sex with her, and she became pregnant by him.
38.19: Then she got up and left. Later, she took off her shawl and put on her mourning clothes.
38.20: Later on, Judah sent his Adullamite friend to take her a young goat, intending to retrieve what he had put up as security from the woman, but he could not find her.
38.21: He asked the men who lived in that area, "Where's that temple prostitute who was sitting alongside the road at Enaim?" But they replied, "There's been no temple prostitute here."
38.22: So he returned to Judah and said, "I haven't found her. Also, the men who are from there said, 'There's been no prostitute here.'"
38.23: Then Judah said, "Let her have them. Otherwise, we'll become contemptible. I sent this young goat, but you didn't find her."
38.24: Three months later, it was reported to Judah, "Your daughter-in-law Tamar has been sleeping around! And look! She's pregnant on account of her prostitution." "Bring her out," Judah responded. "Let's burn her to death!"
38.25: While they were bringing her out, she sent this message to her father-in-law：  "I am pregnant by the man to whom these belong. Furthermore," she added, "tell me to whom this signet ring, cord, and staff belongs."
38.26: When Judah recognized them, he admitted, "She is more upright than I, because I never did give her my son Shelah." And he never had sex with her again.
38.27: Later, when it was time for her to give birth, there were twins in her womb!
38.28: While she was giving birth, one of them put out his hand, so the midwife grabbed it and tied something scarlet around his hand saying, "This one came out first."
38.29: As it was, he withdrew his hand, and then his brother was born. Amazed, she cried out loud, "What's this -  A breach birth?" So he was named Perez.
38.30: Afterwards, his brother came out, and around his hand was the scarlet. So they called him Zerah.

39.1: Meanwhile, Joseph had been delivered to Egypt and turned over to Potiphar, one of Pharaoh's court officials and the commander-in-chief of the imperial guards. An Egyptian, he bought Joseph from the Ishmaelites, who had brought him down there.
39.2: But the Lord was with Joseph. He became a very prosperous man while in the house of his Egyptian master.
39.3: His master could see that the Lord was with him, because the Lord made everything that he did prosper.
39.4: That's how Joseph found favor in his sight as he ministered to him. Eventually, Potiphar appointed Joseph overseer of his entire household. Moreover, he entrusted everything that he owned into his responsibility.
39.5: From the time he appointed Joseph to be overseer over his entire household and everything that he owned, the Lord blessed the household of the Egyptian because of Joseph. The blessing of the Lord rested on him, whether in Potiphar's household or in Potiphar's fields.
39.6: Everything that he owned, he entrusted into Joseph's care. He never worried about anything, except for the food he ate. Now Joseph was well built and handsome in appearance.
39.7: That's why, some time later, Joseph's master's wife looked straight at Joseph and propositioned him： "Come on! Let's have a little sex!"
39.8: But he refused. He replied to his master's wife, "Look! My master doesn't have to worry about anything in the house with me in charge, and he has entrusted everything into my charge.
39.9: No one has more authority in this house than I do. He has withheld nothing from me, except you, and that's because you're his wife. So how can I do this great evil -  How can I sin against God -
39.10: She kept on talking to him like this day after day, but he wouldn't listen to her. Not only would he refuse to have sex with her, he refused even to stay around her.
39.11: One day, though, he went into the house to do his work. None of the household servants were inside,
39.12: so she grabbed him by his outer garment and demanded "Let's have some sex!" Instead, Joseph ran outside, leaving his outer garment still in her hand.
39.13: When she realized that he had left his outer garment right there in her hand, she ran outside
39.14: and yelled for her household servants. "Look!" she cried out. "He has brought in a Hebrew man to humiliate us. He came in to have sex with me, but I screamed out loud!
39.15: When he heard that I was raising my voice to scream, he left his outer garment with me and fled outside."1439
39.16: She kept his outer garment by her side until Joseph's master came home,
39.17: and then she told him these words： "That Hebrew slave whom you brought to us came in here to rape me.
39.18: But when I raised my voice and screamed, he left his outer garment with me and ran outside."
39.19: When Joseph's master heard that his wife was saying words to the effect that "This is how your servant treated me," he flew into a rage,
39.20: arrested Joseph, and locked him up in the same prison where the king's prisoners were confined. So Joseph remained there in prison.
39.21: But the Lord was with Joseph. He extended gracious love to him, granting him favor with the prison warden.
39.22: So the prison warden entrusted into Joseph's care all the prisoners who were confined in prison. Whatever they did, Joseph was in charge of the work detail.
39.23: The prison warden did not have to worry about anything under Joseph's care, because the Lord was with him. That's why Joseph prospered in everything he did.

40.1: Some time after these things, both the senior security advisor to the king of Egypt and his head chef offended their master, Egypt's king.
40.2: Pharaoh was so angry with his two officers - his senior security advisor and his head chef -
40.3: tha the locked them up in the prison dungeon operated by the captain of the guard, the very place where Joseph was imprisoned.
40.4: The captain of the guard entrusted them to Joseph's custody, who ministered to them, since they were to remain there in custody for a number of days.
40.5: Then the two of them each had a dream. They both had their dreams the same night and they both had their own unique interpretations for their dreams - the senior security advisor and the head chef to the king of Egypt, who had confined them in prison.
40.6: When Joseph came to see them in the morning, he noticed how downcast they looked! They were both very sad.
40.7: So he asked the officers of Pharaoh, who were with him in prison in his master's house, "Why are you so sad today?"
40.8: They responded to him, "We had a dream, but there's no one to interpret it." Then Joseph said to them, "Interpretations belong to God. Please tell me your stories."
40.9: So the senior security advisor related his dream to Joseph. "In my dream," he said, "all of a sudden there was a vine in front of me!
40.10: On the vine were three branches. It budded, blossoms shot out, and clusters grew up and produced ripe grapes.
40.11: Then, with Pharaoh's cup in my hand, I took the grapes, squeezed them into Pharaoh's cup, then handed the cup directly to Pharaoh."
40.12: Then Joseph said to him, "This is its interpretation. The three branches are three days.
40.13: Withinthreedays,Pharaohwillencourageyou and return you to your responsibilities. You'll attend to Pharaoh's personal wine cup, just as you did when you were his senior security advisor.
40.14: But keep me in mind when things go well for you. Be sure to extend kindness to me by remembering me to Pharaoh. Bring me out of this prison,
40.15: because I was kidnapped from the land of the Hebrews. Not only that, I haven't done anything that deserves me being confined to this pit."
40.16: When the head chef heard that the interpretation was good, he said to Joseph, "I was also in my dream. All of a sudden, there were three baskets with white bread stacked on top of my head.
40.17: On th e basket that was uppermost, there were all kinds of food, including baked food for Pharaoh. The birds were eating them from the basket on my head.
40.18: Joseph replied, "This is its interpretation： The three baskets are also three days.
40.19: Within three more days, Pharaoh will lift your head from you and hang you on gallows, where birds will eat your flesh from you."
40.20: On the third day, which just happened to be Pharaoh's birthday, he threw a party for all his servants. He lifted the head of his senior security advisor and of his head chef in front of his servants -
40.21: he restored his senior security advisor to his former responsibilities, including attending to Pharaoh's personal wine cup,
40.22: but he hanged the head chef, just as Joseph had interpreted for them.
40.23: Despite all of this, the senior security advisor not only didn't remember Joseph, he deliberately forgot him.

41.1: Two years later - to the day - Pharaoh dreamed that he was standing by the Nile River,
41.2: when all of a sudden seven healthy, plump cows emerged from the Nile to graze in the grass that grew in the reeds that lined the bank.
41.3: Right after that, seven more cows came up out of the Nile. Ugly and gaunt, they stood next to the other cows on the bank of the Nile River.
41.4: But all of a sudden they ate up the seven healthy, plump cows! Then Pharaoh woke up.3
41.5: After he had fallen back to sleep, he had a second dream, in which seven ears of plump, fruit-filled grain grew up on a single stalk.
41.6: Suddenly seven thin ears of grain that had been scorched by an east wind sprouted up right after them
41.7: and ate up the seven plump, fruit-filled ears. Then Pharaoh woke up a second time, and it had been a very vivid dream!
41.8: The very next morning, he was frustrated about the dream, so he sent word to summon all the magicians and wise men of Egypt. Pharaoh told them what he had dreamed, but no one could interpret the dreams for Pharaoh.
41.9: Then Pharaoh's senior security advisor spoke up. "Maybe I should make a confession.
41.10: When Pharaoh was angry with some of his servants, he incarcerated me in custody of the captain of the bodyguard, along with Pharaoh's senior chef.
41.11: We each had a dream on the same night, and each dream had its own meaning.
41.12: There was a Hebrew young man incarcerated with us, who was also serving as a servant to the captain of the bodyguard. "We each related our dreams, and then he interpreted them for us. He provided specific meanings for each of our dreams.
41.13: And what he interpreted for each of us came true! Pharaoh restored me to my responsibilities, but he executed the other man.
41.14: Pharoah sent word to summon Joseph quickly from the dungeon, so they shaved his beard, changed his clothes, and then sent him straight to Pharaoh.
41.15: "I've had a dream," Pharaoh told Joseph, "but nobody can interpret it. I've heard that you can interpret dreams."
41.16: "I cannot do that," Joseph replied, "but God is concerned about Pharaoh's well-being."
41.17: So Pharaoh told Joseph, "In my dream, I was standing on the bank of the Nile River,
41.18: and all of a sudden seven healthy, plump, beautiful cows emerged from the Nile and began to graze among the reeds that line the bank.
41.19: Just then, seven other cows emerged after them. Poor, ugly, and appearing very gaunt in their flesh, I've never seen anything as ugly as those cows throughout the entire land of Egypt!
41.20: But those thin, gaunt cows gobbled up the first seven healthy cows!
41.21: "Not only that," Pharaoh continued, "after they had finished devouring the cows, nobody could tell that they had gobbled them up, because they were just as ugly as before. Then I woke up.
41.22: Later, I also dreamed about seven plump, fruit-filled ears of grain that grew up out of a single stalk.
41.23: All of a sudden, seven thin, withered ears of grain, scorched by the east wind, sprouted up after them.
41.24: But the thin ears gobbled up the seven good ears. I told all this to my advisors, but nobody was able to explain it to me."
41.25: "Pharaoh's dreams are identical," Joseph replied. "God has told Pharaoh what he is getting ready to do.
41.26: The seven healthy cows represent seven years, as do the seven healthy ears. The dreams are identical.
41.27: The seven gaunt cows that arose after the healthy cows are seven years, as are the seven gaunt ears scorched by the east wind. There will be seven years of famine.
41.28: So the message that I have for Pharaoh is that God is telling Pharaoh what he is getting ready to do.
41.29: Be advised that seven years of phenomenal abundance are coming throughout all the land of Egypt, 01Gn 41:30 but after them seven years of famine are ahead, during which all of the abundance will be forgotten throughout the land of Egypt. The famine will ravage the land so severely that
41.31: surplus will be unknown in the land due to the coming famine, because it will be very severe.
41.32: "Now about the repeating of that dream twice for the benefit of Pharaoh, this event has been scheduled by God, and God will bring it to pass very soon.
41.33: Therefore let the Pharaoh select a wise, discerning person to place in charge over the land of Egypt.
41.34: Also, let the Pharaoh immediately proceed to appoint supervisors over the land of Egypt, who will collect one fifth of its agricultural production during the coming seven years of abundance.
41.35: "Let them collect all the food during the coming fruitful years, store up the grain in cities governed by the Pharaoh's authority, and place it under guard.
41.36: Let the food be kept in reserve to feed the land for the seven years of famine that will occur throughout Egypt, so the land won't perish during the famine."35
41.37: What Joseph proposed pleased Pharaoh and all of his advisors,
41.38: so Pharaoh asked his servants, "Can we find anyone else like this - someone in whom the Spirit of God lives -
41.39: Since God has revealed all of this to you," Pharaoh said to Joseph, "there is no one so wise and discerning as you.
41.40: So you are to be appointed in charge over my palace, and all of my people are to do whatever you command them to do. Only the throne will have greater authority than you."39
41.41: "Look!" Pharaoh confirmed to Joseph, "I've put you in charge of the entire land of Egypt!"
41.42: Then Pharaoh removed his signet ring from his hand, placed it on Joseph's hand, had him clothed in fine linen garments, and placed a gold chain around his neck.
41.43: Then he provided him with a chariot for his second-in-command, outfitted with a group of people who shouted out in front of him, "Bow your knees!" And that's how Pharaoh set Joseph over the entire land of Egypt.
