41.44: Furthermore, Pharaoh said to Joseph, "I'm still Pharaoh, but without your permission nobody in all of the land of Egypt will so much as lift up their hands or take a step!"
41.45: Pharaoh also changed Joseph's name to Zaphenath-paneah and gave Asenath, daughter of Potiphera, the priest of On, as his wife. And so Joseph gained authority throughout the land of Egypt.
41.46: Joseph was 30 years old when he began to serve Pharaoh, king of Egypt, by traveling throughout the land of Egypt, away from the oversight of Pharaoh.
41.47: While the earth was producing bumper crops during the seven abundant years,
41.48: he collected the surplus food during the seven years of plenty throughout the land of Egypt, storing food in cities; that is, he gathered the food from fields that surrounded every city and stored it there.
41.49: Joseph stored up so much grain in so much abundance - like sand on the seashore! - that he stopped keeping records because it was proving to be impossible to measure how much they were gathering.
41.50: Before the years of famine arrived, Joseph fathered two sons by Asenath, the daughter of Potiphera, the priest of On.
41.51: Joseph named his firstborn son Manasseh because, he claimed, "God has made me forget all of my hard life and my father's house."
41.52: He named his second son Ephraim because, he claimed, "God has made me fruitful in the land of my troubles."
41.53: As soon as the seven years of abundance that pervaded the land of Egypt came to a conclusion,
41.54: the seven years of famine started, just as Joseph had predicted. It was an international famine, but there was food everywhere throughout the land of Egypt.
41.55: Eventually, the land of Egypt began to feel the effects of the famine, so they cried out to Pharaoh for food. "Go see Joseph," Pharaoh announced to all the Egyptians, "and do whatever he tells you to do."
41.56: Because the famine had spread nationwide, Joseph opened all of the storehouses and sold grain to the Egyptians, because the famine was beginning to be severe throughout the land of Egypt.
41.57: In addition, all of the surrounding nations came to Joseph to buy grain from Egypt, because the famine had become severe throughout the world.

42.1: Eventually, Jacob observed that there was grain in Egypt, so he asked his sons, "Why do you keep on staring at one another -
42.2: Pay attention now! I've heard that there is grain in Egypt, so go down there and buy some grain for us, so we can live, instead of dying."
42.3: So ten of Joseph's brothers left to buy grain from Egypt.
42.4: Jacob would not send Joseph's brother Benjamin to accompany them, because he was saying, "I'm afraid that he'll come to some kind of harm."
42.5: Israel's sons went in a caravan that included others who were going to Egypt to buy grain, because the famine pervaded the land of Canaan, too.
42.6: Meanwhile, Joseph continued to be ruler over the land, in charge of selling to everyone in the land. Joseph's brothers appeared and bowed down to him, face down.
42.7: As soon as Joseph saw his brothers, he knew who they were, but he remained disguised and asked them gruffly, "Where are you from?" "From the land of Canaan," they replied. "We're here to buy food."
42.8: But Joseph had already recognized his brothers, even though they had not recognized him.
42.9: Furthermore, Joseph remembered the dreams that he had about them. So he accused them, "You're spies! You've come here to reconnoiter our undefended territories!"
42.10: "No, your majesty," they replied. "Your servants have come here to buy food.
42.11: We're all sons of a common father. We're honest men, your majesty. We're not spies!"
42.12: But Joseph kept on persisting, "It's just as I've said - you've come here to reconnoiter our undefended territories!"
42.13: "But your majesty," they pleaded, "your servants include twelve brothers, the sons of a common father back in the land of Canaan. Please! Our youngest brother remains with our father, and the other is no longer alive."
42.14: "I'm right!" Joseph insisted. "Just as I said, you're spies!
42.15: So here's how we'll test you. You can bet the life of Pharaoh that you're not leaving here until your youngest brother comes here!
42.16: One of you is to be sent back so he can get your brother while you remain in custody. That way, we'll test whether or not you're telling the truth. If you're not, as surely as the Pharaoh lives, you're spies!"15
42.17: Then Joseph locked them all together in prison for three days.
42.18: Three days later, Joseph told them, "I fear God, so do this and you'll live.
42.19: If you're honest men, leave one of your brothers here in custody, then the rest of you can leave and transport some grain to alleviate the famine that's affecting your households.
42.20: Just be sure to bring your youngest brother back to me so what you've claimed can be verified. That way, you won't die."
42.21: "We're all guilty because of what we did to our brother!" they said to each other. "We kept on watching his suffering while he pleaded with us! We're in this mess because we wouldn't listen!"
42.22: "Didn't I tell you!" Reuben replied. "'Don't wrong the kid!' I said, but would you listen -  No! Now it's payback time!"
42.23: Meanwhile, they had no idea that Joseph could understand them, since he was talking to them through an interpreter.
42.24: He turned away from them and began to weep. When he returned, he spoke with them, but then he took Simeon away from them and had him placed under arrest right in front of them.
42.25: After this, Joseph gave orders to fill up their sacks with grain, to return each of their own money to their sacks, and to supply them each with provisions for their return journey. All of this was done for them.
42.26: Then they each mounted up, their donkeys having been loaded with grain, and left from there.
42.27: Later on, one of them opened up his sack to give his donkey some fodder after they had stopped at the place where they intended to lodge for the night. There, in the mouth of his sack, was all of his money!
42.28: He reported to his brothers, "My money has been returned! It's right here in my sack!" Trembling from their mounting consternation, they each asked one another, "What is God doing to us?"
42.29: As soon as they had returned to their father Jacob in the land of Canaan, they told him everything that had happened to them.
42.30: "The man who was in charge of the land spoke harshly to us," they said. "He accused us of being spies!
42.31: But we told him, 'No! We're honest men! We're not spies!
42.32: Our father has twelve sons, but one of us isn't alive anymore, and our youngest brother is with our father today back home in Canaan.'
42.33: "But the man who was in charge of the land responded, 'I'm going to test your honesty. Leave one of your brothers with me, take some grain for the famine that's afflicting your households, and leave.
42.34: But bring your youngest brother back to me so I can be sure that you're honest men, and not spies. Then I'll return your brother to you, and you'll be allowed to trade anywhere in the land.'"
42.35: Later on, as they went about unloading their sacks, every man's bundle of money was found in each of their sacks. When they and their father saw their bundles of money, they were greatly distressed.
42.36: Their father Jacob told them, "You're causing me to lose my children! Joseph is gone. Now Simeon is gone, and you're planning to take Benjamin, too. Everything's going against me!"
42.37: "Feel free to put my own two sons to death," Reuben responded to his father, "if I don't bring him back to you. Trust me, and I'll bring him back to you."
42.38: But Jacob replied, "My son isn't going back with you, since his brother is dead and he's the only one left. If something should harm him along the road that you're going to take, then you'll send me and my gray hair to my death in sorrow!"

43.1: Meanwhile, the famine remained severe throughout the region.
43.2: As a result, when Jacob's family was beginning to eat the last of the grain that they had brought back from Egypt, their father told his sons, "Go back to Egypt and buy us some food."
43.3: "But Judah reminded him, "The man distinctly warned us 'You'll never see my face unless your brother comes with you.'
43.4: So if you send our brother with us, we'll go down and buy some food.
43.5: But if you don't send him, we're not going, because the man told us, 'You'll never see my face unless your brother is with you.'"
43.6: Israel replied, "Why did you make all this trouble by telling the man that you have another brother?"
43.7: "The man specifically asked about us and our relatives," they responded. "He asked us, 'Is your father still alive?' and 'Do you have another brother?' So we answered his questions. How could we have known that he would tell us to bring our brother back with us?"
43.8: "Send the young man with me," Judah said to his father Israel, "and we'll get up and go so we can survive and not die - and that includes all of us, you and our families.
43.9: I'll even offer myself to guarantee that I'll be responsible for him. If I don't bring him back and present him to you, I'll personally bear the consequences forever.
43.10: After all, if we hadn't delayed, we could have been there and back twice by now!"9
43.11: "If that's the way it has to be," their father Israel replied, "then do this： take some of the best produce of the land in your containers and take them to the man as a gift - some resin ointment, some honey, fragrant resins, myrrh, pistachios, and almonds.
43.12: Also take twice as much money with you so you can return the money that had been replaced in the mouth of your sacks. Maybe it was an accounting mistake on his part.
43.13: And be sure to take your brother, too. So get up, return to the man,
43.14: and may God almighty give you compassion in the presence of the man. May he send all of you back, including your other brother and Benjamin. Now as for me, if I lose my children, I lose them."
43.15: So the men took their gift and double the money. Then they got up, took Benjamin with them, and set out for Egypt, and eventually appeared before Joseph.
43.16: As soon as Joseph noticed that Benjamin had come with them, he ordered his palace manager, "Bring the men into the palace. Slaughter an animal and prepare it, because these men will be dining with me for lunch."
43.17: So the man did what Joseph had ordered, and brought the men to Joseph's palace.
43.18: The men were terrified as they were being taken to Joseph's palace. "It's because of that money that was returned to our sacks the first time we were brought to him," they reasoned. "He's seeking an excuse to attack us, enslave us, and confiscate our donkeys!"
43.19: So they approached Joseph's palace manager and talked with him at the palace entrance.
43.20: "Your Excellency," they said, "The first time we came here to buy food,
43.21: when we arrived at our overnight lodging place, we opened our sacks and discovered each man's money was still in the mouth of his sack. All of our money was there! We've brought it back with us in full.
43.22: We've also brought along some more money to buy supplies, but we don't know who put our money back into our sacks."
43.23: "Relax," the manager said. "You can stop being afraid, now. Your God, the God of your father, has placed hidden treasure within those sacks for you. I've been paid in full." Then he brought Simeon out to them,
43.24: ushered the men into Joseph's palace, gave them water to wash their feet and fodder for their donkeys.
43.25: Then off he went to prepare the honorary meal that was to be made ready for Joseph's arrival at noon, since they had been informed that they were going to be eating there.
43.26: When Joseph arrived at his palace, they brought to him their gifts that they had carried with them and bowed to the ground in front of him.
43.27: Joseph asked them how they had been doing. "Is your father well, the older gentleman about whom you spoke?" he inquired. "Is he still alive?"
43.28: "Your servant, our father, is doing well," they replied. "He is still alive." Then they bowed down in humility.
43.29: As Joseph looked up and recognized his brother Benjamin, his own mother's son, he asked, "Is this your youngest brother about whom you spoke to me?" And he addressed him directly, "May God be gracious to you, my son."
43.30: At this, Joseph hurried out, deeply moved because of his brother, and looked for a place to weep by himself. He entered his personal quarters, wept there for a while,
43.31: then washed his face and came out. Barely controlling himself, he ordered his staff to serve the meal.
43.32: Joseph's staff served him by himself, his brothers separately, and the Egyptian staff members by themselves, because the Egyptians wouldn't take their meal with the Hebrews, since doing so was detestable for the Egyptians.
43.33: Meanwhile, the brothers were seated in front of Joseph in birth order, as befitting the firstborn's birthright and as befitting the youngest man's age. The men stared at one another in astonishment.
43.34: Joseph himself brought portions to them from his own table, except that he provided to Benjamin five times as much as he did for each of the others. So they feasted together and drank freely with him.

44.1: Later, Joseph commanded his palace manager, "Fill the men's sacks to full capacity with food and replace each man's money at the top of the sack.
44.2: Then place my cup - the silver one - in the top of the sack belonging to the youngest one, along with the money he brought to buy grain." So the manager did precisely what Joseph told him to do.1
44.3: Early the next morning, the men were sent on their way, along with their donkeys.
44.4: They had not traveled far from the city when Joseph ordered his palace manager, "Get up, follow those men, and when you've caught up with them, ask them, 'Why did you repay evil for good -
44.5: Don't you have the cup that my master uses to drink from and also uses to practice divination -  You're wrong to have done this.'"
44.6: So he went after them and made that accusation.5
44.7: "Your Excellency," they replied, "Why do you speak like this -  Far be it from your servants to act like this.
44.8: Look, we brought back to you from the land of Canaan the money that we found at the top of our sacks. How, then, could we have stolen silver or gold from your master's palace -
44.9: Go ahead and execute whichever one of your servants is discovered to have it, and we'll remain as your master's slaves."
44.10: "Agreed," he responded. "Just as you've said, the one who is found to have it in his possession will become my slave, and the rest of you will be innocent."
44.11: So they quickly dismounted, unloaded their sacks to the ground, and each man opened his own sack.
44.12: The palace manager searched for the cup, beginning with the oldest and ending with the youngest, and it was discovered in Benjamin's sack.
44.13: At this, they all tore their clothes, reloaded their donkeys, and returned to the city.
44.14: Joseph was waiting for them back at his palace when his brothers returned. They fell to the ground in front of him,
44.15: and Joseph asked them, "Why did you do this -  Don't you know that I'm an expert at practicing divination?"
44.16: "What can we say, your Excellency," Judah replied. "How can we explain this or justify ourselves -  God has discovered the sin of your servants, and now we've become slaves to you, your Excellency, both we and the one in whose possession the cup has been discovered."
44.17: "Far be it from me to do this," Joseph responded. "The man in whose possession the cup was discovered will be my slave, but the rest of you may leave in peace to be with your father."
44.18: But Judah approached him and begged him, "Your Excellency, please allow your servant to speak to you privately. Please don't be angry with your servant, since you are equal to Pharaoh.
44.19: Your Excellency asked his servants, 'Do you have a father or brother?'
44.20: and we answered your Excellency, 'We have an aged father and a younger child who was born when he was old. His brother is now dead, so he's the only survivor living from his mother. His father loves him.'
44.21: "But then you ordered your servants, 'Bring him here to me so I can see him for myself.'
44.22: "So we told your Excellency, 'The young man cannot leave his father, because if he were to do so, his father would die.'
44.23: But then you told your servants, 'Unless your youngest brother comes back with you, you won't see my face again.'
44.24: "Later on, after we had gone back to your servant, my father, we told him what your Excellency had said.
44.25: "'Go back,' our father ordered, 'and buy us a little food.'
44.26: "But we told him, 'We can't go back there. If our youngest brother accompanies us, we'll go back, but we cannot see the man's face again unless our youngest brother accompanies us.'
44.27: "Then your servant, our father, said to us, 'You know my wife bore me two sons.
44.28: One of them left me, so I concluded "I'm certain that he has been torn to pieces," and I haven't seen him since then.
44.29: If you take this one from me, too, and then something harmful happens to him, you will be sending me and my gray hair to death in sorrow.'
44.30: "So when I go back to your servant, my father, and the young man isn't with us, since he's constantly living life focused on his son,
44.31: when he notices that the young man hasn't come back with us, he'll die, and your servants really will have brought the gray hair of your servant, our father, to death in sorrow.
44.32: Furthermore, your servant pledged his own life as a guarantee of the young man's safety. I told my father, 'If I don't bring him back to you, then let me be responsible to my father forever.'
44.33: "Therefore, please allow your servant to remain as a slave to your Excellency, instead of the young man, and let the young man go back home with his brothers.
44.34: After all, how can I go back to my father if the young man doesn't accompany me -  I'm afraid of what might happen to my father."

45.1: At this point, Joseph could not control his emotions any longer, so he cried out to everyone who was standing nearby, "Everybody get away from me!" As a result, none of his staff was anywhere near him when he revealed himself to his brothers.
45.2: He cried so loudly that the Egyptians heard him, including Pharaoh's household.1
45.3: Joseph blurted out, "I'm Joseph! Is my father really alive?" But his brothers could not answer him, because they had become terrified to be in his presence.
45.4: Joseph implored his brothers, "Please come close to me." So they did. "I'm your brother Joseph," he told them, "whom you sold into slavery in Egypt.
45.5: But don't be distressed or angry at yourselves by selling me here, because God sent me ahead of you all in order to deliver us.
45.6: That's because this famine has been going on for two years now in this region, and there are still five years left, during which there won't be any plowing or harvesting.5
45.7: "God sent me ahead of you to preserve you on the earth, and to save you all through a magnificent deliverance.
45.8: As a result, it wasn't you who sent me here, but God himself! He established me as a father-figure to Pharaoh himself! I'm in charge of his entire palace and ruler over the entire land of Egypt.
45.9: So hurry up, go back to my father, and tell him that your son Joseph tells him, 'God has made me master of all of Egypt. Come live with me, and don't delay!'
45.10: "You are to live in the land of Goshen, near where I am, including you, your children, your grandchildren, your flocks, your herds, and everything that you own.
45.11: I'll provide for you there, since there are still five years of famine left to go, and you, your households, and everything you own would have otherwise become impoverished.
45.12: "Look, now! All of you can see me! And my own brother Benjamin can tell that's my own mouth speaking to you!
45.13: So go tell my father about all of my splendor in Egypt. Tell him about everything that you've seen. Be quick about it, and bring my father down here!"
45.14: Then he collapsed on Benjamin's neck and wept while Benjamin wept on his neck.
45.15: He kissed all of his brothers and wept on them, too, and then his brothers were able to talk with him.
45.16: As soon as the news reached Pharaoh's palace that Joseph's brothers had arrived, Pharaoh and his servants were ecstatic. 17Pharaoh told Joseph, "Be sure to tell your brothers, 'Do this： load up your livestock, go back to the land of Canaan,
45.18: get your father and your households, and come back to me. I'll give you the best of the land of Egypt and you can live off the abundance of the land.'
45.19: "In addition," Pharaoh ordered, "Do this： take some transport wagons from the land of Egypt for your little ones to ride in, along with your wives, and bring your father and come!
45.20: Don't worry about your household goods, because the best of all the land of Egypt is yours."
45.21: So Israel's sons did what they were asked to do, and Joseph provided wagons for them, as Pharaoh had commanded. He also gave them provisions for the journey.
45.22: He gave each of them some changes of clothes, but he also gave Benjamin 300 pieces of silver and five changes of clothes.
45.23: He sent his father ten male donkeys loaded with the best of Egyptian goods and ten female donkeys loaded with grain, bread, and provisions for his father during the journey.
45.24: Then Joseph sent his brothers away, and they left for home. As they were leaving, Joseph admonished them, "Don't quarrel on the way back!"
45.25: So Joseph's brothers left Egypt and returned to the land of Canaan and to their father Jacob,
45.26: where they informed their father, "Joseph is still alive! As a matter of fact, he's ruling the entire land of Egypt." But Jacob didn't believe them, because his heart had grown numb.
45.27: However, as soon as they had told him everything Joseph had said, and after he saw the wagons that Joseph had sent along to carry him, their father Jacob's spirit was encouraged.
45.28: "It's enough," Israel replied. "My son Joseph is still alive. I'm going to go see him before I die!"

46.1: Later, Israel began his journey, taking along everything that he owned, and arrived at Beersheba, where he offered sacrifices to the God of his father Isaac.
46.2: God spoke to Israel through night visions, addressing him, "Jacob! Jacob!" "Here I am!" Jacob replied.
46.3: "I'm God, your father's God. Don't be afraid to move down to Egypt, because I'm going to turn you into a mighty nation there.
46.4: I'm going down with you to Egypt, and I'm certainly going to bring you back again. And Joseph will close your eyes."
46.5: So Jacob got up and left Beersheba, and Israel's sons carried their father Jacob, their little ones, and their wives in the transport wagons that Pharaoh had sent to carry them.
46.6: They took their livestock and their household property that they had acquired in the land of Canaan and traveled to Egypt. Jacob and all of his descendants went with him -
46.7: including his sons, his grandsons, his daughters, and his granddaughters - every one of his descendants accompanied him to Egypt.
46.8: Here's a list of the names of Israel's sons, that is, of Jacob and his sons who moved to Egypt： Reuben, Jacob's firstborn;
46.9: Reuben's sons Hanoch, Pallu, Hezron, and Carmi;
46.10: Simeon's sons Jemuel, Jamin, Ohad, Jachin, Zohar, and Shaul, who was the son of a Canaanite woman;
46.11: Levi's sons Gershon, Kohath, and Merari;
46.12: and Judah's sons Er, Onan, Shelah, Perez, and Zerah. (Technically, Er and Onan had died in the land of Canaan.) Perez's sons were Hezron and Hammul.
46.13: Also included were Issachar's sons Tola, Puvvah, Job, and Shimron;
46.14: along with Zebulun's sons Sered, Elon, and Jahleel.
46.15: These were all sons from Leah, whom she bore to Jacob in Paddan-Aram, along with his daughter Dinah. His sons and daughters numbered 33.12
46.16: Also included were Gad's sons Ziphion, Haggi, Shuni, Ezbon, Eri, Arodi, and Areli;
46.17: Asher's sons Imnah, Ishvah, Ishvi, Beriah, and their sister Serah. Beriah's sons Heber and Malchiel were also included.
46.18: These were all sons from Zilpah, whom Laban had given to his daughter Leah. She bore these sixteen persons to Jacob.
46.19: Jacob's wife Rachel's sons were Joseph and Benjamin.
46.20: Joseph's son born in the land of Egypt included Manasseh and Ephraim, whom Asenath, daughter of Potiphera, the priest of On, bore to him.
46.21: Benjamin's sons included Bela, Becher, Ashbel, Gera, Naaman, Ehi, Rosh, Muppim, Huppim, and Ard.
46.22: These were all the sons of Rachel, who were born to Jacob - fourteen persons in all.
46.23: Also included were Dan's son Hushim;
46.24: Naphtali's sons Jahzeel, Guni, Jezer, and Shillem.
46.25: There were sons of Bilhah, whom Laban had given to his daughter Rachel. She bore these children to Jacob - seven persons in all.
46.26: All of these people, who belonged to Jacob's family, traveled to Egypt. All of his direct descendants, not including Jacob's sons' wives, numbered 66 persons in all.
46.27: Joseph had two sons borne to him in Egypt, and all of the people who came to Egypt of Jacob's household numbered 70.
46.28: He sent Judah ahead of them to meet with Joseph, who would be guiding them to Goshen, and so they arrived.
46.29: Joseph prepared his chariot and went to meet his father Israel in Goshen. As soon as Jacob appeared in his presence, he fell on his neck and wept on his neck for a long time.
46.30: "Now let me die," Israel told Joseph, since I've seen your face and confirmed that you're still alive!"
46.31: But Joseph addressed his brothers and his father's household and told them, "I'll go up and tell Pharaoh that my brothers and my father's household have arrived from Canaan to be with me.
46.32: I'll mention that the men are shepherds. Because they've been taking care of livestock, they brought along their flocks, their herds, and everything else that they own.
46.33: When Pharaoh calls for you and asks you 'What's your occupation'
46.34: you are to tell him, 'Your servants have been taking care of livestock from our youth right up to this day. This includes we and our ancestors.' That way, you'll be able to live in the Goshen territory, since shepherds are detestable to the Egyptians."

47.1: After this, Joseph went to inform Pharaoh. "My father and brothers have come here from Canaan," he said, "and they've come with their flocks, herds, and everything else they have. I settled them in the Goshen territory!"
47.2: He brought along five of his brothers to present before Pharaoh.
47.3: Pharaoh asked his brothers, "What are your occupations?" "Your servants are shepherds," they replied, "both we and our ancestors.
47.4: We've come to live for a while in this region, since there is no pasture back in Canaan for your servants' flocks. May your servants please live in the Goshen territory?"
47.5: Then Pharaoh replied to Joseph, "Now that your father and your brothers have come to you,
47.6: Egypt is at your disposal, so settle your father and brother in the best part of the land! Let them live in the Goshen territory. If you learn that any of them are especially skilled, put them in charge of my livestock."
47.7: Later on, Joseph brought his father Jacob, introduced him to Pharaoh, and blessed Pharaoh.
47.8: "How old are you?" he asked Jacob.
47.9: "I'm 130 years old," Jacob replied. "My years have turned out to be few and unpleasant, but I haven't yet reached the age my ancestors did during their sojourn on earth."
47.10: Then Jacob blessed Pharaoh and then left the throne room.
47.11: Jacob settled his father and brothers, assigned them land to own in the best part of Egypt, in the territory of Rameses, just as Pharaoh had ordered.
47.12: Joseph provided food for his father, his brothers, and all of his father's household, proportionate to the number of young children.
47.13: Meanwhile, there continued to be no food throughout the land, because the famine remained very severe. As a result, both Egypt and Canaan languished under the effects of the famine.
47.14: So Joseph kept on gathering all the money that was to be found throughout Egypt and Canaan in exchange for the grain that was being purchased. He stored the money in Pharaoh's palace.
47.15: After all the money had been spent throughout Egypt and Canaan, all the Egyptians came to Joseph and demanded, "Give us food! Why should we die right in front of you -  Our money is spent!
47.16: "You can surrender your livestock," Joseph replied. "I'll feed them in exchange, since your money is gone."
47.17: So they brought their livestock to Joseph, and Joseph traded them for food in exchange for horses, various flocks and herds, and donkeys. He fed them with food in exchange for their livestock during that year.
47.18: The following year, they came to him and reminded him, "We won't hide from you, your Excellency, that we've spent all of our money, and that our livestock all belong to you. There's nothing left to trade with you, your Excellency, except our bodies and our territories.
47.19: So why should we and our land die right in front of you -  Buy us and our land in exchange for food, and we and our land will be slaves to Pharaoh. Give us seed, so we can survive and not die, and so the land won't stay desolate."
47.20: So Joseph purchased all of the Egyptian territory for Pharaoh. Every Egyptian sold his field, because the famine's effect was so severe. That's how Pharaoh came to own the land.
47.21: Then Joseph transported the people to cities from one end of Egypt to the other.
47.22: However, he did not purchase land belonging to the priests, because the priests held an allotment, previously provided to them by Pharaoh, from which they lived. That's why they did not sell their land.
47.23: After this, Joseph addressed the people. "Pay attention," he said, I've bought you and your land for Pharaoh today, in exchange for seed for you. Now go sow the land.
47.24: When harvest season arrives, you are to provide a fifth of the harvest to Pharaoh. The remaining four fifths are to be for your use, for seed, and to feed you, your households, and your little ones."
47.25: "You've saved our lives," they replied. "Let us be approved by you, your Excellency, and we'll be Pharaoh's slaves."
47.26: So Joseph crafted a statute concerning Egypt that remains valid to this day that Pharaoh should own a fifth of the produce, excluding the land belonging to the priests, which remained outside of Pharaoh's control.
47.27: Israel remained in Egypt's Goshen territory, acquired land there, became prosperous, and grew very numerous.
47.28: He lived for seventeen more years in Egypt, until he had lived a total of 147 years.
47.29: As the time approached for Israel to die, he called for his son Joseph and addressed him. "Please," he asked, "if you're happy with me, make a solemn promise that you'll treat me fairly and kindly by not burying me in Egypt.
47.30: Instead, when I've died, as my ancestors have, you are to carry me out of Egypt and bury me in their tomb." "I'll do what you've asked," he replied.
47.31: "Promise me," Israel insisted. So Joseph promised. Then Israel collapsed on the bed.

48.1: Some time later, somebody informed Joseph, "Your father is ill!" So he took his two sons Manasseh and Ephraim with him to visit Jacob.
48.2: As soon as Jacob was informed, "Look! Your son Joseph has come to visit you," Israel rallied his strength and sat up in bed.
48.3: Jacob reminded Joseph, "God Almighty revealed himself to me at Luz in Canaan and blessed me.
48.4: He told me, 'Pay attention! I'm going to make you fruitful and numerous. I'm going to build you into a vast nation of people and then I'll give this land to your descendants who come after you for an eternal possession.'
48.5: "You have two sons who were born to you in Egypt before I came to be with you, whom I now take as my own. Ephraim and Manasseh are mine, just as Reuben and Simeon are.
48.6: Your descendants born after you are to be reckoned as yours and are to be referred to among the names of their brothers in their respective inheritances.
48.7: "Now as for me, Rachel died after I arrived in Canaan from Paddan, much to my sorrow. While I was on the journey to Ephrath, I buried her there on the way to Ephrath, which is also known as Bethlehem."
48.8: Just then, Israel saw Joseph's sons and asked, "Who are these?"
48.9: "These are my sons," Joseph replied to his father. "God gave them to me here in Egypt." "Please bring them close to me," Jacob said, "so I can bless them."
48.10: Now Israel's eyesight had become dim from age. Because he couldn't see well, Joseph brought them close to him, and Israel kissed them both and embraced them.
48.11: Then he told Joseph, "I never thought I'd see you again, and now God has allowed me to see your children as well!"4810
48.12: Joseph took them off his knees and then bowed low with his face to the ground. 01Gn 48:13Then he brought them both close to his father, placing Ephraim with his right hand toward Israel's left and Manasseh with his left hand toward Israel's right.
48.14: But Israel stretched out his right hand, laying it on Ephraim's head (he was the younger son) and laying his left hand on Manasseh's head (even though Manasseh was the first born).
48.15: Then Israel blessed Joseph by saying： "May the God in whose presence my ancestors Abraham and Isaac walked, the God who has continued shepherding me my whole life even until today,
48.16: the angel who has been redeeming me from all sorts of evil, bless these young men. May my name continue to live on within them, including the names of my ancestors Abraham and Isaac, and may they grow into a vast multitude in the midst of the earth."
48.17: But Joseph observed that his father had laid his right hand on Ephraim's head. That displeased him, so he grabbed his father's hand and started to remove it from Ephraim's head to Manasseh's head.
48.18: "No, father, this one is the first born. Place your right hand on his head."
48.19: But his father refused. "I know," he said. "I know. He's going to produce a large nation, and he's going to be very great. However, his younger brother will become even greater than he, and his descendants will become a multitude of nations."
48.20: That very day, he blessed them with this blessing： "By you Israel will extend this blessing： 'May God make you like Ephraim and Manasseh!'" By doing this, he placed Ephraim before Manasseh.
48.21: Then Israel told Joseph, "Pay attention! I'm about to die, but God will be with you. He'll bring you back to the land that belongs to your ancestors.
48.22: I'm assigning one portion more than your brothers from the land that I confiscated from the control of the Amorites in battle.

49.1: After this, Jacob called his sons together and told them, "Assemble yourselves around me so I can tell you all what is going to happen to you in the last days.
49.2: "Gather together and listen, you children of Jacob. Listen to your father Israel."
49.3: "Reuben, you're my first born, my strength, and the firstfruit of my vitality. You excel in rank and excel in power.
49.4: But you're as undisciplined as a roaring river, so eventually you won't succeed, because you got in your father's bed, defiled it, and then approached my couch."
49.5: "Simeon and Levi are brothers; their violent weapon is their swords.
49.6: I'll never join their council; I'll never enter their assembly. In their anger they committed murder and lamed cattle just for fun.
49.7: Their anger is cursed, because it is so fierce, as is their vehemence, because it is so cruel. I will separate them throughout Jacob's territory and disperse them throughout Israel."
49.8: "Your brothers will praise you, Judah. Your hand will be at the throat of your enemies, and your father's children will bow down to you.
49.9: Judah is a lion cub. My son, you have gone up from the prey.       Crouching like a lion, he lies down, Like a lioness, who would dare rouse him -
49.10: The scepter will never depart from Judah, nor a ruler's staff from between his feet, until the one comes who owns them, and to him will belong the allegiance of nations.
49.11: Binding his donkey to the vine and his mare's foal to its thick tendrils, he will wash his garments in wine and its robe in the juice of grapes.
49.12: His eyes are darker than wine and his teeth whiter than milk."
49.13: "Zebulun will settle down near the sea shore and become a safe haven for shipping, bordering Sidon."
49.14: "Issachar is a strong donkey, resting between sheepfolds.
49.15: He observed that his resting place was excellent, and that the land was pleasant, he bent down, picked up his burdens, and became a slave at forced labor."
49.16: "Dan will judge his people as one of Israel's tribe.
49.17: Dan will be a snake on the path, a viper on the road that snaps at the heels of horses, causing its rider to fall off.
49.19: "Bandits will raid Gad, but Gad will raid them back."
49.20: "Asher's food will be delicious; he will be a provider of delicacies fit for royalty."
49.21: "Naphtali is a free running deer who produces eloquent literature."
49.22: "Joseph is descended from a fruitful vine, a fruitful vine planted near water springs. His branches climb over walls.
49.23: Even though enemies attacked him, shooting at him and pursuing him viciously,
49.24: nevertheless his bow remained steady and his arms kept in shape by the strength of Jacob's Mighty One, in the name of the Shepherd, Israel's Rock,
49.25: by your father's God, who will continually help you, by the Almighty, who will keep on blessing you with blessings from heaven above, with blessings from the deepest ocean, with blessing from the breast and womb.
49.26: Your father's blessings will prove to be stronger than blessings from the eternal mountains or bounties from the everlasting hills. May they come to rest on Joseph's head, May they be set upon the brow of the one who was separated from his own brothers."
49.27: "Benjamin is vicious like a wolf; what he kills in the morning he devours in the evening."
49.28: That's how Israel blessed these twelve tribes of Israel, and this is what their father told them when he pronounced his blessing for them, blessing each one with a blessing suitable for them.
49.29: In his last words, Jacob issued this set of instructions to them all： "I'm about to be taken home to our people. Bury me alongside my ancestors in the cave in the field that used to belong to Ephron the Hittite.
49.30: It's the cave in the field near Mamre at Machpelah in the land of Canaan that Abraham bought to serve as a cemetery.
49.31: It's where Abraham and his wife Sarah were buried, where Isaac and his wife Rebekah were buried, and where I buried Leah.
49.32: Both the field and the cave that's in it were purchased from the Hittites."
49.33: After concluding this set of instructions to his sons, he tucked his feet up into bed, quit breathing, and was gathered to his people.

50.1: Then Joseph embraced his father, cried over him, and kissed him.
50.2: Then he issued orders to his physician servants to embalm his father. So they embalmed Israel.
50.3: It took 40 days to complete the process, the normal period required for embalming. Meanwhile, the Egyptians mourned for him for 70 days.
50.4: At the conclusion of the mourning period, Joseph addressed Pharaoh's household. "If you're satisfied with me, please take this message to Pharaoh for me. Tell him,
50.5: 'My father said to me, "Look! I'm about to die. Bury me in my grave that I dug for myself in the land of Canaan." So please let me travel to bury my father. I'll be right back.'"
50.6: "Please go," Pharaoh replied. "Bury your father, as he asked you to do."
50.7: So Joseph got up and went to bury his father, accompanied by all of Pharaoh's servants, all of the elders of Egypt,
50.8: all of Joseph's household, his brothers, and his father's household. They left behind in the territory of Goshen only their youngest children, their flocks, and their herds.
50.9: Chariots and horsemen also accompanied him, so there was a very large company
50.10: When they arrived at Atad's threshing floor, which is located beyond the Jordan River, they held a great and mournful memorial service, during which he spent seven days in mourning for his father.
50.11: As soon as the Canaanites who lived in the land observed the mourning going on at Atad's threshing floor, they commented "This is a significant time of mourning for the Egyptians." That's why the place, which is located beyond the Jordan River, became known as Abel-Mitzraim.
50.12: And so Israel's sons did what he had instructed them to do：
50.13: his sons carried him to the territory of Canaan and buried him in the cave in Machpelah field near Mamre that Abraham had purchased as a cemetery from Ephron the Hittite.
50.14: After he had buried his father, Joseph and his brothers returned to Egypt, along with everyone who had gone with him to attend the burial.13
50.15: Later, after Joseph's brothers faced the reality of their father's death, they asked themselves, "What happens if Joseph decides to hold a grudge against us -  What if he pays us back in full for all the wrong things we did to him?"
50.16: So they sent this message to Joseph：
50.17: "Before he died, your father left some instructions. He told us, "Tell Joseph, 'Please forgive your brothers' offenses. I beg you, forgive their sins, because they wronged you.'" So please forgive the transgression of the servants of your father's God." Joseph wept when they talk to him.
50.18: So his brothers went to visit, fell prostrate in his presence, and declared, "Look! We're your servants."
50.19: "Don't be afraid," Joseph responded. "Am I sitting in God's place -
50.20: As far as you're concerned, you were planning evil against me, but God intended it for good, planning to bring about the present result so that many people would be preserved alive.
50.21: So don't be afraid! I'll take care of you and your little ones." So Joseph kept on comforting then, speaking to the needs of their hearts.
50.22: Joseph continued to live in Egypt, along with his father's household, until he was 110 years old.
50.23: Joseph saw the third generation of Ephraim's children, as well as the children who had been born to Manasseh's son Machir, whom he adopted as his own.
50.24: Later, Joseph told his brothers, "I'm going to die soon, but God will certainly provide for you and bring you up from this land to the land that he promised with an oath to give to Abraham, to Isaac, and to Jacob."
50.25: So Joseph made all of Israel's other children promise "Because God is certainly going to take care of you, you are to carry my bones up from here."
50.26: Some time later, Joseph died at the age of 110 years, and he was embalmed and placed in a coffin in Egypt.

1.1: These are the names of the Israelites who entered Egypt with Jacob, each one having come with his family：
1.2: Reuben, Simeon, Levi, Judah,
1.3: Issacar, Zebulun, Benjamin,
1.4: Dan, Naphtali, Gad, and Asher.
1.5: All those who descended from Jacob totaled 75 persons. Now Joseph was already in Egypt.
1.6: Then Joseph, all his brothers, and that entire generation died.
1.7: But the Israelites were fruitful and increased abundantly. They multiplied in numbers and became very, very strong. As a result, the land was filled with them.
1.8: Eventually a new king who was unacquainted with Joseph came to power in Egypt.
1.9: He said to his people, "Look, the Israelite people are more numerous and more powerful than we are.
1.10: Come on, let's be careful how we treat them, so that when they grow numerous, if a war breaks out they won't join our enemies, fight against us, and leave our land."
1.11: So the Egyptians placed supervisors over them, oppressing them with heavy burdens. The Israelites built the supply cities of Pithom and Rameses for Pharaoh.
1.12: But the more the Egyptians afflicted the Israelites, the more they multiplied and flourished, so that the Egyptians became terrified of the Israelites.
1.13: The Egyptians ruthlessly forced the Israelites to serve them,
1.14: making their lives bitter through hard labor with mortar, bricks, and all kinds of outdoor labor. They ruthlessly imposed all this work on them.
1.15: Later, the king of Egypt spoke to the Hebrew midwives, one of whom was named Shifrah and the other Puah.
1.16: "When you help the Hebrew women give birth," he said, "watch them as they deliver. If it's a son, kill him; but if it's a daughter, let her live."
1.17: But the midwives feared God and did not do what the king of Egypt told them. Instead, they let the boys live.
1.18: When the king of Egypt called for the midwives, he asked them, "Why have you done this and allowed the boys to live?"1
1.19: "Hebrew women aren't like Egyptian women," the midwives replied to Pharaoh. They're so healthy that they give birth before the midwives arrive to help them."
1.20: God was pleased with the midwives, and the people multiplied and became very strong.
1.21: Because the midwives feared God, he provided families for them.
1.22: Meanwhile, Pharaoh continued commanding all of his people, "You're to throw away every Hebrew son who is born, but you're to allow every Hebrew daughter to live."

2.1: A man of the family of Levi married a Levite's daughter.
2.2: Later, the woman became pregnant and gave birth to a son. She saw that he was a beautiful child, and hid him for three months.
2.3: But when she was no longer able to hide him, she took a papyrus container, coated it with asphalt and pitch, placed the child in it, and put it among the reeds along the bank of the Nile.
2.4: His sister positioned herself some distance away in order to find out what would happen to him.
2.5: Then Pharaoh's daughte rcame down to the Nile to bathe while her maids walked along the river bank. She saw the container among the reeds and sent a servant girl to get it.
2.6: When she opened it and saw the child, the little boy suddenly began crying. Filled with compassion for him, she exclaimed, "This is one of the Hebrew children!"
2.7: Then his sister asked Pharaoh's daughter, "Shall I go and call one of the nursing Hebrew women so she can nurse the child for you?"
2.8: Pharaoh's daughter said to her, "Go," so the young girl went and called the child's mother.
2.9: Pharaoh's daughter instructed her, "Take this child and nurse him for me, and I'll pay you a salary." So the woman took the child and nursed him.
2.10: After the child had grown older, she brought him to Pharaoh's daughter, and he became her son. She named him Moses, because she said, "I drew him out of the water."
2.11: Years later, after Moses had grown up, he went out to his own people, and took notice of their heavy burdens. He saw an Egyptian beating up a Hebrew, one of his own people.
2.12: Looking around and seeing no one else, he killed the Egyptian and hid him in the sand.
2.13: Going out the next day, Moses noticed two Hebrew men fighting right in front of him. He said to the one who was at fault, "Why did you strike your companion?"
2.14: The man replied, "Who appointed you to be an official judge over us -  Are you planning to kill me like you killed the Egyptian?" Then Moses became terrified and said to himself, "Surely this event has become known!"
2.15: When Pharaoh heard about this matter, he tried to kill Moses. So Moses fled from Pharaoh, settled in the land of Midian, and sat down by a well.
2.16: Meanwhile, the seven daughters of a certain Midianite priest would come to draw water in order to fill water troughs for their father's sheep.
2.17: Some shepherds came to drive them away, but Moses got up, came to their rescue, and watered their sheep.
2.18: When they returned to their father Reuel, he asked, "Why have you returned so quickly today?"
2.19: "An Egyptian rescued us from the shepherds," they replied, "and he even drew water for us and watered the sheep!"2
2.20: "Then where is he?" He asked his daughters. "Why did you leave the man behind -  Go invite him to have something to eat."
2.21: Moses agreed to stay with the man, and he gave his daughter Zipporah to Moses in marriage.
2.22: Later she gave birth to a son, and Moses named him Gershom, because he used to say, "I became an alien in a foreign land."
2.23: The king of Egypt eventually died, and the Israelites groaned because of the bondage. They cried out, and their cry for deliverance from slavery ascended to God.
2.24: God heard their groaning and remembered his covenant with Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob.
2.25: God watched the Israelites and took notice of them.

3.1: Meanwhile, Moses continued tending the sheep that belonged to his father-in-law Jethro, the priest of Midian. He led the sheep to the western desert and came to Horeb, God's mountain, where
3.2: the angel of the Lord appeared to him in flaming fire from the center of a bush. As Moses continued to watch, amazingly the bush kept on burning but was not consumed.
3.3: Then Moses said to himself, "I'll go over and see this remarkable sight. Why isn't the bush burning up?"2
3.4: When the Lord saw that he had gone over to look, God called to him from the center of the bush, "Moses! Moses!" He said, "Here I am."
3.5: "Do not come any closer," God said. "Remove your sandals from your feet, because the place where you are standing is holy ground."
3.6: Then he said, "I am the God of your ancestors, the God of Abraham, the God of Isaac, and the God of Jacob." At this, Moses hid his face because he was afraid to look at God.
3.7: The Lord said, "I have certainly seen the affliction of my people who are in Egypt, and I have heard their cry caused by their slave masters. I really do understand their pain,
3.8: so I have come down to deliver them from their domination by the Egyptians and to bring them out of that land to a good and spacious land, a land flowing with milk and honey, to the territory of the Canaanites, the Hittites, the Amorites, the Perizzites, the Hivites, and the Jebusites.
3.9: Now, listen carefully! The cry of the Israelites has come to my attention about how severely the Egyptians have been oppressing them.
3.10: So go! I am sending you to Pharaoh. Bring my people the Israelites out of Egypt."
3.11: But Moses said to God, "Who am I -  How can I go to Pharaoh and bring the Israelites out of Egypt?"
3.12: Then God said, "I certainly will be with you. And this will be the sign for you that it is I who sent you： When you have brought the people out of Egypt, all of you will serve God on this mountain."
3.13: Moses said to God, "Look, when I go to the Israelites and tell them, 'The God of your ancestors sent me to you,' they'll say to me, 'What is his name?' What should I say to them?"
3.14: God replied to Moses, "I AM WHO I AM," and then said, "Tell the Israelites： 'I AM sent me to you.'"
3.15: God also said to Moses, "Tell the Israelites, 'The Lord, the God of your ancestors, the God of Abraham, the God of Isaac, and the God of Jacob sent me to you.' This is my name forever, and this is how I am to be remembered from generation to generation.
3.16: Go and gather the elders of Israel. Tell them, 'The Lord, the God of your ancestors, appeared to me - the God of Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob - and he said, "I have paid close attention to you and to what has been done to you in Egypt.
3.17: I have said that I will bring you out of the affliction of Egypt to the land of the Canaanites, the Hittites, the Amorites, the Perizzites, the Hivites, and the Jebusites - to a land flowing with milk and honey."'3
3.18: "The elders of Israel will listen to you, and then you and they are to go to the king of Egypt and say to him, 'The Lord, the God of the Hebrews, has met with us. Now, let us take a three day journey into the desert to sacrifice to the Lord our God.'
3.19: I know that the king of Egypt won't allow you to go unless compelled to do so by force,
3.20: so I will stretch out my hand and strike Egypt with all my wonders that I will do there. After that he will release you.
3.21: I will grant this people public favor with the Egyptians, and as a result, when you leave you won't go empty-handed.
3.22: Each woman is to ask her neighbor or any foreign woman in her house for articles of gold and for clothing, and use them to clothe your sons and daughters. You will plunder the Egyptians."3

4.1: Then Moses answered, "Look, they won't believe me and they won't listen to me. Instead, they'll say, 'The Lord didn't appear to you.'"
4.2: "What's that in your hand?" the Lord asked him. Moses answered, "A staff."
4.3: Then God said, "Throw it on the ground." He threw it on the ground and it became a snake. Moses ran away from it.
4.4: Then God said to Moses, "Reach out and grab its tail." So he reached out, grabbed it, and it became a staff in his hand.
4.5: God said, "I've done this so that they may believe that the Lord, the God of their ancestors - the God of Abraham, the God of Isaac, and the God of Jacob - has appeared to you."
4.6: Again the Lord said to him, "Put your hand into your bosom." He put his hand into his bosom and as soon as he brought it out it was leprous, like snow.
4.7: Then God said, "Put your hand back into your bosom." He returned it to his bosom and as soon as he brought it out, it was restored like the rest of his skin.
4.8: "Then if they don't believe you and respond to the first sign, they may respond to the second sign.
4.9: But if they don't believe even these two signs, and won't listen to you, then take some water out of the Nile and pour it on the dry ground. The water you took from the Nile will turn into blood on the dry ground."
4.10: Then Moses said to the Lord, "Please, Lord, I'm not eloquent. I never was in the past nor am I now since you spoke to your servant. In fact, I talk too slowly and I have a speech impediment."
4.11: Then God asked him, "Who gives a person a mouth -  Who makes him unable to speak, or deaf, or able to see, or blind, or lame -  Is it not I, the Lord -
4.12: Now, go! I myself will help you with your speech, and I'll teach you what you are to say."
4.13: Moses said, "Please, Lord, send somebody else."
4.14: Then the Lord was angry with Moses and said, "There is your brother Aaron the Levite isn't there -  I know that he certainly is eloquent. Right now he's coming to meet you and he will be pleased to see you.
4.15: You're to speak to him and tell him what to say. I'll help both you and him with your speech, and I'll teach both of you what you are to do.
4.16: He is to speak to the people for you as your spokesman and you are to act in the role of God for him.
4.17: Now pick up that staff with your hand. You'll use it to perform the signs."
4.18: Moses left and returned to his father-in-law Jethro. Moses said to him, "Please let me go and return to my own people in Egypt so I can see whether they're still alive." Jethro said to Moses, "Go in peace."
4.19: The Lord said to Moses in Midian, "Go back to Egypt, because all the men who wanted to kill you are dead."
4.20: So Moses took his wife and son, put them on the donkey, and headed back to the land of Egypt. Moses took the staff of God in his hand.
4.21: Then the Lord said to Moses, "When you set out to return to Egypt, keep in mind all the wonders that I've put in your power, so that you may do them before Pharaoh. But I'll harden his heart so that he won't let the people go.
4.22: You are to say to Pharaoh, 'This is what the Lord says： "Israel is my firstborn son.
4.23: And I say to you, 'Let my son go so he may serve me.' If you refuse to let him go, then, I will kill your firstborn son."'"4
4.24: But later on, at the lodging place along the way, the Lord met Moses and was about to kill him.
4.25: Zipporah took a flint knife, cut off the foreskin of her son, and touched Moses' feet with it, saying while doing so, "…because you are a bridegroom of blood to me."
4.26: Then the Lord withdrew from him, and she said, "…a bridegroom of blood because of circumcision."
4.27: The Lord said to Aaron, "Go meet Moses in the desert." So Aaron went, found him at the mountain of God, and kissed him.
4.28: Moses told Aaron all of the Lord's messages that he had sent with Moses, and all of the signs that he commanded him to do.
4.29: Later, Moses and Aaron brought together all the elders of the Israelites.
4.30: Aaron spoke everything that the Lord had spoken to Moses, and Moses performed the miracles before the very eyes of the people.
4.31: The people believed and understood that the Lord had paid attention to the Israelites and had seen their affliction. They bowed their heads and prostrated themselves in worship.

5.1: After Moses and Aaron arrived, they said to Pharaoh, "This is what the Lord God of Israel says： 'Let my people go so they may make a pilgrimage for me in the desert."
5.2: Pharaoh said, "Who is the Lord that I should listen to him and let Israel go -  I don't know about the Lord, nor will I let Israel go!"
5.3: Then they said, "The God of the Hebrews has met with us. Please let us go a three-day journey into the desert to offer sacrifices to the Lord our God so he does not strike us with pestilence or sword.
5.4: The king of Egypt replied to them, "Moses and Aaron, why are you keeping the people from their labor -  Go back to your work!"
5.5: Then Pharaoh said, "Look, the people in the land are now numerous, and you are stopping them from working."
5.6: That day Pharaoh ordered the taskmasters of the people and their officials,
5.7: "You're no longer to give the people straw for making bricks, as in the past. They must gather straw for themselves.
5.8: But you're to impose the previous quota of bricks that they're making. You're not to reduce it! It is because they're lazy that they're crying out, 'Let's go offer sacrifices to our God.'
5.9: So increase the work load on the people, and let them do it so they don't pay attention to deceptive speeches."8
5.10: Then the taskmasters of the people and their officials went out and said to the people, "This is Pharaoh's response： 'I'll no longer give you any straw.
5.11: Go get straw for yourselves wherever you can find it, but your work quotas won't be reduced at all.'"
5.12: So the people scattered throughout the entire land of Egypt to collect stubble for straw.
5.13: The taskmasters pressured them by saying, "Finish your work - each day's quota - just as when you were given straw."
5.14: The Israelite supervisors whom Pharaoh's taskmasters had appointed over them were beaten and told, "Why didn't you, both yesterday and today, fulfill your quota for making bricks as before?"
5.15: The Israelite supervisors came and cried out to Pharaoh, "Why are you doing this to us -
5.16: No straw is being given to us, yet they're saying to us, 'Make bricks!' Look, we are being beaten. It's wrong how you are treating your people!"
5.17: Then Pharaoh said, "You are lazy, lazy! That's why you're saying, 'Let's go offer sacrifices to the Lord.'
5.18: Now, go! Work! And straw won't be given to you, but you will deliver the same number of bricks!"
5.19: The Israelite supervisors realized they were in trouble when he said, "You won't reduce each day's quota of bricks!"
5.20: As they left Pharaoh's presence, they met Moses and Aaron standing there.
5.21: The supervisors said to them, "May the Lord look on you and judge you! You have made us repulsive in the eyes of Pharaoh and in the eyes of his servants. You have put a sword in their hands to kill us."
5.22: So Moses returned to the Lord and asked him, "Lord, why have you caused trouble for this people -  Why have you sent me here -
5.23: Ever since I came to Pharaoh to speak in your name, he has caused trouble for this people, and you have done nothing to deliver your people."

6.1: The Lord said to Moses, "Now you will see what I'll do to Pharaoh. Indeed, he'll send them out under compulsion and he'll drive them out of his land violently."
6.2: Later, God told Moses, "I am the Lord.
6.3: I appeared to Abraham, to Isaac, and to Jacob as God Almighty, and did I not reveal to them my name 'Lord' -
6.4: I also established my covenant with them to give them the land of Canaan, the land where they lived as resident aliens for a time.
6.5: Also, I've heard the groaning of the Israelites whom the Egyptians have forced to labor for them, and I've remembered my covenant.
6.6: Therefore, say to the Israelites, 'I am the Lord. I'll bring you out from under the burdens of the Egyptians, and I'll deliver you from their bondage. I'll redeem you with an outstretched arm and with great acts of judgment.
6.7: I'll take you for my own people, and I'll be your God. Then you will know that I am the Lord your God who brings you out from under the burdens of the Egyptians.
6.8: I'll bring you to the land that I swore to give to Abraham, to Isaac, and to Jacob. I'll give it to you as a possession. I am the Lord.'"
6.9: Then Moses reported this to the Israelites, but they did not listen to Moses due to their irritation and impatience because there was no deliverance and because of the cruel bondage.
6.10: Then the Lord said to Moses,
6.11: "Go, speak to Pharaoh, king of Egypt, that he should let the Israelites go out of his land."
6.12: Then Moses said right in front of the Lord, "Look, the Israelites didn't listen to me, so how will Pharaoh -  I'm not a persuasive speaker"
6.13: Then the Lord spoke to Moses and Aaron, issuing orders to them regarding the Israelites for delivery to Pharaoh, king of Egypt; that is, to bring the Israelites out of the land of Egypt.
6.14: These are the heads of their ancestors' households： the sons of Reuben, the firstborn of Israel： Hanoch and Pallu; Hezron and Carmi. These are the families of Reuben, including
6.15: Simeon's sons Jemuel, Jamin, Ohad, Jakin, Zohar, and Shaul, the Canaanite woman's son. These are the families of Simeon.
6.16: These are the names of Levi's sons according to their genealogies： Gershon, Kohath, and Merari. Levi lived 137 years.
6.17: Gershon's sons were Libni and Shimei, according to their families.
6.18: Kohath's descendants included Amram, Izhar, Hebron, and Uzziel.1Now Kohath lived for 133 years.
6.19: The sons of Merari： Mahli and Mushi. These are the families of the Levites according to their genealogies.6
6.20: Amram married Jocebed, his father's sister, and she bore him Aaron and Moses. Amram lived for 137 years.
6.21: The sons of Izhar： Korath, Nepheg, and Zichri.
6.22: The sons of Uzziel： Mishael, Elzaphan, and Sithri.
6.23: Then Aaron married Elisheba daughter of Amminadab, sister of Nahshon. She bore him Nadab, Abihu, Eleazer, and Ithamar.
6.24: The sons of Korah： Assir, Elqanah, and Abiasaph. These were the families of the Korahites.
6.25: Aaron's son Eleazar married one of Putiel's daughters, and she bore him Phineas. These are the heads of the ancestors of the Levites, according to their families.
6.26: This is the same Aaron and Moses to whom the Lord said, "Bring the Israelites out of the land of Egypt by their tribal divisions."
6.27: They were the ones speaking to Pharaoh king of Egypt to bring the Israelites out of Egypt; this is the same Moses and Aaron.
6.28: And it happened when the Lord spoke to Moses in the land of Egypt 29that the Lord told Moses, "I am the Lord. Tell Pharaoh king of Egypt everything that I'm saying to you."
6.30: Moses said in the presence of the Lord, "Look, I'm not a persuasive speaker, so how will Pharaoh listen to me?"

7.1: The Lord said to Moses, "Listen! I've put you in the role of God to Pharaoh, and your brother Aaron will be your prophet.
7.2: You are to speak all that I've commanded you, and then your brother Aaron will speak to Pharaoh, telling him to let the Israelites go out of his land.
7.3: I'll harden Pharaoh's heart and I'll add more and more of my signs and wonders in the land of Egypt.
7.4: When Pharaoh won't listen to you, I'll let loose my power upon Egypt. I'll bring out my tribal divisions - my people the Israelites - from the land of Egypt with great acts of judgment.
7.5: The Egyptians will know that I am the Lord when I stretch out my hand over Egypt to bring the Israelites out from among them."
7.6: Moses and Aaron did what the Lord commanded them.
7.7: Moses was 80 years old and Aaron was 83 when they spoke to Pharaoh.
7.8: Then the Lord said to Moses and to Aaron,
7.9: "When Pharaoh says to you, 'Perform a miraculous sign,' then you are to say to Aaron, 'Take your staff and throw it in front of Pharaoh.' It will become a serpent."
7.10: Then Moses and Aaron went in to Pharaoh and did what the Lord had commanded them. Aaron threw his staff in front of Pharaoh and his officials, and it became a serpent.
7.11: Then Pharaoh also called for the wise men and sorcerers, and they - along with the Egyptian magicians - did the same thing with their secret arts.
7.12: So each one threw down his staff and it became a serpent, but Aaron's staff swallowed up their staffs.
7.13: Yet Pharaoh's heart was stubborn and he did not listen to them, just as the Lord had said would happen.
7.14: Then the Lord said to Moses, "Pharaoh's heart is hard. He has refused to let the people go.
7.15: Go to Pharaoh in the morning as he's going down to the water. Stand on the bank of the Nile and meet him. Be sure to take with you the staff that was turned into a snake.
7.16: Then say to him, 'The Lord, the God of the Hebrews, has sent me to you. He says, "Let my people go so they may serve me in the desert, but until now you haven't obeyed.
7.17: 'This is what the Lord says： "By this you will know that I am the Lord： Right now I'm going to strike the water of the Nile with the staff that's in my hand, and it will be turned to blood.
7.18: The fish in the Nile will die and the river will stink. The Egyptians will be unable to drink water from the Nile."'"'"
7.19: The Lord also told Moses, "Tell Aaron, 'Take your staff and stretch out your hand over the waters of Egypt, over their rivers, over their Nile, over their ponds, and over their reservoirs, and they'll become blood. There will be blood throughout the land of Egypt, even in their wood and stone containers.'"
7.20: Moses and Aaron did just what the Lord had commanded. Aaron raised his staff and struck the water in the Nile in front of Pharaoh and his officials, and all the water in the Nile turned to blood.
7.21: The fish in the Nile died and the river stank. The Egyptians were not able to drink water from the Nile, and blood was throughout the land of Egypt.
7.22: But the Egyptian magicians did the same thing with their secret arts. Pharaoh's heart was stubborn, and he did not listen to them, just as the Lord had said.
7.23: Then Pharaoh turned away, went to his palace, and paid no attention to any of this.
7.24: All the Egyptians dug around the Nile for water to drink because they could not drink from the water in the Nile.24

8.1: 6Then the Lord said to Moses, "Tell Aaron, 'Stretch out your staff, strike the dust of the ground, and the dust will become gnats throughout the land of Egypt.'"
8.2: And if you refuse to let them go, then I'm going to strike all your territory with frogs.
8.3: The Nile will swarm with frogs. They'll come up and enter your house, your bedroom, your bed, and your servants' houses. They'll jump on your people, into your ovens, and into your kneading troughs.
8.4: The frogs will be all over you and your servants."'"
8.5: Then the Lord said to Moses, "Tell Aaron, 'Stretch out your hand with your staff over the rivers, over the Nile, and over the ponds, and bring up frogs over the land of Egypt.'"
8.6: So Aaron stretched his hand over the waters of Egypt, and the frogs came up and covered the land of Egypt.
8.7: But the magicians did the same thing with their secret arts, and they brought up frogs on the land of Egypt.
8.8: Then Pharaoh called to Moses and Aaron and said, "Plead with the Lord so that he may remove the frogs from me and my people. I'll let the people go so they can offer sacrifices to the Lord."
8.9: Moses said to Pharaoh, "You decide when I should plead for you, your servants, and your people to remove the frogs from you and your household. They'll remain only in the Nile."
8.17: They did this. Aaron stretched his hand out with his staff, struck the dust of the land, and gnats came on people and animals - all the dust of the ground became gnats throughout the land of Egypt.
8.18: The magicians tried to do the same thing with their secret arts, but they were unable to bring out the gnats. The gnats were on the people and the animals.
8.19: The magicians said to Pharaoh, "It is the finger of God!" But Pharaoh's heart was stubborn and he did not listen to them, just as the Lord had said.
8.20: The Lord said to Moses, "Get up early in the morning and stand before Pharaoh as he's going down to the water. You are to say to him, 'This is what the Lord says： "Let my people go so they can serve me.
8.21: But if you don't let my people go, I'll send swarms of insects upon you, your servants, your people, and your households. The houses of Egypt - and even the ground on which they stand - will be filled with swarms of insects.
8.22: On that day I'll treat the land of Goshen where my people live differently so that swarms of insects won't be there. As a result, you will know that I the Lord am in the midst of the land.
8.23: I'll make a distinction between my people and your people, and this sign will occur tomorrow."'"
8.24: The Lord did this, and dense swarms of insects came into the house of Pharaoh and into the houses of his servants. The land was ruined throughout Egypt because of the swarms of insects.
8.25: Then Pharaoh summoned Moses and Aaron and said, "Go, offer sacrifices to your God in the land."
8.26: Moses said, "It wouldn't be right to sacrifice in this way because if we do, we will sacrifice to the Lord our God what is offensive to the Egyptians. If we offer sacrifices that are offensive to the Egyptians in front of them, they'll stone us, won't they -
8.27: We must go a three-day journey into the desert, and we'll offer sacrifices to the Lord our God just as he has told us."
8.28: Then Pharaoh said, "I'll let you go so you can offer sacrifices to the Lord your God in the desert. But you must not go very far away. Pray for me."
8.29: Moses said, "Right now I'm going to leave you, and I'll pray to the Lord that the swarms of insects may depart from Pharaoh, from his officials, and from his people tomorrow. But Pharaoh, don't continue lying by not letting the people go to offer sacrifices to the Lord."
8.30: Then Moses left Pharaoh's presence and prayed to the Lord.
8.31: The Lord did what Moses asked, and the swarms of insects departed from Pharaoh, his officials, and his people. Not one remained.
8.32: But this time also Pharaoh hardened his heart, and he did not let the people go.8

9.1: Then the Lord said to Moses, "Go to Pharaoh and say to him, 'This is what the Lord God of the Hebrews says： "Let my people go so they may serve me.
9.2: But if you refuse to let them go and continue to hold them,
9.3: then the hand of the Lord will come with a very severe plague on your livestock in the fields, on horses, on donkeys, on camels, on cattle, and on sheep.
9.4: The Lord will distinguish between the livestock of Israel and the livestock of the Egyptians, so that nothing that belongs to the Israelites will die."'"
9.5: The Lord set the time： "Tomorrow the Lord will do this thing in the land."
9.6: The Lord did this thing the next day, and all the livestock of the Egyptians died. But not one of the livestock died that belonged to the Israelites.
9.7: Then Pharaoh inquired and discovered that not a single one of the livestock of Israel had died, but Pharaoh's heart was stubborn and he would not let the people go.
9.8: Then the Lord said to Moses and Aaron, "Take handfuls of soot from a kiln, and let Moses throw it into the air in front of Pharaoh.
9.9: The soot will become dust over the entire land of Egypt, and it will become boils erupting into sores on people and animals throughout the land of Egypt."
9.10: So they took soot from the kiln and stood before Pharaoh. Then Moses threw it into the air, and it became boils producing running sores on people and animals.
9.11: The magicians were not able to stand before Moses because of the boils, because the boils were on the magicians and on all the Egyptians.
9.12: The Lord made Pharaoh's heart stubborn so that he would not listen to them, just as the Lord had said to Moses.
9.13: The Lord said to Moses, "Get up early in the morning, present yourself to Pharaoh, and say to him, 'This is what the Lord God of the Hebrews says： "Let my people go so they may serve me.
9.14: Indeed, this time I'm sending all my plagues against you, your officials, and your people, so you may know that there is no one like me in all the earth.
9.15: Indeed, by now I could have sent forth my hand and struck you and your people with a plague, and you would have been destroyed from the earth.
9.16: However, I've kept you standing in order to show you my power and to declare my name in all the earth.
9.17: You are still acting arrogantly against my people by not letting them go.
9.18: Look! About this time tomorrow, I'll send a severe hail storm, such as has not happened in Egypt from the day it was founded until now.
9.19: So send for your livestock and everything that belongs to you that's out in the field, because every person and animal found in the field that has not been brought inside to shelters will die when the hail comes down on them."'"
9.20: Whoever feared the word of the Lord among Pharaoh's officials made his servants and livestock flee into shelters.
9.21: But whoever did not pay attention to the word of the Lord left his servants and his livestock outside in the fields.
9.22: Then the Lord said to Moses, "Stretch out your hand toward heaven, and there will be hail in all the land of Egypt, on people, animals, and all the vegetation of the field throughout the land of Egypt.
9.23: When Moses stretched out his staff toward heaven, the Lord sent thunder and hail, and lightning struck the earth. The Lord rained hail on the land of Egypt.
9.24: There was very heavy hail, and lightning was continually flashing in the midst of the hail. There had not been anything like it in the land of Egypt since it had become a nation.
9.25: The hail struck everything, including people and animals, outside in the fields throughout the land of Egypt. The hail struck all the vegetation of the fields and shattered all the trees in the orchards.
9.26: Only in the land of Goshen, where the Israelites were, was there no hail.
9.27: Pharaoh sent word and called for Moses and Aaron. "I've sinned this time," he said to them. "The Lord is righteous, but I and my people are wicked.
9.28: Pray to the Lord! There has been enough of God's thunder and hail! I'll let you go, and you need not stay any longer."
9.29: Moses said to him, "When I leave the city I'll spread out my hands to the Lord. The thunder will cease and the hail won't continue, so that you may know that the earth belongs to the Lord.
9.30: But as for you and your officials, I know that you don't yet fear the Lord God."
9.31: (Now the flax and the barley were ruined because the barley was in ear and the flax was in bud.
9.32: The wheat and the wild grain were not ruined because they were late crops.)929
9.33: Then Moses went out of the city from Pharaoh and spread out his hands to the Lord. The thunder and hail stopped, and the rain no longer poured out on the land.
9.34: When Pharaoh saw that the rain, hail, and thunder had stopped, he continued to sin. He, along with his officials, hardened his heart. 35Pharaoh's heart was stubborn, and he did not let the Israelites go, just as the Lord had said through Moses.933

10.1: Then the Lord said to Moses, "Go to Pharaoh, for I've hardened his heart and the hearts of his officials in order to perform these signs of mine among them,
10.2: so you may tell your children and your grandchildren how I toyed with the Egyptians and about my miraculous signs that I performed among them, so all of you may know that I am the Lord.
10.3: Moses and Aaron went to Pharaoh and said to him, "This is what the Lord God of the Hebrews says： 'How long will you refuse to humble yourself before me -  Let my people go, so they may serve me.
10.4: But if you refuse to let my people go, tomorrow I'm going to bring locusts into your territory.
10.5: They'll cover the surface of the land so a person cannot see the ground, and they'll eat what is left for you of the residue from the hail. They'll also eat all your trees that grow in the orchards.
10.6: Your houses will be filled along with the houses of all your officials and the houses of all the Egyptians - something that neither your fathers nor your ancestors ever saw from the time they were on earth until now.'" Then Moses turned and left Pharaoh's presence.
10.7: Then the officials of Pharaoh said to him, "How long will this man be a snare to us -  Let the people go so they may serve the Lord their God! Don't you realize yet that Egypt is about to be destroyed?"
10.8: Moses and Aaron were brought back to Pharaoh and he said to them, "Go, serve the Lord your God. But exactly who will go?"
10.9: Moses said, "We will go with our young and with our old. We will go with our sons and our daughters, with our sheep and our cattle, because it's a festival to the Lord for us."
10.10: Then Pharaoh said to them, "The Lord will certainly be with you if I let you and your little ones go. I know some evil plan is in your mind.
10.11: No! Let the men go and serve the Lord, for that is what you were seeking." Then they were driven out from the presence of Pharaoh.
10.12: The Lord said to Moses, "Stretch out your hand over the land of Egypt to bring the locusts, and they'll come up over the land of Egypt and eat all the vegetation of the land, everything that the hail left."
10.13: Moses stretched out his staff over the land of Egypt, and the Lord sent an east wind into the land all that day and throughout the night. When morning came, the east wind brought the locusts.
10.14: The locusts came up over all the land of Egypt and settled on all the territory of Egypt in great swarms. There had never been locusts like this before nor would there ever be again.
10.15: They covered the surface of the entire land so that it was dark. They ate all the vegetation of the land and the fruit from the trees that the hail left. Nothing green was left on the trees or on the vegetation in all the land of Egypt.
10.16: Pharaoh quickly called Moses and Aaron and said, "I've sinned against the Lord your God and against you.
10.17: Now, please forgive my sin only this time, and pray to the Lord your God that he would at least remove this from me."
10.18: Moses left Pharaoh and prayed to the Lord.
10.19: Then the Lord brought a very strong west wind that took the locusts and drove them into the Reed Sea. Not one locust remained in all the territory of Egypt.
10.20: But the Lord made Pharaoh's heart stubborn and he would not let the Israelites go.1910
10.21: Then the Lord said to Moses, "Stretch your hand toward the sky and there will be darkness over the land of Egypt, a darkness that one can feel."
10.22: So Moses stretched his hand toward the sky, and there was thick darkness in all the land of Egypt for three days.
