10.23: No one could see anyone else, nor could anyone get up from his place for three days. But there was light for all the Israelites in their dwellings.
10.24: Pharaoh called Moses and said, "Go serve the Lord, but your flocks and your cattle are to remain. Even your little ones can go with you!"
10.25: Moses said, "You must let us have sacrifices and burnt offerings to offer to the Lord our God.
10.26: And even our livestock must go with us. Not a hoof will be left behind because we will use some of them to serve the Lord our God, and until we get there we won't know what we need to serve the Lord."
10.27: The Lord made Pharaoh's heart stubborn, and he did not want to let them go.
10.28: Then Pharaoh said to him, "Get away from me! Watch out that you never see my face again, because on the day you see my face, you will die!"10
10.29: Moses said, "Just as you have said, I won't see your face again!"

11.1: Then the Lord told Moses, "I'll bring one more plague on Pharaoh and Egypt. After that he'll let you leave from here, and when he lets you go, he will certainly drive you out from here.
11.2: Tell the people that each man is to ask his neighbor and each woman her neighbor for articles of silver and gold."
11.3: The Lord made the Egyptians look on the people with favor. Also the man Moses was highly regarded in the land of Egypt, both in the sight of Pharaoh's officials and in the sight of the people.
11.4: So Moses announced to Pharaoh, "This is what the Lord says： 'About midnight I'm going throughout Egypt,
11.5: and all the firstborn in the land of Egypt will die, from the firstborn of Pharaoh who sits on his throne to the firstborn of the slave girl who operates the hand mill, along with the firstborn of the animals.
11.6: There will be a great cry throughout the land of Egypt, like there has never been and never will be again.
11.7: But among the Israelites, from people to animals, not even a dog will bark, so you may know that the Lord is distinguishing between the Egyptians and the Israelites.'
11.8: All these officials of yours will come down to me, prostrate themselves to me, and say, 'Get out, you and all the people following you!' After that I'll go out." Then Moses angrily left Pharaoh.
11.9: The Lord said to Moses, "Pharaoh won't listen to you. As a result, my wonders will increase throughout the land of Egypt.
11.10: Then Moses and Aaron did all these wonders in front of Pharaoh, but the Lord made Pharaoh's heart stubborn, and he would not let the Israelites go out from his land.

12.1: The Lord said to Moses and Aaron in the land of Egypt,
12.2: "This month will mark the beginning of months for you. It will be the first month of the year for you.
12.3: Tell the entire congregation of Israel, 'On the tenth of this month they're each to take a lamb for themselves, according to their ancestors' households, one lamb for each household.
12.4: If a household is too small for a lamb, then it and its closest neighbor are to obtain one based on the number of individuals - dividing the lamb based on what each person can eat.
12.5: Your lamb is to be a year old male without blemish. You may take it from the sheep or from the goats.
12.6: It is to remain under your care until the fourteenth day of this month, and then the entire assembly of the congregation of Israel is to slaughter it at twilight.
12.7: They're to take some of the blood and put it on the two doorposts and on the lintel of the houses where they eat the lamb.
12.8: That very night they're to eat the meat, roasted over the fire, with unleavened bread and bitter herbs.
12.9: Don't eat any of it raw or boiled in water. Instead, roast it over the fire, with its head, legs, and internal organs.
12.10: Don't leave any of it until morning, and whatever does remain of it until morning you are to burn in the fire.5
12.11: "'This is how you are to eat it： with your cloak tucked into your belt, your sandals on your feet, and your staff in your hand. You are to eat it hurriedly - it's the Lord's Passover.
12.12: I'll pass through the land of Egypt that night and strike every firstborn in the land of Egypt, both people and animals. I'll execute judgments on all the gods of Egypt. I am the Lord.
12.13: The blood will be a sign for you on the houses where you are. I'll see the blood and pass over you. There will be no plague to destroy you when I strike the land of Egypt.
12.14: "'This day is to be a memorial for you, and you are to celebrate it as a festival to the Lord. You are to celebrate it as a perpetual ordinance from generation to generation.
12.15: You are to eat unleavened bread for seven days. On the first day be sure to remove all the leaven from your houses, because any person who eats anything leavened from the first day until the seventh will be cut off from Israel.
12.16: Also, on the first day you're to hold a holy assembly, and on the seventh day you're to hold a holy assembly. No work is to be done during those days, except for preparing what is to be eaten by each person.
12.17: "'You are to observe the Festival of Unleavened Bread, since on this very day I brought your tribal divisions from the land of Egypt. You are to observe this day from generation to generation as a perpetual ordinance.
12.18: In the first month, from the evening of the fourteenth day of the month until the evening of the twenty-first day of the month, you are to eat unleavened bread.
12.19: For seven days leaven is not to be found in your houses. Indeed, any person who eats anything leavened, is to be cut off from the congregation of Israel, whether an alien or a native of the land.
12.20: You are not to eat what is leavened. You are to eat unleavened bread in all your settlements.'"19
12.21: Then Moses summoned all the elders of Israel and said to them, "Choose sheep for your families, and slaughter the Passover lamb.
12.22: Take a bundle of hyssop and dip it in the blood that is in the basin, and apply some of the blood in the basin to the lintel and the two doorposts. None of you is to go out of the doorway of his house until morning,
12.23: because the Lord will pass through to strike down the Egyptians, and when he sees the blood on the lintel and the two doorposts, the Lord will pass over the doorway, and won't allow the destroyer to enter your houses to strike you down.
12.24: You are to observe this event as a perpetual ordinance for you and your children forever.
12.25: When you enter the land that the Lord will give you, just as he promised, you are to observe this ritual.
12.26: And when your children say to you, 'What does this ritual mean?'
12.27: you are to say, 'It is the Passover sacrifice to the Lord, who passed over the houses of the Israelites in Egypt when he struck down the Egyptians but spared our houses.'" Then the people bowed down and worshiped.
12.28: The Israelites did this. Moses and Aaron did just what the Lord had commanded.21
12.29: And so at midnight the Lord struck down every firstborn in the land of Egypt, from the firstborn of Pharaoh who sat on his throne to the firstborn of the prisoner who was in the dungeon, and all the firstborn of the livestock.
12.30: Pharaoh got up during the night, he, all his officials, and all the Egyptians, and there was loud wailing in Egypt, for there was not a house without someone dead in it.
12.31: Then he summoned Moses and Aaron during the night and told them： "Get up, go out from among my people, both you and the Israelites! Go, serve the Lord as you have said.
12.32: Take both your sheep and your cattle, just as you demanded and go! And bless me too!"31
12.33: The Egyptian officials urged the people to send them out of the land quickly, because they were saying, "We'll all be dead!"
12.34: So the people took their dough before it was leavened, with their kneading bowls wrapped up in their cloaks on their shoulders.
12.35: Meanwhile, the Israelites had done as Moses said; they had asked the Egyptians for objects of silver and objects of gold, and for clothes.
12.36: The Lord had given the people favor in the eyes of the Egyptians, so that they gave them what they requested; and so they plundered the Egyptians.34
12.37: About 600,000 Israelite men traveled from Rameses to Succoth on foot, not counting children.
12.38: A mixed multitude also went up with them, along with a very large number of livestock, including sheep and cattle.
12.39: They baked the dough that they brought out of Egypt into thin cakes of unleavened bread. It had not been leavened because they were driven out of Egypt and could not wait, nor had they prepared provisions for themselves.
12.40: Now the time that the Israelites lived in Egypt was 430 years.
12.41: At the end of 430 years, to the very day, all the tribal divisions of the Lord went out from the land of Egypt.
12.42: That was for the Lord a night of vigil to bring them out of the land of Egypt. This same night belongs to the Lord, and is to be a vigil for all the Israelites from generation to generation.
12.43: The Lord said to Moses and Aaron, "These are the regulations for the Passover： No foreigner is to eat it,
12.44: though any slave purchased with money may eat it after you have circumcised him.
12.45: But no temporary resident or a hired servant is to eat it.
12.46: It is to be eaten in one house, and you are not to take any of the meat outside the house, nor shall you break any of its bones.
12.47: The whole congregation of Israel shall observe it.
12.48: If an alien who resides with you wants to observe the Passover to the Lord, every male in his household must be circumcised, and then he may come near to observe it. He shall be like a native of the land, but no uncircumcised person shall eat it.
12.49: A single law exists for the native and the alien who resides among you."
12.50: All the Israelites did this. They did exactly as the Lord commanded Moses and Aaron.
12.51: And on that very day, the Lord brought the Israelites out of the land of Egypt by their tribal divisions.

13.1: The Lord spoke to Moses,
13.2: Exod 13:21The Lord went in front of them by day in a pillar of cloud to lead them along the way, and by night in a pillar of fire to give them light, so they could travel both day and night.
13.3: Then Moses said to the people, "Remember this day on which you came out of Egypt, from the house of bondage, because the Lord brought you out from this place with a strong show of force. Moreover, nothing leavened shall be eaten.
13.4: Today, in the month of Abib, you are going out.
13.5: When the Lord brings you to the land of the Canaanite, the Hittite, the Amorite, the Hivite, and the Jebusite, which he swore to your ancestors to give you - a land flowing with milk and honey - you are to observe this ritual in this month.
13.6: You are to eat unleavened bread for seven days, and on the seventh day there shall be a festival to the Lord.
13.7: Unleavened bread shall be eaten for seven days, and nothing leavened shall be seen among you, nor is leaven to be seen among you throughout your territory.
13.8: And you are to tell your child on that day, 'This is because of what the Lord did for me when I came out of Egypt.'
13.9: It shall be a sign for you on your hand and a reminder on your forehead, so that you may speak about the instruction of the Lord; for the Lord brought you out of Egypt with a strong show of force.
13.10: You are to keep this ordinance at its appointed time from year to year."8
13.11: "When the Lord brings you to the land of the Canaanite and gives it to you, just as he promised you and your ancestors,
13.12: you are to dedicate to the Lord everything that first opens the womb. All the firstborn males of your livestock belong to the Lord.
13.13: You are to redeem every firstborn donkey with a lamb, and if you don't redeem it, you are to break its neck. You are to redeem every firstborn among your sons.
13.14: "Then when your child asks you in the future, 'What is this?', you are to say to him, 'The Lord brought us out of Egypt, from the house of bondage with a strong show of force.
13.15: And when Pharaoh stubbornly refused to let us go, the Lord killed every firstborn in the land of Egypt, from the firstborn of humans to the firstborn of animals. Therefore, I sacrifice to the Lord every male that first opens the womb, but I redeem every firstborn of my sons.'
13.16: It shall be a sign on your hand and an emblem on your forehead, because the Lord brought us out of Egypt with a strong show of force."
13.17: When Pharaoh let the people go, God did not lead them along the road through the land of the Philistines, even though it was nearer, because God had said, "If the people face war, they may change their minds and return to Egypt."
13.18: So God led the people the round about way of the desert toward the Reed Sea. The Israelites went up from the land of Egypt in military formation.
13.19: Moses took the bones of Joseph with him, because Joseph had made the Israelites take this solemn oath： "God will certainly take notice of you, and then you must carry my bones up with you from here."
13.20: They left Succoth and camped in Etham at the edge of the desert.
13.22: Neither the pillar of cloud by day nor the pillar of fire by night left its place in front of the people.

14.1: The Lord said to Moses,
14.2: "Tell the Israelites that they are to turn back and camp in front of Pi-hahiroth, between Migdol and the sea. You are to camp in front of Baal-zephon, opposite it by the sea.
14.3: Pharaoh will say about the Israelites, 'They're wandering aimlessly in the land, and the desert has closed in on them.'
14.4: I've made Pharaoh's heart stubborn so he will pursue them. And I'll receive honor by means of Pharaoh and his army, so that the Egyptians will know that I am the Lord." So this is what the Israelites did.
14.5: W hen the king of Egypt was told that the people had fled, the minds of Pharaoh and his officials changed toward the people, and they said, "What have we done in releasing Israel from serving us?"
14.6: S o Pharaoh had his chariot prepared and took his troops with him.
14.7: He took 600 of the best chariots, and all the other chariots of Egypt with officers in charge of each one.
14.8: The Lord made the heart of Pharaoh king of Egypt stubborn, and he defiantly pursued the Israelites as they were leaving.
14.9: The Egyptians pursued them - all the chariot-horses of Pharaoh, along with his horsemen and army - and they overtook them camped by the sea, near Pi-hahiroth, in front of Baal Zephon.
14.10: As Pharaoh approached, the Israelites looked up, and there were the Egyptians bearing down on them! Extremely frightened, the Israelites cried out to the Lord.
14.11: They also said to Moses, "Was it because there were no graves in Egypt that you took us out to die in the desert -  What have you done to us, by bringing us out of Egypt -
14.12: Is this not what we told you in Egypt, when we said, 'Leave us alone!' and 'Let us serve the Egyptians?' Indeed, it would have been better for us to serve the Egyptians than to die in the desert!"
14.13: Moses said to the people, "Don't be afraid! Stand still and watch how the Lord will deliver you today, because you will never again see the Egyptians whom you're looking at today.
14.14: The Lord will fight for you while you keep still."
14.15: Then the Lord said to Moses, "Why are you crying out to me -  Tell the Israelites to move out!
14.16: You are to raise your staff, stretch out your hand over the sea, and divide it, so the Israelites may go into the middle of the sea on dry land.
14.17: Even now I'm hardening the heart of the Egyptians so they'll go after the Israelites. Then I'll receive honor by means of Pharaoh and all his army, his chariots and his horsemen.
14.18: Then the Egyptians will know that I am the Lord when I receive honor by means of Pharaoh, his chariots, and his horsemen."17
14.19: Then the angel of God, who was going in front of the camp of Israel, moved behind them. The pillar of cloud also moved from in front of them and stood behind them,
14.20: coming between the camp of the Egyptians and the camp of Israel. The cloud remained there even in the darkness, illuminating the night, but the one side did not come near the other all night.19
14.21: Then Moses stretched out his hand over the sea, and the Lord caused the water to retreat by a strong east wind all night, turning the sea into dry land. As the waters were divided,
14.22: the Israelites went into the middle of the sea on dry land, and the waters formed a wall for them on their right and on their left.
14.23: The Egyptians pursued - all the horses of Pharaoh, his chariots, and his horsemen - and they went into the middle of the sea after them.
14.24: In the morning watch, the Lord looked down on the Egyptian camp through the pillar of fire and cloud, and he threw the Egyptian camp into confusion.
14.25: He made the wheels of their chariots wobble so that they drove them with difficulty. The Egyptians said, "Let's flee from Israel because the Lord is fighting for them and against us."
14.26: Then the Lord said to Moses, "Stretch out your hand over the sea and the waters will come back over the Egyptians, over their chariots, and over their horsemen."
14.27: Moses stretched out his hand over the sea, and the water returned to its normal depth at daybreak. The Egyptians tried to retreat in front of the advancing water, but the Lord destroyed the Egyptians in the middle of the sea.
14.28: The waters returned, covering the chariots and the horsemen of Pharaoh's entire army that had pursued the Israelites into the sea. Not a single one of them remained.
14.29: But the Israelites walked through the middle of the sea on dry land, and the waters stood like a wall for them on their right and on their left.14
14.30: On that day the Lord delivered Israel from the hand of the Egyptians, and Israel saw the Egyptians dead along the seashore.
14.31: When Israel saw the great force by which the Lord had acted against the Egyptians, the people feared the Lord, and they believed the Lord and Moses his servant.

15.1: Then Moses and the Israelites sang this song to the Lord： "I'll sing to the Lord, for he is highly exalted. The horse and its rider he has thrown into the sea.
15.2: The Lord is my strength and song, and he has become my salvation. This is my God and I'll praise him, the God of my father and I'll exalt him.
15.3: The Lord is a man of war, the Lord is his name!
15.4: "Pharaoh's chariots and his army he has hurled into the sea; his best officers sank in the Reed Sea.
15.5: The deep covered them, they went down into the depths like a rock.
15.6: Your right hand, Lord, was majestic in strength, your right hand, Lord, shattered the enemy.
15.7: In the greatness of your majesty you broke down your enemies. You sent forth your anger, it consumed them like stubble.
15.8: By the breath of your nostrils the waters were piled up, the flowing waters stood up like a hill, the deep waters congealed in the heart of the sea.
15.9: The enemy said, "I'll pursue them, I'll overtake them, I'll divide the spoil. I'll satisfy my anger on them, I'll draw my sword, and my hand will bring them to ruin.
15.10: You blew with your breath, and the sea covered them; they sank like lead in the mighty waters.
15.11: "Who is like you among the gods, Lord -  Who is like you, majestic in holiness, awesome in splendor, and working wonders -
15.12: You stretched out your right hand, and the earth swallowed them.
15.13: "You have led with your gracious love this people whom you redeemed. You have guided them with your strength to your holy dwelling.
15.14: The people heard and they quaked, anguish has seized the inhabitants of Philistia.
15.15: Then the chiefs of Edom were terrified, the nobles of Moab trembled uncontrollably, and all the inhabitants of Canaan melted away.
15.16: Dread and fear have fallen on them, because of the strength of your arm. They have become silent as a stone, until your people pass by, Lord, until this people you acquired pass by.
15.17: You will bring them in and plant them on the mountain of your inheritance. You have made a place where you will reside, Lord. Your own hands have established a sanctuary, Lord.
15.18: The Lord will reign forever and ever."
15.19: When the horses of Pharaoh, his chariots, and his horsemen went into the sea, the Lord caused the waters of the sea to come back over them, but the Israelites walked through the middle of the sea on dry land.
15.20: Then Miriam the prophetess, Aaron's sister, took a tambourine in her hand and went out with all the women behind her with tambourines and dancing.
15.21: Miriam sang to them, "Sing to the Lord, for he is highly exalted! The horse and its rider he has thrown into the sea."
15.22: Then Moses led Israel from the Reed Sea and they went to the desert of Shur. They traveled into the desert for three days and did not find water.
15.23: When they came to Marah, they could not drink the water at Marah because it was bitter. (That is why it's called Marah.)
15.24: Then the people complained against Moses： "What are we to drink?"
15.25: Moses cried out to the Lord, and the Lord showed him a tree, which he threw into the water, and the water became sweet. There the Lord presented to them a statute and an ordinance, and there he tested them.
15.26: He said, "If you will carefully obey the Lord your God, do what is right in his eyes, listen to his commandments, and keep all his statutes, then I won't inflict on you all the diseases that I inflicted on the Egyptians, because I am the Lord your healer."
15.27: Then they came to Elim where there were twelve springs of water and 70 palm trees, and they camped there by the water.15

16.1: Later, they left Elim, and the whole congregation of the Israelites came to the desert of Sin, which lay between Elim and Sinai, on the fifteenth day of the second month after their departure from the land of Egypt.
16.2: The whole congregation of the Israelites complained against Moses and Aaron in the desert.
16.3: The Israelites said to them, "If only we had died by the Lord's hand in the land of Egypt when we sat by the cooking pots, when we ate bread until we were filled - because you brought us to this desert to kill this entire congregation with hunger."
16.4: The Lord said to Moses, "Listen very carefully! I'll cause food to rain down for you from heaven, and the people are to go out and gather each day's portion on that day. In this way I'll test them to demonstrate whether or not they'll live according to my instructions.
16.5: On the sixth day, when they prepare what they bring in, it will be double what they gather on other days."
16.6: So Moses and Aaron addressed the entire congregation of the Israelites, "This evening you will know that the Lord has brought you out of the land of Egypt,
16.7: and in the morning you will see the glory of the Lord, for he has heard your complaints against him. After all, who are we that you complain against us?"
16.8: Moses also said, "When the Lord gives you meat to eat in the evening, and bread in the morning to satisfy you, the Lord will hear your complaints directed against him. Who are we -  Your complaints aren't against us, but rather against the Lord."
16.9: Then Moses instructed Aaron, "Say to the whole congregation of the Israelites, 'Come near into the Lord's presence, because he has heard your complaints.'"
16.10: While Aaron was speaking to all the congregation of the Israelites, they turned toward the desert, and there the glory of the Lord was seen in the cloud.
16.11: The Lord said to Moses,
16.12: "I've heard the complaints of the Israelites. Tell them, 'At twilight you are to eat meat and in the morning you are to be filled with bread, so you may know that I am the Lord your God.'"
16.13: Later that evening quail came up and covered the camp, and then in the morning there was a layer of dew around the camp.
16.14: When the layer of dew evaporated, on the surface of the desert a fine flaky substance, as fine as frost, appeared on the ground.
16.15: When the Israelites saw it, they asked one another, "What is it?", because they did not know what it was. Moses told them, "It's the food that the Lord has given you to eat.
16.16: "This is what the Lord has commanded： 'You are to gather from it what each person is to eat, about two quarts per person according to the number of your people, and one person is to gather for everyone in his tent.'"
16.17: The Israelites did this, some gathering much, some little.
16.18: When they measured it with two quart vessels, the one who gathered much did not have an excess, while the one who gathered little did not lack. They gathered exactly what each needed to eat.
16.19: Then Moses said to them, "No one is to leave any of it until morning."
16.20: But they did not listen to Moses - some people left part of it until morning, and it produced maggots and smelled bad, so Moses got angry at them.
16.21: Every morning they gathered it, according to what each needed to eat; and when the sun became hot, it melted.20
16.22: On the sixth day they gathered twice as much bread, about four quarts per person. Then all the leaders of the congregation came and reported to Moses,
16.23: and he told them, "This is what the Lord said： 'Tomorrow is a Sabbath observance, a holy Sabbath to the Lord. Bake what you want to bake and boil what you want to boil, and put aside whatever remains to be kept for yourselves until morning.'"
16.24: They put it away until morning, as Moses commanded, and it did not smell bad, and there were no maggots in it.
16.25: Moses said, "Eat it today, since today is a Sabbath to the Lord, and today you won't find it in the field.
16.26: For six days you are to gather it, but on the seventh day, the Sabbath, there won't be any."25
16.27: Nevertheless, that seventh day some of the people went out to gather, but they did not find any.
16.28: Then the Lord said to Moses, "How long will you people refuse to keep my commandments and my instructions -
16.29: You see that the Lord has given you the Sabbath, and so on the sixth day he gives you food for two days. Let each person stay where he is; let no one leave his place on the seventh day."
16.30: So the people rested on the seventh day.16
16.31: The Israelites named it "manna." It was white like coriander seed, and tasted like a wafer made with honey.
16.32: Moses said, "This is what the Lord has commanded： 'Set aside about two quarts of it for future generations, so that they may see the food with which I fed you in the desert when I brought you out of the land of Egypt.'"
16.33: Then Moses said to Aaron, "Take a jar, fill it with about two quarts of manna, and place it in the Lord's presence, to be preserved throughout future generations."
16.34: So Aaron placed it before the Testimony to be kept, just as the Lord had commanded Moses.
16.35: The Israelites ate manna for 40 years until they came to a land where they could settle. They ate manna until they came to the border of the land of Canaan.
16.36: Now two quarts is a tenth of an ephah.

17.1: The whole congregation of the Israelites set out from the desert of Sin, traveling from place to place according to the command of the Lord. They camped at Rephidim, but there was no water for the people to drink.
17.2: The people quarreled with Moses： "Give us water to drink." M oses said to them, "Why are you quarreling with me -  Why are you testing the Lord?"
17.3: But the people were thirsty there for water and they complained against Moses： "Why did you bring us up from Egypt to kill us, our children, and our livestock with thirst?"
17.4: So Moses cried out to the Lord： "What shall I do with these people -  Just a little more and they'll stone me."
17.5: Then the Lord said to Moses, "Go over in front of the people and take some of the elders of Israel with you. Take in your hand the staff with which you struck the Nile, and go.
17.6: I'll be standing there in front of you on the rock at Horeb. You are to strike the rock and water will come out of it, so the people can drink." Moses did this in front of the elders of Israel.
17.7: He named the place Massah and Meribah, because the Israelites quarreled and tested the Lord by saying： "Is the Lord really among us or not?"
17.8: Then the Amalekites came and fought with the Israelites at Rephidim.
17.9: Moses said to Joshua, "Choose some men for us and go out to fight against the Amalekites. Tomorrow I'll stand on top of the hill with the staff of God in my hand."
17.10: So Joshua did as Moses told him and fought against the Amalekites, while Moses, Aaron, and Hur went up to the top of the hill.
17.11: Whenever Moses raised his hand, the Israelites prevailed, but when his hand remained at his side, then the Amalekites prevailed.
17.12: When Moses' hands became heavy, they took a stone and put it under him, and he sat on it. Aaron and Hur supported his hands, one on one side and one on the other, and so his hands were steady until the sun went down.
17.13: Joshua defeated Amalek and his army with the sword.12
17.14: Then the Lord said to Moses, "Write this in a book as a memorial and recite it to Joshua： 'I'll certainly wipe out the memory of the Amalekites from under heaven.'"
17.15: Moses built an altar and named it "The Lord Is My Banner."
17.16: He said, "Because a fist has been raised in defiance against the throne of the Lord, the Lord will wage war against Amalek from generation to generation."

18.1: Jethro, the priest of Midian, Moses' father-in-law, heard all that God had done for Moses and for his people Israel, and how the Lord had brought Israel out of Egypt.
18.2: Now Jethro, Moses' father-in-law, had taken back Moses' wife Zipporah after she had been sent away,
18.3: along with her two sons. The name of the one was Gershom, for he said, "I was an alien in a foreign land,"
18.4: while the name of the other was Eliezer, for he said, "My father's God helped me and delivered me from Pharaoh's sword."1
18.5: Moses' father-in-law Jethro, together with Moses' two sons and his wife, came to Moses in the desert where he was camped at the mountain of God.
18.6: He said to Moses, "I, your father-in-law Jethro, am coming to you along with your wife and her two sons."
18.7: When Moses went out to meet his father-in-law, he bowed low and kissed him, and they greeted one another. Then they went into the tent.
18.8: Moses told his father-in-law all that the Lord had done to Pharaoh and to the Egyptians on Israel's behalf, all the hardships that they had encountered along the way, and how the Lord had delivered them.
18.9: Jethro rejoiced over all the good that the Lord had done for Israel in delivering them from the hand of the Egyptians.
18.10: Jethro said, "Blessed be the Lord who delivered you from the hand of the Egyptians and from the hand of Pharaoh, and who delivered the people from the oppression of the Egyptians.
18.11: Now I know that the Lord is greater than all other gods, because of what happened to the Egyptians when they acted arrogantly against Israel."
18.12: Jethro, Moses' father-in-law, brought a burnt offering and sacrifices for God, and Aaron and all the elders of Israel came to dine with Moses' father-in-law in the presence of God.
18.13: The next day Moses sat down to judge the people, and the people stood around Moses from morning until evening.
18.14: When Moses' father-in-law saw all that he was doing for the people, he said, "What is this that you are doing for the people -  Why do you alone sit as judge, with all the people standing around you from morning until evening?"
18.15: Moses said to his father-in-law, "Because the people come to me to seek God's will.
18.16: When they have a dispute, it comes to me and I decide between a person and his neighbor, and make known the statutes of God and his instructions."
18.17: Moses' father-in-law said to him, "What you are doing is not good.
18.18: You will surely wear yourself out, both you and these people who are with you, because the task is too heavy for you. You cannot do it by yourself.
18.19: Now listen to me. I'll advise you, and may God be with you. You are to represent the people before God and shall bring the disputes to God.
18.20: You are to teach them the statutes and instructions and make known to them the way they're to go and the things they're to do.
18.21: "You are to look for capable men among the people, men who fear God, men of integrity who hate dishonest gain. You are to set these men over them as officials over thousands, hundreds, fifties, and tens.
18.22: They shall judge the people at all times. Let them bring every major matter to you, but let them judge every minor matter. It will lighten your burden, and they'll bear it with you.
18.23: If you do this, and God so commands you, you will be able to stand the strain, and all these people will also go to their homes in peace."
18.24: Moses listened to his father-in-law and did everything he said.
18.25: Moses chose capable men from all Israel and appointed them as heads over the people, as officials over thousands, hundreds, fifties, and tens.
18.26: They judged the people at all times; the difficult matters they brought to Moses, but every minor matter they judged.
18.27: Moses sent his father-in-law on his way, and he went to his own land.1826

19.1: On the third new moon after the Israelites went out of the land of Egypt, on that very day, they came to the desert of Sinai.
19.2: They had set out from Rephidim and arrived at the desert of Sinai where they camped in the desert. Israel camped there in front of the mountain.
19.3: Then Moses went up to God, and the Lord called to him from the mountain： "This is what you are to say to the house of Jacob and declare to the sons of Israel,
19.4: 'You saw what I did to the Egyptians, and how I carried you on eagles' wings and brought you to myself.
19.5: And now if you carefully obey me and keep my covenant, you are to be my special possession out of all the peoples. For the whole earth belongs to me,
19.6: but you are to be a kingdom of priests and a holy nation to me.' These are the words you are to declare to the Israelites."
19.7: When Moses came, he summoned the elders of the people and told them everything that the Lord had commanded him.
19.8: All the people answered together： "All that the Lord has said, we will do!" Then Moses reported all the words of the people back to the Lord.
19.9: The Lord said to Moses, "Look, I'm coming to you in a thick cloud, so that the people may hear when I speak with you and believe you always." Moses reported the words of the people to the Lord.
19.10: The Lord said to Moses, "Go to the people and consecrate them today and tomorrow. They must wash their clothes,
19.11: and be ready for the third day, for on the third day the Lord will come down on Mount Sinai in the sight of all the people.
19.12: You are to set boundaries for the people all around： 'Be very careful that you don't go up on the mountain or touch the side of it. Anyone who touches the mountain will surely be put to death.
19.13: No hand shall touch that person, but he shall surely be stoned or shot with arrows; whether animal or person, he shall not live.' They shall approach the mountain only when the ram's horn sounds a long blast."19
19.14: When Moses went down from the mountain to the people, he consecrated the people, and they washed their clothes.
19.15: He said to the people, "Be ready for the third day; don't go near a woman."
19.16: When morning came on the third day, there was thunder and lightning with a heavy cloud over the mountain and the very loud sound of a ram's horn. All the people in the camp trembled.
19.17: Moses brought the people from the camp to meet God, and they stood at the base of the mountain.
19.18: Mount Sinai was completely enveloped in smoke because the Lord had come down in fire on it. Smoke went up from it like smoke from a kiln, and the whole mountain shook violently.
19.19: As the sound of the ram's horn grew louder and louder, Moses would speak and God would answer with thunder.
19.20: When the Lord came down on Mount Sinai to the top of the mountain, he summoned Moses to the top of the mountain, and Moses went up.
19.21: The Lord said to Moses, "Go down and warn the people so they don't break through to look at the Lord, and many of them perish.
19.22: Even the priests who approach the Lord must consecrate themselves. Otherwise, the Lord will attack them."
19.23: Moses said to the Lord, "The people cannot come up to Mount Sinai because you warned us： 'Set boundaries around the mountain and consecrate it.'"
19.24: The Lord said to him, "Go down, and come back up with Aaron, but the priests and the people must not break through to go up to the Lord. Otherwise, he will attack them.
19.25: So Moses went down to the people and spoke to them.

20.1: Then God spoke all these words:
20.2: "I am the Lord your God who brought you out of the land of Egypt, out of the house of slavery. 3"You are to have no other gods besides me.
20.4: "You are not to make for yourselves an idol, or any likeness of what is in heaven above, or on earth below, or in the water under the earth.
20.5: You are not to bow down to them in worship or serve them; because I, the Lord your God, am a jealous God, punishing the children for the iniquity of the parents, to the third and fourth generations of those who hate me,
20.6: but showing gracious love to thousands who love me and keep my commandments.
20.7: "You are not to misuse the name of the Lord your God, because the Lord will not leave the one who misuses his name unpunished.
20.8: "Remember to keep the Sabbath day holy.
20.9: Six days you are to labor and do all your work,
20.10: but the seventh day is a Sabbath to the Lord your God. You are not to do any work, neither you, nor your son, nor your daughter, nor your male or female servant, nor your livestock, nor the alien who is within your gates,
20.11: because the Lord   made the heavens and the earth and the sea, and all that is in them, in six days, then he rested on the seventh day. Therefore, the Lord blessed the Sabbath day and made it holy.
20.12: "Honor your father and your mother so that you may live long in the land which the Lord your God is giving you.
20.13: "You are not to murder.
20.14: "You are not to commit adultery.
20.15: "You are not to steal.
20.16: "You are not to testify falsely against your neighbor.
20.17: "You are not to covet your neighbor's house. You are not to covet your neighbor's wife, or his male or female servant, or his ox, or his donkey, or anything that belongs to your neighbor."
20.18: All the people experienced the thunder and lightning, the sound of the ram's horn, and the smoking mountain. And as the people experienced it, they trembled and stood at a distance.
20.19: They said to Moses, "You speak to us and we will listen, but don't let God speak with us, or we may die.
20.20: Moses said to the people, "Don't be afraid, for God has come to test you, so that you may fear him in order that you don't sin."
20.21: Then the people stood at a distance, and Moses approached the thick cloud where God was.2020
20.22: The Lord said to Moses, "This is what you are to say to the Israelites, 'You have seen for yourselves that I spoke to you from heaven.
20.23: You are not to make gods of silver alongside me, nor shall you make for yourselves gods of gold.
20.24: 'You are to make an altar of earth for me, and you are to sacrifice on it your burnt offerings and peace offerings, your sheep, and your cattle. Everywhere I cause my name to be remembered, I'll come to you and bless you.
20.25: If you make an altar of stone for me, you must not build it of cut stones, because if you strike it with your chisel, you will profane it.
20.26: You are not to ascend to my altar on steps, so that your nakedness may not be exposed on it.'

21.1: "These are the ordinances that you are to set before them.
21.2: "When you acquire a Hebrew servant he shall serve for six years, and in the seventh he shall go out a free man without paying anything.
21.3: If he came in by himself, he shall go out by himself. If he was married, his wife shall go out with him.
21.4: If his master gives him a wife and she bears him sons or daughters, the wife and children belong to her master, and he shall go out by himself.
21.5: "But if the servant, in fact, says, 'I love my master, my wife, and my children and I won't go out a free man,'
21.6: then his master shall bring him before the judges and he shall bring him to the door or the doorpost. His master shall pierce his ear with an awl, and he shall serve him permanently.
21.7: "When a man sells his daughter as a servant, she won't go out as the male servants do.
21.8: If she's displeasing to her master who selected her for himself, he must let her be redeemed. He does not have the right to sell her to foreign people because he has dealt unfairly with her.
21.9: If he has selected her for his son, he shall treat her according to the ordinance for daughters.
21.10: If he takes another woman for himself, he may not withhold from the first her food, her clothing, or her marital rights.
21.11: If he does not do these three things for her, she may go out without paying anything at all.
21.12: "Whoever strikes a man so that he dies, shall surely be put to death.
21.13: If he didn't lie in wait, but God let him fall into his control, then I'll appoint for you a place to which he may flee.
21.14: If a man acts deliberately against his neighbor to kill him by treachery, you are to take him to die even if he's at my altar.
21.15: "Whoever strikes his father or his mother shall surely be put to death.
21.16: "Whoever kidnaps a person, whether he has sold him or whether the victim is still in his possession, shall surely be put to death.
21.17: "Whoever curses his father or his mother shall surely be put to death.
21.18: "If people quarrel and one strikes the other with a rock or his fist, and he does not die but ends up in bed,
21.19: and the injured person then gets up and walks around outside with the help of his staff, the one who struck him is not liable, except that he shall compensate him for his loss of time and take care of his complete recovery.
21.20: "If a man strikes his male or female servant with a stick and he or she dies as a direct result, the master must be punished.
21.21: But if the servant survives a day or two, the master is not to be punished because the servant is his property.
21.22: "If two men are fighting and they strike a pregnant woman and her children are born prematurely but there is no harm, he shall surely be fined as the husband of the woman demands of him, and he will pay as the court decides.
21.23: If there is harm, then you are to require life for life,
21.24: eye for eye, tooth for tooth, hand for hand, foot for foot, 25burn for burn, wound for wound, bruise for bruise.22
21.26: "If a man strikes the eye of his male or female servant and destroys it, he shall release him as a free man in exchange for his eye.
21.27: If he knocks out the tooth of his male or female servant, he shall release him as a free man in exchange for his tooth.26
21.28: "If an ox gores a man or woman so that they die, the ox shall surely be stoned and its flesh may not be eaten, but the owner of the ox is free from liability.
21.29: But if the ox has gored previously, and its owner has been warned about it but didn't restrain it, and it kills a man or woman, the ox shall be stoned and its owner shall also be put to death.
21.30: If a fine is imposed on him, he may pay all that was imposed on him as a ransom for his life.
21.31: This same ordinance applies if it gores a son or daughter.
21.32: "If the ox gores a male or female servant, the owner shall give 30 shekels of silver to the servant's master, and the ox shall be stoned.
21.33: If a man opens a pit or digs a pit and does not cover it, and an ox or donkey falls into it,
21.34: the owner of the pit shall make restitution. He shall pay money to its owner, and the dead animal will become his.2133
21.35: "If a man's ox strikes his neighbor's ox and it dies, they are to sell the live ox and divide the money. They shall also divide the dead animal.
21.36: But if it was known that the ox had gored previously, and its owner didn't restrain it, he shall surely repay ox for ox, and the dead ox will become his.3521

22.1: "If a man steals an ox or sheep and slaughters it or sells it, he shall repay five oxen for the ox and four sheep for the sheep.
22.2: "If a thief is found while breaking into a house, and is struck down and dies, it is not a capital crime in that case,
22.3: but if the sun has risen on him, then it is a capital crime in that case. "A thief shall surely make restitution, but if he has nothing, he shall be sold for his theft.
22.4: If what was stolen is actually found alive in his possession, whether an ox, a donkey or a sheep, he shall repay double.
22.5: "When a man lets a field or vineyard be grazed over or releases his livestock so that they graze in another man's field, he shall make restitution from the best of his field or vineyard.
22.6: "When a fire breaks out and spreads into thorn bushes and consumes stacked grain or standing grain or the field, the one who started the fire shall surely make restitution.
22.7: "When a man gives his neighbor money or goods for safekeeping and it's stolen from the neighbor's house, the thief, if found, shall repay double.
22.8: If the thief is not found, the owner of the house shall appear before the judges to see whether or not the thief took his neighbor's property.
22.9: "In every ownership dispute involving an ox, donkey, sheep, garment, or anything that is lost where a person says, 'This is mine,' the case between the two of them shall come before the judges, and the one that the judges declare guilty shall repay double to his neighbor.
22.10: "When a man gives a donkey or ox or sheep or any animal to his neighbor for safe keeping, and it dies or is injured or is driven away when no one is looking,
22.11: the two of them shall take an oath in the Lord's presence that the accused has not taken his neighbor's property. Its owner shall accept this, and the neighbor shall not make restitution.
22.12: But if it was actually stolen from him, the neighbor shall make restitution to its owner.
22.13: If it was torn to pieces, let the neighbor bring the remains as evidence, and he shall not make restitution for what was torn apart.
22.14: "When a man borrows an animal from his neighbor, and it's injured or dies while its owner was not with it, he shall surely make restitution.
22.15: If its owner was with it, he shall not make restitution. If it was hired, its fee covers the loss."
22.16: "When a man seduces a virgin who is not engaged to be married and lies with her, he must pay her bride-price, and she becomes his wife.
22.17: If her father absolutely refuses to give her to him, he shall pay an amount equal to the bride-price for virgins.
22.18: "You are not to allow a sorceress to live.
22.19: "Whoever has sexual relations with an animal shall surely be put to death.
22.20: "Anyone who sacrifices to a god, except the Lord alone, shall be utterly destroyed.
22.21: "You are not to wrong or oppress an alien, because you were aliens in the land of Egypt.
22.22: You are not to mistreat any widow or orphan.
22.23: If you do mistreat them, they'll surely cry out to me, and I'll immediately hear their cry.
22.24: And I'll be angry and will kill you with the sword, and your wives shall become widows and your children orphans.
22.25: "If you loan money to my people, to the poor among you, don't be like a creditor to them and don't impose interest on them.
22.26: If you take your neighbor's coat as collateral, you are to return it to him by sunset,
22.27: for it's his only covering; it's his outer garment, for what else can he sleep in -  And when he cries out to me, I'll hear him, for I am gracious.22
22.28: "You are not to blaspheme God or curse a ruler of your people.
22.29: "You are not to hold back the fullness of your harvest and the outflow of your wine presses. You are to give to me the firstborn of your sons.
22.30: You are to do the same with your oxen and your sheep. They shall be with their mother for seven days and then on the eighth day you are to give them to me.2229
22.31: "You are to be people set apart for me. You are not to eat flesh torn apart in the field; you are to throw it to the dogs."

23.1: "You are not to spread a false report, nor shall you join forces with the wicked to be a malicious witness.
23.2: You are not to follow the majority in doing wrong, and you are not to testify in a lawsuit so as to follow the majority and pervert justice.
23.3: You are not to show partiality to a poor man in his lawsuit.
23.4: "If you come across your enemy's ox or donkey wandering off, you are to certainly return it to him.
23.5: If you see your enemy's donkey lying helpless under its load, you must not abandon it, rather, you are to surely return it to him.
23.6: "You are not to pervert justice for the poor among you in their lawsuits.
23.7: Stay far away from a false charge, and don't kill the innocent or the righteous, because I won't acquit the guilty.
23.8: You are not to take a bribe because a bribe blinds the clear-sighted and distorts the words of the righteous.
23.9: You are not to oppress the resident alien, because you were aliens in the land of Egypt.
23.10: "You are to sow your land and gather its crops for six years,
23.11: but you are to let it rest the seventh year, leaving it unplanted. The poor of your people may eat from it, and the wild animals may eat what they leave. You are to do the same with your vineyards and olive groves.
23.12: You are to do your work for six days, but on the seventh day you are to refrain from work so that your ox and donkey may rest, and so the son of your maidservant and the alien may be refreshed.
23.13: "Be careful about everything I've told you, and don't mention the name of other gods. Don't let them be heard in your mouth!
23.14: "Three times a year you are to celebrate a festival for me.
23.15: You are to observe the Festival of Unleavened Bread. As I commanded you, you are to eat unleavened bread for seven days at the appointed time in the month Abib, for in it you came out of Egypt. No one shall appear before me empty handed.
23.16: You are to observe the Festival of Harvest, celebrating the first fruits of your work in planting the field, and the Festival of Ingathering at the end of the year, when you gather the fruit of your work from the field.
23.17: Three times a year all your males shall appear in the presence of the Lord God."
23.18: "You are not to offer the blood of my sacrifice with anything leavened, and you are not to let the fat portion of my sacrifice remain overnight until morning.
23.19: "You are to bring the best of the first fruits of your soil to the house of the Lord your God. "You are not to boil a young goat in its mother's milk."
23.20: "Look, I'm sending an angel in front of you to guard you on the way and to bring you to the place I've prepared.
23.21: Be careful in his presence - be sure to obey him. Don't rebel against him, for he won't forgive your transgression, since my name is in him.
23.22: Indeed, if you carefully obey him and do everything that I say, then I'll be an enemy to your enemies and an adversary to your adversaries.
23.23: For my angel will go ahead of you and will bring you to the Amorites, the Hittites, the Perizzites, the Canaanites, the Hivites, and the Jebusites, and I'll annihilate them.
23.24: You are not to bow down to their gods or serve them. You are not to follow their practices, but you are to completely overthrow them and smash their sacred stones to pieces.
23.25: You are to serve the Lord your God, and he will bless your food and water, and I'll remove sickness from you.
23.26: No woman will miscarry or be barren in your land, and I'll make every day of your life complete.24
23.27: "I'll go ahead of you and terrorize all the people to whom you are coming. I'll confuse your enemies and make them turn their backs on you and run away.
23.28: I'll send hornets ahead of you and they'll drive out the Hivites, the Canaanites, and the Hittites before you.
23.29: I won't drive them out before you in a single year, so that the land does not become desolate and so that wild do not overrun you.
23.30: I'll drive them out ahead of you little by little until you increase in numbers and possess the land.2328
23.31: "I'll set your borders from the Reed Sea to the Sea of the Philistines, and from the desert to the Euphrates River, for I'll bring the inhabitants of the land under your control, and you are to drive them out ahead of you.
23.32: You are not to make a covenant with them or with their gods.
23.33: They shall not live in your land. Otherwise they will cause you to sin against me. If you worship their gods, it will become a snare for you."

24.1: The Lord said to Moses, "Come up to the Lord, you and Aaron, Nadab and Abihu, and seventy of the elders of Israel, and worship at a distance.
24.2: Only Moses shall approach the Lord, but the others shall not approach; the people shall not come up with him."
24.3: Then Moses came and reported all the words of the Lord and all the statutes to the people, and they all answered with one voice, "All the things that the Lord has spoken, we will do."
24.4: So Moses wrote down all the words of the Lord. He got up early in the morning and built an altar with twelve pillars for the twelve tribes of Israel at the bottom of the mountain.
24.5: He sent young Israelite men to offer up burnt offerings and sacrifice bulls as peace offerings to the Lord.
24.6: Moses took half the blood and put it in bowls, while he sprinkled the other half on the altar.
24.7: He took the Book of the Covenant and read it to the people. They said, "All that the Lord has spoken we will do and obey."
24.8: Moses took the blood, sprinkled it on the people, and said, "This is the blood of the covenant which the Lord made with you based on all these words."
24.9: Then Moses and Aaron, Nadab and Abihu and seventy of the elders of Israel went up
24.10: and saw the God of Israel. Under his feet was something like a pavement made of sapphire, and as clear as the sky.
24.11: Because God did not punish the Israelite leaders, they looked at God, yet lived to eat and drink.
24.12: Then the Lord said to Moses, "Go up to me on the mountain and stay there. I'll give you stone tablets with the instruction and law that I've written to teach the people."24
24.13: Moses got up with Joshua his servant, and went up on the mountain of God.
24.14: He said to the elders, "Wait here for us until we return to you. Look, Aaron and Hur are with you, and whoever has a dispute, let him come to them."
24.15: When Moses went up on the mountain, the cloud covered it.
24.16: The glory of the Lord settled on Mount Sinai, and the cloud covered it for six days. Then on the seventh day he called to Moses from within the cloud.
24.17: To the Israelites the appearance of the glory of the Lord was like a consuming fire on top of the mountain.
24.18: When Moses went up on the mountain, he went into the center of the cloud and was on the mountain for 40 days and 40 nights.1624

25.2: "Tell the Israelites to take an offering for me, and you are to accept my offering from every person whose heart moves him to give.
25.3: This is the offering that you are to accept from them： gold, silver, and bronze;
25.4: blue, purple, and scarlet material; fine linen and goat hair;
25.5: ram skins dyed red, dolphin skins, and acacia wood;
25.6: oil for lighting, spices for the anointing oil and for aromatic incense;
25.7: onyx stones, stones for setting on the ephod and the breast piece.
25.8: Let them make a sanctuary for me so I may dwell among them.
25.9: This is how you are to make it： according to all that I'm showing you, according to the pattern for the tabernacle and the pattern for all its furnishings."
25.10: "They shall make an ark of acacia wood, 45 inches long, 27 inches wide, and 27 inches high.
25.11: You are to overlay it with pure gold - you are to overlay it inside and outside - and you are to make a gold molding around it.
25.12: You are to cast four rings for it and put them on its four feet, two rings on one side of it and two rings on its other side.
25.13: You are to make poles of acacia wood and overlay them with gold.
25.14: You are to put the poles into the rings on the sides of the ark with which to carry it.
25.15: The poles are to remain in the rings of the ark and are not to be removed from it.
25.16: You are to put the Testimony that I will give you into the ark.14
25.17: "You are to make a Mercy Seat of pure gold, 45 inches long and 27 inches wide.
25.18: You are to make two cherubim of gold; you are to make them of hammered work at the two ends of the Mercy Seat.
25.19: Place one cherub at one end and one cherub at the other end. You are to make the cherubim at the two ends of the Mercy Seat, and of one piece with it.
25.20: The cherubim shall spread their wings upward, covering the Mercy Seat with their wings and facing each other. The faces of the cherubim shall be turned toward the Mercy Seat.
25.21: You are to put the Mercy Seat on top of the ark, and put the Testimony that I'll give you into the ark.
25.22: I'll meet with you there, and I'll tell you all my commandments for the Israelites from above the Mercy Seat, from between the two cherubim that are on the Ark of the Testimony.20
25.23: "You are to make a table of acacia wood, three feet long, a foot and a half wide, and 27 inches high.
25.24: You are to overlay it with pure gold, and put a gold molding around it.
25.25: You are to make a three-inch wide rim around it, and you are to make a gold molding around the rim.
25.26: You are to make four gold rings for it, and put the rings on the four corners where its four feet are.
25.27: The rings shall be close to the rim as holders for the poles to carry the table.
25.28: You are to make the poles of acacia wood, and overlay them with gold so the table can be carried with them.
25.29: You are to make its plates, dishes, jars, and bowls from which libations will be poured, and you are to make them of pure gold.
25.30: You are to put the bread of the Presence on the table before me continually."24
25.31: "You are to make a lamp stand of pure gold： the lamp stand and its base and stem shall be of hammered work, and its cups, calyxes, and flowers shall be of one piece with it.
25.32: Six branches shall extend from its sides, three branches of the lamp stand from one side of it and three branches of the lamp stand from its other side.
25.33: Three cups shaped like almond blossoms with calyxes and flowers shall be on one branch and three cups shaped like almond blossoms with calyxes and flowers shall be on the other branch, and so for the six branches extending from the lamp stand.3225
25.34: "On the lamp stand itself there shall be four cups shaped like almond blossoms with their calyxes and flowers.
25.35: A calyx shall be under the two branches that extend out of the stem; a calyx shall be under the next pair of branches that extend out of the stem; and a calyx shall be under the last pair of branches that extend out of the stem, and so for the six branches extending from the lamp stand.
25.36: Their calyxes and their branches shall be of one piece with it; all of it shall be made of one piece of hammered work of pure gold.
25.37: "You are to make seven lamps for it, and its lamps shall be mounted so as to give light in front of it.
25.38: Its tongs and trays shall be of pure gold.
25.39: The lamp stand and all its furnishings shall be made from a talent of pure gold.
25.40: Now see that you make them according to the pattern for them which you are being shown on the mountain."

26.1: "You are to make the tabernacle with ten curtains of fine woven linen and with blue, purple, and scarlet material. You are to make them with cherubim skillfully worked into them.
26.2: The length of each curtain shall be 42 feet, the width of each curtain six feet, and all the curtains shall have the same measurements.
26.3: "Five of the curtains shall be joined together, and the other five curtains shall be joined together.
26.4: You are to make loops of blue material along the edge of the outermost curtain in the first set, and likewise you are to make loops along the edge of the outermost curtain in the second set.
26.5: You are to make 50 loops in the one curtain, and you are to make 50 loops along the edge of the curtain that is in the second set, with the loops opposite each other.
26.6: Then you are to make 50 gold clasps, and join the curtains to each other with the clasps so that the tabernacle will be one piece.
26.7: "You are to make curtains of goat hair for a tent over the tabernacle. You are to make 11 curtains.
26.8: The length of each curtain shall be 45 feet, and the width of each curtain six feet; the measurements of each of the 11 curtains shall be the same.
26.9: You are to join five curtains by themselves, and six curtains by themselves, and you are to double over the sixth curtain at the front of the tent.
26.10: You are to make 50 loops along the edge of the outermost curtain in the first set, and 50 loops along the edge of the curtain of the other set.
26.11: You are to make 50 bronze clasps, put the clasps into the loops, and join the tent together so that it will be one piece.
26.12: As for the excess that remains of the curtains of the tent - the half curtain that remains - shall hang over the back of the tabernacle.
26.13: The nine inches that remain on either end of the length of the curtains of the tent shall hang over each side of the tabernacle to cover it.8
26.14: "You are to make a cover for the tabernacle of ram skins dyed red and a covering of dolphin skins above that.
26.15: "You are to make upright boards of acacia wood for the tabernacle.
26.16: Each board shall be fifteen feet long and 27 inches wide.
26.17: Each board shall have two pegs joined to one another, and you are to do this for all the boards of the tabernacle.
26.18: You are to make the boards for the tabernacle： twenty boards for the south side.
26.19: And you are to make 40 silver sockets under the twenty boards： two sockets under the one board for its two pegs and two sockets under the next board for its two pegs.15
26.20: "For the second side of the tabernacle to the north you are to make twenty boards
26.21: and 40 silver sockets for them, two sockets under one board and two sockets under the next board.
26.22: On the west you are to make six boards for the rear of the tabernacle,
26.23: and you are to make two boards for the rear corners of the tabernacle.
26.24: They shall be interlocked together at the bottom and connected on top by one ring. Do this for the two of them, and they are to be the two corners.
26.25: There shall be eight boards with their sixteen silver sockets, two sockets under one board and two sockets under the next board.
26.26: "You are to make bars of acacia wood, five for the boards on one side of the tabernacle,
26.27: five bars for the boards on the second side of the tabernacle, and five bars for the boards on the back side of the tabernacle to the west.
26.28: The center bar in the middle of the boards shall pass through from end to end.
26.29: You are to overlay the boards with gold, and you are to make gold rings for them as holders for the bars, and you are to overlay the bars with gold.
26.30: You are to erect the tabernacle according to the plan for it that was shown you on the mountain.
26.31: "You are to make a curtain of blue, purple, and scarlet material, and fine woven linen. You are to make it with cherubim skillfully worked into it.
26.32: You are to hang it on four pillars of acacia overlaid with gold, which have hooks of gold, and are set on four sockets of silver.
26.33: You are to hang the curtain from the clasps and bring the Ark of the Testimony there inside the curtain. The curtain shall separate for you the Holy Place from the Most Holy Place.
26.34: "You are to put the Mercy Seat on the Ark of the Testimony in the Most Holy Place.
26.35: You are to put the table outside the curtain. You are to put the table on the north side with the lamp stand opposite the table on the south side of the tabernacle.
26.36: For the doorway of the tent you are to make a screen of blue, purple, and scarlet material, and fine woven linen, the work of an embroiderer.
26.37: You are to make five pillars of acacia for the screens and overlay them with gold. Their hooks shall be of gold, and you are to cast five bronze sockets for them."

27.1: "You are to make the altar of acacia wood. It shall be seven and a half feet long, seven and a half feet wide; the altar shall be a square, and it shall be four and a half feet high.
27.2: You are to make horns on its four corners. Its corners shall be of one piece with it, and you are to overlay it with bronze.
27.3: You are to make pans for removing its ashes, shovels, bowls, forks, and fire-pans for it, and you are to make all its utensils of bronze.
27.4: You are to make a lattice, a netting of bronze for it, and you are to make four bronze rings on the netting at its four corners.
27.5: You are to put it under the ledge of the altar, so that the netting extends halfway up the altar.
27.6: You are to make poles for the altar, poles of acacia wood, and you are to overlay them with bronze.
27.7: The poles for it shall be put through the rings, so the poles will be on the two sides of the altar when it's carried.
27.8: You are to make it hollow out of boards - just as it was shown you on the mountain, so they are to make it."
27.9: "You are to make the court of the tabernacle. On the south side there shall be hangings of fine woven linen for the court, 150 feet long on one side.
27.10: It shall have twenty pillars, with twenty bronze sockets, and the hooks of the pillars and their bands shall be of silver.
27.11: Likewise for the length of the north side there shall be hangings 150 feet long, and it shall have twenty pillars with twenty bronze sockets, and the hooks of the pillars and their bands shall be of silver.10
27.12: "The width of the court on the west side shall have hangings 75 feet long with ten pillars and ten sockets.
27.13: The width of the court on the east side shall be 75 feet.
27.14: The hangings for the one section shall be twenty-two and a half feet, with their three pillars and three sockets.
27.15: "For the second section there shall be hangings of twenty-two and a half feet with their three pillars and three sockets.
27.16: There shall be a screen of 30 feet of blue, purple, and scarlet material and fine woven linen, the work of an embroiderer, for the gate of the court, and it shall have four pillars and four sockets.
27.17: All the pillars around the court shall be banded with silver. Their hooks shall be of silver and their sockets of bronze.
27.18: The length of the court shall be 150 feet, the width 75 feet, and the height seven and a half feet, with the hangings of fine woven linen, and the sockets of bronze.
27.19: All the utensils of the tabernacle for its service, all its pegs, and all the pegs for the court shall be of bronze."2716
27.20: "And you are to command the Israelites to bring you pure olive oil, extracted by hand, for the light in order to keep the lamp burning continually.
27.21: In the Tent of Meeting, outside the curtain that is before the Testimony, Aaron and his sons shall maintain the lamp stand from evening until morning in the Lord's presence. It shall be a perpetual ordinance from generation to generation among the Israelites."

28.1: "You are to bring your brother Aaron, along with his sons, from among the Israelites so they can serve as priests for me： Aaron and his sons, Nadab and Abihu, Eleazer and Ithamar.
28.2: You are to make holy garments for Aaron your brother, for dignity and beauty.
28.3: You are to speak to all who are skilled, whom I've endowed with talent, that they should make Aaron's garments for consecrating him to serve me as priest.
28.4: These are the garments that they are to make： a breast piece, an ephod, a robe, a checkered tunic, a turban, and a sash. They shall make holy garments for Aaron your brother and for his sons to serve me as priests.
28.5: They shall use gold, blue, purple, and scarlet material, as well as fine linen."3
28.6: "They shall make the ephod of gold, along with blue, purple, and scarlet material and fine woven linen, all of it skillfully worked.
28.7: It shall have two shoulder-pieces attached to its two edges so it can be joined together.
28.8: The skillfully woven band that is on it shall be made like it, of one piece with it： of gold, blue, purple, and scarlet material, and fine woven linen.
28.9: You are to take two onyx stones and engrave the names of the sons of Israel on them,
28.10: six of their names on one stone, and the six remaining names on the other stone. Engrave them according to their order of birth.
28.11: With work like a jeweler engraves on a signet, you are to inscribe the two stones with the names of the sons of Israel, and you are to mount them in settings of gold filigree.
28.12: You are to put the two stones on the shoulder pieces of the ephod as stones of remembrance for the sons of Israel, and Aaron shall carry their names into the Lord's presence on his two shoulders for remembrance.
28.13: You are to make settings of gold filigree,
28.14: and you are to make two chains of pure gold twisted like cords, and then fasten the twisted chains to the filigree settings."
28.15: "You are to make a breast piece to be worn by the high priest when he makes legal decisions. It shall be skillfully worked, made like the work of the ephod from gold, blue, purple, and scarlet material, and from fine woven linen.
28.16: It shall be square when folded double, nine inches long and nine inches wide.
28.17: You are to mount on it a setting for four rows of stones. The first row shall contain carnelian, topaz, and emerald;
28.18: the second row ruby, sapphire, and crystal;
28.19: the third row jacinth, agate, and amethyst;
28.20: the fourth row beryl, onyx, and jasper, and they are to be set in gold filigree.
28.21: The stones shall correspond to the names of the sons of Israel, twelve stones corresponding to their names. They shall be engraved like a signet, each with the name of one of the twelve tribes.
28.22: "You are to make chains of pure gold, twisted like cords for the breast piece.
28.23: You are to make two gold rings for the breast piece, and put the two rings on the two edges of the breast piece.
28.24: You are to put the two gold cords on the two gold rings at the edges of the breast piece,
28.25: and you are to attach the other two ends of the two cords to the filigree settings and attach them to the shoulder pieces of the ephod in front.
28.26: "You are to make two gold rings and attach them to the two edges of the breast piece, on the side of it which is toward the inner side of the ephod.
28.27: You are to make two gold rings and attach them in front on the lower part of the two shoulder pieces of the ephod close to the place where it's joined, above the skillfully woven band of the ephod.
28.28: They shall fasten the rings on the breast piece to the rings on the ephod with a blue cord so it will rest on the skillfully woven band of the ephod and so the breast piece won't come loose from the ephod.27
28.29: "Aaron shall carry the names of Israel's sons on his heart on the breast piece to be worn by the high priest when he makes legal decisions whenever he goes into the Holy Place in order to remember them in the Lord's presence continually.
28.30: You are to put the Urim and Thummim into the breast piece of judgment, and they are to be on Aaron's heart when he goes into the Lord's presence. He shall carry the breast piece of Israel's sons on his heart in the Lord's presence continually.29
28.31: "You are to make the robe of the ephod entirely of blue.
28.32: There shall be an opening at its top, in the middle, with a woven binding around the opening like the opening of a coat of mail so that it cannot be torn.
28.33: On its hem you are to make blue and purple and scarlet pomegranates, all around the skirt, with gold bells between them all the way around.
28.34: You are to have a gold bell and a pomegranate, then a gold bell and a pomegranate, on the hem of the robe all the way around it.
28.35: Aaron shall wear the robe when he ministers so its sound may be heard when he enters and leaves the Holy Place in the Lord's presence, so that he won't die.2832
28.36: "You are to make a medallion of pure gold, and engrave on it 'Holy to the Lord,' like the engravings of a signet.
28.37: You are to put it on a blue cord and place it on the turban. It shall be on the front of the turban
28.38: and worn on Aaron's forehead in order to take away any guilt contained in the holy things which the Israelites consecrate as holy gifts. It shall remain on his forehead continually, so they may be accepted in the Lord's presence.
28.39: You are to weave the checkered tunic of fine linen, you are to make a turban of fine linen, and you are to make an embroidered sash.
28.40: "You are to make tunics for the sons of Aaron, you are to make sashes for them, and you are to make head coverings for them for dignity and beauty.
28.41: You are to put them on Aaron your brother, and on his sons with him, and shall anoint them and ordain them and consecrate them to serve as my priests.
28.42: You are to make linen undergarments for them to cover their naked flesh, and they are to reach from the loins to the thighs.
28.43: They shall be on Aaron and his sons when they enter the Tent of Meeting or when they approach the altar to minister in the Holy Place so they don't incur guilt and die. This shall be a perpetual ordinance for him and his descendants after him."2843

29.1: "This is what you are to do to them to consecrate them to serve me as priests： Take a young bull, two rams without blemish,
29.2: unleavened bread, unleavened cakes mixed with oil, and unleavened wafers spread with oil, which you are to make from fine wheat flour.
29.3: You are to put them in one basket and bring them in the basket along with the bull and the two rams.
29.4: You are to bring Aaron and his sons to the doorway of the Tent of Meeting, and wash them with water.
29.5: Take the garments and clothe Aaron with the tunic, the robe of the ephod, the ephod, and the breast piece, and then gird him with the skillfully woven band of the ephod.
29.6: Then put the turban on his head, and place the holy crown on the turban.
29.7: You are to take the anointing oil, pour it on his head, and anoint him.
29.8: Then you are to bring his sons and clothe them with tunics.
29.9: You are to gird Aaron and his sons with sashes and tie headdresses on them. The priesthood shall belong to them by perpetual ordinance, and you are to ordain Aaron and his sons.3
29.10: "You are to bring the bull in front of the Tent of Meeting, and Aaron and his sons shall lay their hands on the head of the bull.
29.11: Then you are to slaughter the bull in the Lord's presence at the doorway of the Tent of Meeting.
29.12: Take some of the blood of the bull, put it on the horns of the altar with your finger, and pour out the rest of the blood at the base of the altar.
29.13: You are to take all the fat that covers the entrails, the lobe of the liver, the two kidneys, and the fat that is on them and send them up in smoke on the altar.
29.14: You are to burn the flesh of the bull, its hide, and its refuse with fire outside the camp. It is a sin offering.13
29.15: "You are to take one of the rams, and Aaron and his sons shall lay their hands on its head.
29.16: Then you are to slaughter the ram, take its blood, and scatter it around the altar.
29.17: You are to cut the ram into pieces, wash its entrails and legs, put them on the altar along with the pieces and its head,
29.18: and send up the whole ram in smoke on the altar. It is a burnt offering to the Lord; it's a soothing aroma, an offering by fire to the Lord.
29.19: "You are to take the other ram, and Aaron and his sons shall lay their hands on the head of the ram.
29.20: You are to slaughter the ram, take some of its blood, and put it on the lobe of Aaron's right ear, the lobe of his sons' right ears, the thumbs of their right hands, and the big toes of their right feet. Then you are to scatter the rest of the blood around the altar.
29.21: You are to take some of the blood which is on the altar, along with some of the anointing oil, and sprinkle it on Aaron and his garments, and on his sons and their garments. He shall be consecrated with his garments, along with his sons and their garments
29.22: "You are to take the fat from the ram, the fat tail, the fat that covers the entrails, the lobe of the liver, the two kidneys and the fat that is on them, the right thigh (for it's a ram of ordination),
29.23: and one loaf of bread, one cake of bread mixed with oil, and one wafer out of the basket of unleavened bread that is in the Lord's presence.
29.24: You are to put all of these in the hands of Aaron and in the hands of his sons, and present them as a wave offering in the Lord's presence.
29.25: Then you are to take them from their hands and send them up in smoke on the altar on top of the burnt offering for a soothing aroma in the Lord's presence. It is an offering by fire to the Lord.23
29.26: "You are to take the breast of the ram of Aaron's ordination, and present it as a wave offering in the Lord's presence, and it shall be your portion.
29.27: You are to consecrate the portion of the ram of ordination that belongs to Aaron and his sons： the breast of the wave offering that was waved and the thigh of the presented offering that was presented.
29.28: These offerings from the Israelites shall be a perpetual ordinance for Aaron and his sons. They are presented offerings, and they are to be presented offerings from the Israelites out of their peace offerings. They are presented offerings to the Lord.27
29.29: "The holy garments of Aaron shall be for his sons after him so that they may be anointed in them and ordained in them. 30Aaron's son who is priest in his place shall wear them for seven days when he comes into the Tent of Meeting to minister in the Holy Place.
29.31: "You are to take the ram of ordination and boil its flesh in a holy place.
29.32: Then Aaron and his sons shall eat the flesh of the ram along with the bread that is in the basket at the doorway of the Tent of Meeting.
29.33: They shall eat these things by which atonement was made at their ordination to consecrate them, but an unqualified person shall not eat because these things are holy.
29.34: If any of the flesh of the ordination ram or any of the bread is left until morning, you are to burn what is left with fire. Because it's holy, what remains is not to be eaten.
29.35: You are to do this for Aaron and his sons, just as I've commanded you. You are to ordain them for seven days,
29.36: and every day you are to offer a bull as a sin offering for atonement. Offer the sin offering on the altar when you make atonement for it and anoint the altar to consecrate it.
29.37: You are to make atonement for the altar for seven days and consecrate it. It will be most holy, and whatever touches it will be holy."3420
