7.89: When Moses entered the tent of meeting to speak with the Lord, he heard a voice speaking to him above the Mercy Seat over the ark of the testimony. He spoke to him from between the two cherubim.

8.1: The Lord told Moses,
8.2: "Tell Aaron, 'When you set up the lamps, the seven lamps will illuminate the area in front of the lamp stand.'"
8.3: So Aaron did so, setting up the lamps to illuminate the area in front of the lamp stand, just as the Lord had commanded Moses.
8.4: This was how the lamp stand was crafted from hammered gold： From its base to its flowers, it was made of hammered gold. Just as Lord showed Moses, Moses crafted the lamp stand.
8.5: Then the Lord told Moses,
8.6: "Take the Levites from the Israelites and purify them.
8.7: This is what you are to do for them in order to purify them： Sprinkle purifying water over them, have them shave their skin, and then have them wash their garments, and they will be purifified.
8.8: They are to take a young bull along with its meal offering made of flour mixed with oil. "Then you are to take a second young bull as sin offering.
8.9: Assemble the Levites in front of the appointed place of meeting, and assemble the whole congregation of Israel, too.
8.10: Bring the Levites into the Lord's presence and have the Israelites lay their hands on the Levites.
8.11: "Then Aaron is to present the Levites as a wave offering before the Lord from the Israelites, because they are to work in the service of the Lord.
8.12: The Levites are then to lay their hands on the head of the bulls, offering one for a sin offering and the other one for a burnt offering to the Lord to atone for the Levites.
8.13: "You are to make the Levites stand in the presence of Aaron and his sons. Then you are to wave them as wave-offerings to the Lord.
8.14: This is how you are to separate the Levites from among the Israelites. The Levites belong to me.
8.15: "After this, the Levites are to come to serve at the appointed place of meeting, after you have purified them and presented them as wave offerings,
8.16: since they've been set apart for me from among the Israelites.1"I've taken them for myself instead of the first to open the womb - every first born of the Israelites,
8.17: Since every firstborn of Israel belongs to me, from human beings to livestock. "On the same day that I destroyed all the firstborn in the land of Egypt, I consecrated them to myself,
8.18: taking the Levites instead of every firstborn of the Israelites.
8.19: I've set the Levites apart from the Israelites so that Aaron and his sons would work in service at the appointed place of meeting, making atonement on behalf of the Israelites so that there won't be a plague among the Israelites whenever they approach the sanctuary."
8.20: So Moses and Aaron and the Israelites did this on behalf of the Levites. The Israelites did everything that the Lord commanded concerning the Levites.
8.21: The Levites therefore purified themselves, washed their clothes, and then Aaron presented them as wave offerings to the Lord. Aaron provided atonement for them to purify them.
8.22: After this, the Levites entered into their work of service at the appointed place, in the presence of Aaron and his sons. They did everything that the Lord commanded Moses concerning the Levites.
8.25: But starting at 50 years of age, he is to retire from service and is no longer to work.
8.26: He may minister to his brothers at the tent of meeting by keeping watch, but he is not to engage in service. This how you are to act with respect to the obligations of the Levites. 825

9.1: The Lord spoke to Moses in the Wilderness of Sinai during the first month of the second year that they had left Egypt,
9.2: "The Israelites are to observe the Passover at its appointed time
9.3: on the fourteenth day of this month. You are to observe it at this appointed time between the evenings. You are to observe it according to all its decrees and laws."
9.4: So Moses instructed the Israelites to observe the Passover.
9.5: They observed the Passover on the fourteenth day of the first month at twilight, in the Wilderness of Sinai. The Israelites did everything that the Lord had commanded through Moses.
9.6: But there were men who couldn't observe the Passover that day because they had come in contact with a corpse. That very day, they approached Moses and Aaron
9.7: and asked, "Why can't we bring an offering to the Lord at the appointed time among the Israelites, even though we are unclean because we came in contact with a corpse?"
9.8: "Wait while I hear what the Lord has to say about you," Moses replied.
9.9: Then the Lord told Moses,
9.10: "Instruct the Israelites that when any of you or your descendants becomes unclean due to contact with a corpse, or if he is on a long journey, he nevertheless is to observe the Lord's Passover.
9.11: On fourteenth day of the second month at twilight, they are to eat it with unleavened bread and bitter herbs.
9.12: They are not to leave any of it to remain until morning nor are they to break any of its bones. They are to observe it according to all the statutes of the Passover.
9.13: "Now as to the person who is clean and isn't traveling, but fails to observe the Passover, that person is to be eliminated from his people, because he didn't bring an offering to the Lord at its appointed time. That person is to bear his sin.
9.14: "If a resident alien lives with you and wants to observe the Lord's Passover, let him observe it according to the statutes and laws of the Passover. You are to maintain the same statute for the resident alien as you do for the native of the land.
9.15: On the same morning that the tabernacle was set up, a cloud covered the tabernacle, that is, the tent of the testimony, and in the evening fire appeared over the tabernacle until morning.
9.16: It was so continuously - there was a cloud covering by day, and a fire cloud appeared at night.
9.17: Whenever the cloud above the tent ascended, the Israelites would travel and encamp in the place where the cloud settled.
9.18: According to whatever the Lord said, the Israelites would travel. According to whatever the Lord said, they would camp as long as the cloud remained over the tent of meeting.
9.19: When the cloud over the tabernacle remained for a longer time, the Israelites did what the Lord had instructed and didn't travel.
9.20: There were times when the cloud remained over the tabernacle for a number days. They camped in accordance with the Lord's instructions and they traveled in accordance with Lord's instructions.
9.21: There were times when the cloud remained from evening until morning, but when the cloud ascended in the morning, they would journey. Whether by day or by night, they would travel whenever the cloud ascended.
9.22: Whether for two days, a month, or for longer periods, whenever the cloud would remain above the tabernacle, the Israelites would remain in camp, not traveling. But whenever it ascended, then they would travel.
9.23: According to what the Lord said, they would remain in camp, and according to what the Lord said, they would travel. They kept the commands that the Lord had given through Moses.

10.1: The Lord also told Moses,
10.2: "Make two trumpets, crafting them from beaten silver, for use in calling the congregation together and for notifying the camps to set out for travel.
10.3: Sound them when the whole assembly is to gather together at the entrance of the appointed place of meeting.
10.4: "When one trumpet is blown, the elders and the heads of the thousands of the Israelites are to gather to you.
10.5: When you sound an alarm, the ones encamped on the east side are to begin to travel.
10.6: "When you sound the alarm the second time, those encamped on the south are to begin to travel. Alarms are to be sounded for their travels.
10.7: "But when you blow the trumpet to assemble the whole congregation, don't use the same sound as you do for sounding an alarm.
10.8: "The descendants of Aaron the priest are to blow the trumpets. Have them do this for you permanently throughout your generations to come."
10.9: When you wage war in your land against an enemy who is hostile to you, you are to sound an alarm with the trumpets. Then you will be remembered before the face of the Lord your God and you will be delivered from your enemies.
10.10: At the beginning of the month, during your time of rejoicing at the appointed place, sound the trumpet over your burnt offering, then sacrifice your peace offering, since they are to be your memorial before the Lord your God. I am the Lord your God.
10.11: On twentieth day of the second month in the second year, the cloud was lifted up from the tabernacle of the testimony,
10.12: so the Israelites set out from the Sinai Wilderness until the cloud settled in the Paran Wilderness,
10.13: doing what the Lord had said according through Moses.
10.14: The standard of the camp of Judah was the first to travel, accompanied by its army with Amminadab's son Nahshon in charge.
10.15: Zuar's son Nethaneel was in charge of the camp of Issachar.
10.16: Helon's son Eliab was in charge of the camp of Zebulon.
10.17: The tabernacle was taken down, and the descendants of Gershon and Merari carried the tabernacle.
10.18: Then the standard of the camp of Reuben set out, accompanied by its army, with Shedeur's son Elizur in charge.
10.19: Zurishaddai's son Shelumiel was in charge of the tribe of Simeon.
10.20: Deuel's son Eliasaph was in charge of the tribe of Gad.
10.21: Then the Kohathites, carrying the sanctuary, set out, since the tabernacle was to be set up before they arrive.
10.22: After this, the standard of the camp of Ephraim set out, accompanied by its army with Ammihud's son Elishama in charge.
10.23: Pedazzur's son Gamaliel was in charge of the tribe of Manasseh.
10.24: Gideoni's son Abidan was in charge of the army of the tribe of Benjamin.
10.25: Then the standard of the camp of Dan set out, functioning as the rear guard for all the encampments, accompanied by its army with Ammishaddai's son Ahiezer.
10.26: Ocran's son Pagiel was in charge of the tribe of Asher.
10.27: Enan's son Ahira was in charge of the tribe of Naphtali.
10.28: This was the travel order for the Israelites, whenever their companies traveled.
10.29: Then Moses said to Ruel's son Hobab from Midian, Moses' father-in-law, "We are traveling to the place about which the Lord said 'I will give it to you.' So Come with us and we'll be good to you, because the Lord has spoken good things about Israel."
10.30: But he said, "I won't go with you because I'm returning to my land and to my own family."
10.31: Then Moses responded, "Please don't leave us now, since you know where we can camp in the wilderness. You could be our guide.
10.32: And when you come with us, the good things that the Lord will grant us, we'll give you as well."
10.33: So they traveled from the mountain of the Lord, a trip of three day, with the Ark of the Covenant of the Lord traveling in front of them - a three day trip to explore a place for them to rest.
10.34: Moreover, the cloud of the Lord protected them during the day when they left their camp.
10.35: Whenever the ark was ready to travel, Moses would say： "Arise, Lord, to scatter your enemies, so that whoever hates you will flee from your presence."
10.36: Whenever the ark was being readied to rest, he would say： "Return, Lord, to the countless thousands of Israel."

11.1: Eventually, as soon as the people began complaining about their distress, the Lord heard. When the Lord heard it, his anger flared up and the Lord's fire incinerated some of them within the outskirts of the camp.
11.2: When the people cried out to Moses, he prayed to the Lord and the fire stopped.
11.3: He then named that place Taberah, because the Lord's fire had incinerated some of them.
11.4: Meanwhile, certain riff-raff among the people had an insatiable appetite for food. As a result, they wept and turned back, and the Israelites cried out, "If only somebody would feed us some meat!
11.5: How we remember the fish that we used to eat in Egypt for free! And the cucumbers, melons, leeks, onions, and garlic!
11.6: But now we can't stand it any more, because there's nothing in front of us except this manna." 5
11.7: Now manna was reminiscent of coriander seed, with an appearance similar to amber.
11.8: People would go out to gather it, then they would grind it in mills or pound it in mortars, and then they would boil it in pots or make cakes out of it that tasted like butter cakes.
11.9: When the dew fell in the camp, the manna came with it.
11.10: Moses heard the people weeping throughout their entire families. Everyone gathered at the entrance of their tents so that the Lord was very angry. Moses thought the situation was bad,
11.11: so he asked the Lord, "Why did you bring all this trouble to your servant -  Why haven't I found favor in your eyes -  After all, you're putting the burden of this entire people on me!
11.12: "Did I conceive this people or give birth to them, so that you would tell me to carry them near my heart like a wet nurse carries a suckling baby to the land that you promised to their forefathers -
11.13: Where am I going to get meat to give this people -  After all, they're crying in front of me, 'Give us meat to eat!'
11.14: I cannot carry this whole nation! The burden is too heavy for me!
11.15: If this is how you treat me, please kill me right now, if I've found favor in your eyes, because I don't want to keep starting at all of this misery!"
11.16: Then the Lord told Moses, "Gather together for me 70 men who are elders of Israel, men whom you know to be elders of the people and officers over them. Then bring them to the tent of meeting and let them stand there with you.
11.17: Then I'll come down and speak with you. I'll take some of the spirit that rests on you and apportion it among them, so that they may help you bear the burden of the people. That way, you won't bear it by yourself.
11.18: "But give this command to the people： 'You are to consecrate yourselves, because tomorrow you're going to eat meat, since you've complained where the Lord can hear it, "Who can give us meat to eat -  After all, life was better with us in Egypt."' Therefore, the Lord is going to give you meat and you'll eat -
11.19: not only for a day, or for two days, or for five days, or for ten days, or for 20 days,
11.20: but for a whole month - until it comes out your nostrils and makes you vomit. This is because you've despised the Lord, who is among you, and you cried out in his presence, complaining, 'Why did we ever leave of Egypt?'"
11.21: Moses responded, "I'm with 600,000 people on foot and you're saying I am to give them enough meat to eat for a whole month -
11.22: What if we were to slaughter our entire inventory of flocks and herds for them -  Would that be enough -  What if we could gather all the fish in the sea in nets for them -  Would that be enough, either?"
11.23: But the Lord responded to Moses, "Is the Lord short on power -  You're now going to witness whether what I say will come to pass or not."
11.24: So Moses went out and told the people what the Lord had said. He gathered 70 men from the elders of the people and stationed them around the tent.
11.25: The Lord came down in a cloud, spoke to Moses, and made an apportionment from the spirit who rested on him to the 70 elders. When the spirit rested on them, they prophesied, but that was it.
11.26: Now two men rhad emained in camp. One was named Eldad and the other was named Medad. When the spirit rested on them, since they were among those who were listed but had not gone out to the tent, they stayed behind and prophesied in the camp.
11.27: A young man ran and reported to Moses, "Eldad and Meldad are prophesying in the camp!"
11.28: In response, Nun's son Joshua, Moses' attendant and one of his choice men, exclaimed, "My master Moses! Stop them!"
11.29: "Are you jealous on account of me?" Moses asked in reply. "I wish all of the Lord's people were prophets and that the Lord would put his spirit upon them!"
11.30: Then Moses - that is, he and the elders of Israel - returned to the camp.
11.31: Just then, a wind burst forth from the Lord, who brought quails from the sea and spread them all around the camp, about a day's journey in each direction, completely encircling the camp about three feet deep on top of the ground!
11.32: The people stayed up all that day, all that night, and all through the next day, gathering quails. The one who gathered least gathered enough to fill ten 80-gallon drums, as they spread out all around the camp.
11.33: But even as they were chewing the meat and before they had swalloed it, the Lord became very angry with the people and struck them with a disasterous plague.
11.34: That's why the place was named Kibroth-hattaavah, because they buried the people there who had an insatiable appetite for meat.
11.35: Later, the people left Kibroth-hattaavah for Hazeroth and camped there.1134

12.1: Miriam and Aaron rebelled against Moses on account of the Cushite woman that he had married.
12.2: They asked, "Has the Lord spoken only through Moses -  Hasn't he also spoken through us?" But the Lord heard it.
12.3: Now the man Moses was very humble - more than any person on earth.
12.4: All of a sudden, the Lord said to Moses, Aaron, and Miriam： "The three of you are to come out to the tent of meeting." So the three of them went out.
12.5: Then the Lord came down in a pillar of cloud, stood at the entrance to the tent of meeting, and summoned Aaron and Miriam. So both of them went forward.
12.6: Then he said to the two of them： "Pay attention to what I have to say! When there is a prophet among you, won't I, the Lord, reveal myself to him in a vision -  Won't I speak with him in a dream -
12.7: "But that's not how it is with my servant Moses, since he has been entrusted with my entire household!
12.8: I speak to him audibly and in visions, not in mysteries. If he can gaze at the image of the Lord, why aren't you afraid to speak against my servant Moses?"
12.9: Because the Lord was very angry with them, he left,
12.10: but when the cloud ascended from the tent, Miriam had become leprous, as white as snow! Aaron turned toward Miriam, and she had leprosy!
12.11: Aaron begged Moses, "I pray my lord, please don't hold this sin against us, since we've acted foolishlyandsinnedindoingso.
12.12: Please don't let her be like one of the living dead, who is born with a congenital skin disease."
12.13: So Moses prayed to the Lord： "O Lord, please heal her."
12.14: But the Lord told Moses, "If her father had merely spit in her face, wouldn't she be humiliated -  She is to be placed in isolation for seven days. After that, she may be brought in."
12.15: So Miriam was isolated outside the camp for seven days and the people didn't travel until Miriam was brought in.
12.16: After that, the people traveled from Hazeroth and encamped in the Wilderness of Paran.

13.1: Later, the Lord told Moses,
13.2: Send men to explore the land of Canaan that I'm about to give to the Israelites. Send one man to represent each of his ancestor's tribes, every one of them a distinguished leader among them."
13.3: So that's just what Moses did, sending them from the Wilderness of Paran according to the Lord's instructions. Each of the men were Israelite leaders.
13.4: These were their names： From Reuben tribe, Zaccur's son Shammua;
13.5: From Simeon's tribe, Hori's son Shaphat;
13.6: From Judah's tribe, Jephunneh's son Caleb;
13.7: from Issachar's tribe, Joseph's son Igal;
13.8: From Ephraim's tribe, Nun's son Hosea;
13.9: From Benjamin's tribe, Raphu's son Palti;
13.10: from Zebulun's tribe, Sodi's son Gaddiel;
13.11: from Joseph's tribe of Manasseh, Susi's son Gaddi;
13.12: From Dan's tribe, Gemalli's son Ammiel;
13.13: from Asher's tribe, Michael's son Sethur;
13.14: from Naphtali's tribe, Vophsi's son Nahbi;
13.15: and from Gad's tribe, Machi's son Geuel.
13.16: These are the names of the men sent by Moses to explore the land. Moses renamed Nun's son Hoshea to Joshua.
13.17: Then he sent them out to explore the land of Canaan. He instructed them, "Go up from here by way of the Negev, then ascend to the hill country.
13.18: See what the land is like. Observe whether the people who live there are strong or weak, or whether they're few or numerous.
13.19: Inspect whether the land where they live is good or bad, and whether the cities in which they live are merely tents or if they're fortified.
13.20: Examine the farmland, whether it's fertile or barren, and see if there are fruit-bearing trees in it or not. Be very courageous, and bring back some samples of the fruit of the land." As it was, that time of year was the season for the first-fruits of the grape harvest.
13.21: So they went to explore the land from the Wilderness of Zin to Rehob, and as far as the outskirts of Hamath.
13.22: They went by way of the Negev and reached Hebron, where Ahaiman, Sheshai, and Talmai, the descendants of Anak lived. (Hebron had been constructed seven years before Zoan in Egypt had been built).
13.23: Soon they arrived in the valley of Eshcol, where they cut a single branch of grapes and carried it on a pole between two men, along with some pomegranates and figs.
13.24: The entire place was called the Eshcol Valley on account of the cluster of grapes that the men of Israel had taken from there.
13.25: At the end of 40 days they all returned from exploring the land,
13.26: came in to Moses and Aaron, and delivered their report to the entire congregation of Israel in the Wilderness of Paran at Kadesh. They brought back their report to the entire congregation and showed them the fruit of the land.
13.27: "We arrived at the place where you've sent us," they reported, "and it certainly does flow with milk and honey. Furthermore, this is its fruit,
13.28: except that the people who have settled in the land are strong, and their cities are greatly fortified. We also saw the descendants of Anak.
13.29: Amalek lives throughout the Negev, while the Hittites, Jebusites, and Amorites live in the hill country. The Canaanites live by the sea and on the bank of the Jordan. "28
13.30: Caleb silenced the people on Moses' behalf and responded, "Let's go up and take control, because we can definitely conquer it."
13.31: "We can't attack those people," the men who were with him said, "because they're too strong compared to us."
13.32: So they put out this false report to the Israelites about the land that they had explored： "The land that we've explored is one that devours its inhabitants. All the people whom we observed were giants.
13.33: We also saw the Nephilim, the descendants of Anak. Compared to the Nephilim, as we see things, we're like grasshoppers and that's their opinion of us!13"

14.1: At this, the entire assembly complained, started to shout, and cried through the rest of that night.
14.2: All the Israelites complained against Moses and Aaron. Then the entire assembly responded, "We wish that we had died in Egypt or in this wilderness.
14.3: What's the point in the Lord bringing us to this land -  To die by the sword so our wives and children would become war victims -  Wouldn't it be better for us to return to Egypt?"
14.4: Then they said to each other, "Let's assign a leader and go back to Egypt."
14.5: Moses and Aaron fell on their faces in front of the entire assembly of the congregation of Israel.
14.6: Nun's son Joshua and Jephunneh's son Caleb, who had accompanied the others who also had explored the land, tore their clothes
14.7: and attempted to reason with the entire congregation of Israel. They told them, "The land that we went through and explored is very, very good.
14.8: If the Lord is pleased with us, he'll bring us into this land and give it to us. It flows with milk and honey.
14.9: However, don't rebel against the Lord or be afraid of the people who live in the land, because we'll gobble them right up.  Their defenses will collapse, because the Lord is with us. You are not to be afraid of them."
14.10: But the entire congregation was talking about stoning them to death. Suddenly, the glory of the Lord appeared at the tent of meeting to all of the Israelites.
14.11: "How long will this people keep on spurning me and refusing to trust me, despite all the miracles that I've done among them?" the Lord asked Moses.
14.12: "That's why I'm going to attack them them with pestilence and disinherit them. Instead, I'll make you a great nation - even mightier than they are!"
14.13: But Moses responded to the Lord, "When Egypt hears that you've brought this people out from among them with a mighty demonstration of power,
14.14: they'll also proclaim to the inhabitants of this land that they've heard you're among this people, Lord, whom they've seen face to face, since your your cloud stands guard over them. You've guided them with a pillar of cloud by day and with a pillar of fire by night.
14.15: "But if you slaughter this people all at the same time, then the nations who heard about your fame will say,
14.16: 'The Lord slaughtered this people in the wilderness because he wasn't able to bring them to the land that he promised them.'
14.17: "Now, let the power of the Lord be magnified, just as you promised when you said,
14.18: 'The Lord is slow to anger and abundant in faithful love, forgiving iniquity and transgression, but he won't acquit the guilty. He recalls the iniquity of fathers to the third and fourth generation.'
14.19: "Forgive, please, the iniquity of this people, according to your great, faithful love, in the same way that you've carried this people from Egypt to this place."
14.20: The Lord responded, "I've forgiven them based on what you've said.
14.21: But just as I live, and just as the whole earth will be filled with the Lord's glory,
14.22: none of those men who saw my glory and watched my miracles that I did in Egypt and in the wilderness - even though they've tested me these ten times and never listened to my voice -
14.23: will ever see the land that I promised to their ancestors. Those who spurned me won't see it.
14.24: "Now as to my servant Caleb, because a different spirit is within him and he has remained true to me, I'm going to bring him into the land that he explored, and his descendants are to inherit it.
14.25: Now the Amalekite and the Canaanite live in the valley. Tomorrow, turn then travel to the wilderness in the direction of the Red Sea."
14.26: Then the Lord told Moses and Aaron,
14.27: "How long will this wicked assembly keep complaining about me -  I've heard the complaints of the Israelites that they've been murmuring against me.
14.28: So tell them that as long as I live - consider this to be an oracle from the Lord - as certainly as you've spoken right into my ears, that's how I'm going to treat you.
14.29: Your corpses will fall in this wilderness - every single one of you who has been counted among you, according to your number from 20 years and above, who complained against me.
14.30: "You will certainly never enter the land about which I made an oath with my uplifted hand to settle you in it, except for Jephunneh's son Caleb and Nun's son Joshua.
14.31: However, I'll bring your little ones  - the ones whom you claimed would become war victims - into the land so that they'll know by experience the land that you've rejected.14
14.32: "Now as for you, your corpses will fall in this wilderness
14.33: and your children will wander throughout the wilderness for 40 years. They'll bear the consequences of your idolatries until your bodies are entirely consumed in the wilderness.
14.34: Just as you explored the land for 40 days, you'll bear the consequences of your iniquities for 40 years - one year for each day - as you experience my hostility.
14.35: I, the Lord have spoken. "I will indeed do this to this evil congregation, who gathered together against me. They'll be eliminated in this wilderness and will surely die."
14.36: After this, the men whom Moses sent out to explore the land, who returned and made the whole congregation complain against him by bringing an evil report concerning the land,
14.37: and who produced an evil report about the land, died of pestilence in the Lord's presence.
14.38: However, Nun's son Joshua and Jephunneh's son Caleb, who had explored the land, remained alive.
14.39: After Moses had told all of this to the Israelites, the people deeply mourned.
14.40: So they got up early the next morning and traveled to the top of the mountain, telling themselves, "Look, we're here and we're going to go up to the place that the Lord had spoken about, even though we've sinned."
14.41: But Moses asked them, "Why do you continue to sin against what the Lord said -  Don't you know that you can never succeed -
14.42: Don't go up, since you know that Lord is no longer with you. You'll be attacked right in front of your own enemies.
14.43: The Amalekites and Canaanites are there waiting for you. You'll die violently, since you've turned your back and have stopped following the Lord. The Lord won't be with you."
14.44: But they presumed to go up to the top of the mountain, even though the ark of the covenant of the Lord and Moses didn't leave the camp.
14.45: The Amalekites came down, accompanied by some Canaanites who lived in the mountains. They attacked and defeated them even while the Israelites were retreating to Hormah.

15.1: Later, the Lord instructed Moses,
15.2: "Tell the Israelites that
15.3: when you enter the land where you'll be living that I'm about to give you, you are to make an offering by fire to the Lord, either a burnt offering, a sacrificial offering to fulfill a vow, or a voluntary offering at the appointed time, to make a pleasing aroma to the Lord either from your cattle or from your flocks.
15.4: "The offeror is to bring the oblation to the Lord, containing one tenth of an ephah of fine flour as a grain offering, mixed with one fourth of a hin of olive oil.
15.5: "Also prepare one fourth of a hin of wine for a drink offering or for the sacrifice of each lamb.
15.6: "For a ram, prepare a grain offering consisting of two tenths of  an ephah of fine flour mixed with one third of a hin of olive oil.
15.7: "Now as for your drink offering, offer one third of a hin of wine as a pleasing aroma to the Lord.
15.8: "When you prepare a bull as a burnt offering, or as a sacrifice to fulfill a vow, or for peace offerings to the Lord,
15.9: then the bullock is to be presented accompanied by a meal offering of three tenth of an ephah of fine flour mixed with half a hin of oil.
15.10: "As for drink offering, offer half a hin of wine, for an offering made by fire is a pleasing aroma to the Lord.
15.11: Do this for each bullock, ram, male lamb, or goat.
15.12: Depending on the number of offerings that you prepare, do for each one according to their number.
15.13: "Every native born person is to do these things, bringing an offering made by fire as a pleasing aroma to the Lord."
15.14: "Now, if a resident alien lives with you, or whoever else is with you throughout your generations, let him make an offering made by fire, a pleasing aroma to the Lord. Just as you do, so is he to do.
15.15: There is to be a single standard for your community, one statute for you and the resident alien who lives with you, a long lasting statute throughout your generations. Just as you do, so is the resident alien to do in the presence of the Lord.
15.16: There is to be one law and one ordinance for you and for the resident alien who lives with you."
15.17: Then the Lord instructed Moses：
15.18: "Tell the Israelites that when they enter the land that I'm about to bring you to,
15.19: when you have eaten some of the bread that the land produces, you are to offer a raised offering to the Lord.
15.20: You are to offer a cake made from the first of your bread dough as a raised offering to the Lord. Offer it as a raised offering right off your threshing floor.
15.21: From  then on, throughout your generations give the first of your bread dough to the Lord.20
15.22: "Here's what you are to do when you all go astray and fail to observe all these commands that the Lord had spoken to Moses,
15.23: including anything that the Lord commanded you by the authority of Moses, starting from the day the Lord commanded Moses and continuing through your generations.
15.24: When anything is done without the knowledge of the congregation, the entire community is to offer one young bull for a burnt offering, a pleasing aroma to the Lord, along with its meal and drink offerings offered according to procedure, and one male goat for a sin offering.
15.25: Then the priest is to make atonement for the entire community of the Israelites, and they will be forgiven for inadvertent sins. They are to bring their offering, an offering made by fire to the Lord, as well as their sin offering, into the Lord's presence on account of their error.
15.26: Then the entire community of Israel, will be forgiven, along with the resident alien who lives among them, since all the people will have sinned inadvertently."
15.27: "Now when one person sins inadvertently, then he is to bring a one year old female goat for a sin offering.
15.28: Then, in the Lord's presence, the priest is to make atonement for the person who sinned inadvertently, that is, to make atonement on his behalf so he may be forgiven.
15.29: You are to have a single law for the one who does things inadvertently, whether for the native-born Israelite or for the resident alien who lives among you.
15.30: "But if some person acts with a high hand, whether a native-born or a resident alien, he blasphemes God, and that person is to be eliminated from among his people.
15.31: Because he has despised the law of the Lord and has broken his commands, that person is certainly to be eliminated. His iniquity will remain on him."
15.32: As it was when the Israelites were in the wilderness, they found a man who was gathering wood on the Sabbath day.
15.33: The ones who found him gathering wood brought him to Moses, Aaron, and all the people.
15.34: Then they confined him until it could be declared what should be done to him.
15.35: Then the Lord said to Moses, "The man is certainly to die. The entire community is to stone him to death outside the camp."
15.36: So the whole community brought him outside the camp and stoned him with stones so that he died, just as the Lord had commanded Moses.
15.38: "Tell the Israelites that they are to make tassels at the edges of their garments throughout their generations and that they are to put a violet cord on the tassels at the edges of their garments.
15.39: That way, when you see the tassel, you'll remember all the commands of the Lord and you'll observe them. Then you won't seek your own interests and desires that lead you to be unfaithful.
15.40: Therefore remember to observe all my commands and to be holy in the presence of your God.
15.41: I am the Lord your God, who brought you out of the land of Egypt to be your God. I am the Lord your God.

16.1: Now Izhar's son Korah, the grandson of Kohath, a Levite, along with Eliab's sons Dathan and Abiram, and Peleth's son On, a descendant of Reuben, took charge
16.2: of a rebellion against Moses, along with 250 community leaders, Israelites who were famous men and representatives from the assembly.
16.3: They gathered together against Moses and Aaron and said to them, "You have appropriated too much for yourselves from the entire congregation, since all of them are holy, and the Lord is among them, too. Why do you exalt yourselves over the Lord's assembly?"
16.4: When Moses heard this, he fell on his face.
16.5: Then he addressed Korah and his entire company, "In the morning, may the Lord reveal who belongs to him and who is holy. May he cause that person to approach him. May he cause to approach him the one whom he has chosen.
16.6: Korah, you and your entire company, are to bring censers,
16.7: put fire and incense in them in the Lord's presence tomorrow. It will be that the man whom the Lord chooses will be holy. You're taking too much for yourselves, you Levites."
16.8: Moses also told Korah, "Listen now, you Levites!
16.9: Is it such an insignificant thing to you that the God of Israel has separated you from the Israelites to draw you to himself, appointing you to do the work of the tabernacle of the Lord and to stand before the community to minister to them -
16.10: He brought you near, along with all of your relatives, the Levites. Are you also seeking the priesthood -
16.11: Therefore you and your group have conspired against the Lord and Aaron. What is it that causes you to complain against him?"
16.12: So Moses sent for Eliab's sons Dathan and Abiram, but they responded, "We're not coming.
16.13: Is it such an insignificant thing that you brought us out of a land flowing with milk and honey, to kill us in the wilderness -  Now you're trying to make yourself be a prince and rule over us, aren't you -
16.14: You still haven't brought us into a land flowing with milk and honey, nor have you given us an inheritance of fields and vineyards. Do you really think that you can make these men look the other way -  We won't go up."13
16.15: Moses was very angry, so he told the Lord, "Please don't accept their offering. I haven't taken even one donkey from them nor have I hurt even one of them."
16.16: Then Moses said to Korah, "You and your entire company are to present yourselves in the Lord's presence tomorrow - you, they, and Aaron.
16.17: Each man is to takes a censer, put incense on it, and bring it ino the Lord's presence, each man with his censer, for a total of 250 censers. You and Aaron are each to bring his own censer."
16.18: So each man took his censer, put fire coals inside of it, placed incense in it, and then stood at the entrance of the tent of meeting, accompanied by Moses and Aaron.
16.19: When Korah had assembled the entire community in opposition to Moses and Aaron at the entrance of the tent of meeting, the glory of the Lord appeared to the entire community.
16.20: Then the Lord told Moses and Aaron,
16.21: "Separate yourselves from among this community, and I'll destroy them in a moment."
16.22: Then they fell on their faces and said, "God, the God of the spirits of all flesh, will you be angry at the entire congregation on account of one man's sin?"
16.24: "Tell the community to move away from where Korah, Dathan, and Abiram are living."
16.25: So Moses got up and went to Dathan and Abiram, and the elders of Israel followed him.
16.26: Then he told the community, "Move away from the camps of these wicked men and don't touch anything that belongs to them. That way you won't be destroyed along with all their sins."
16.27: So they all moved away from the entire area where Korah, Dathan, and Abiram were living. Now Korah, Dathan, and Abiram stood at the entrance of their tents with their wives, sons, and little children.
16.28: Then Moses said, "This is how you'll know that the Lord has sent me to do all these awesome works - they're not coming merely from me.
16.29: If these people die a death similar to all other human beings, or if they are punished with a punishment common to other men, then the Lord didn't send me.
16.30: But if the Lord creates something new, so that the ground opens its mouth and swallows them and everything that belongs to them and they all descend directly to Sheol while still alive, then you'll know that these men have spurned the Lord."
16.31: Just as he finished saying all these things, the ground under them split open.
16.32: The earth opened its mouth and swallowed them, all their households, everyone who was affiliated with Korah, and all of their property.
16.33: So they and all that belonged to them descended alive to Sheol. Then the earth closed over them. That's how they were annihilated from the assembly.
16.34: Then all of the Israelites who were around them ran away when they heard them crying, "…so the ground won't swallow us up, too."
16.35: After this, fire came from the Lord and incinerated the 250 men who offered the incense.
16.37: "Tell Aaron's son Eleazar the priest to take out the censers out of the flames and scatter the coals far away, since they are holy.
16.38: As for the censers of those rebels who died, fasten them into beaten plates to line the altar. Since they brought them into the Lord's presence, they're holy. They are to become a reminder to the Israelites."
16.39: So Eleazar the priest took the bronze censers that had been burned and beat them into metal plates for the altar,
16.40: to serve as a memorial to the Israelites, a reminder that no unauthorized person, who isn't a descendant of Aaron is to attempt to burn incense in the Lord's presence, so that he may not become like Korah and his group, just as the Lord had spoken by the authority of Moses.
16.41: Nevertheless, the very next day, the whole congregation of Israel complained against Moses and Aaron, "You've killed the Lord's people!"
16.42: When the community gathered together against Moses and Aaron, they turned toward the tent of meeting. All of a sudden, a cloud covered it and the glory of the Lord appeared.
16.43: Then Moses and Aaron entered the tent of meeting.
16.45: "Leave this community, so I can annihilate them in a moment." But they fell upon their faces.
16.46: Then Moses said to Aaron. "Take the censer, put fire on it from the altar, and burn some incense. Then walk quickly to the congregation and atone for them, because wrath has already come out from the Lord - the plague has begun." 16
16.47: So Aaron took the censer, just as Moses had spoken, and ran out to the center of the assembly, where a plague had begun among the people. He set the incense on fire and atoned for the people.
16.48: He stood between the dead and the living and restrained the plague.
16.49: Those who died due to the plague numbered 14,700, not counting those who died due to the matter with Korah.1648
16.50: Then Aaron returned to Moses at the entrance of the tent of meeting after the slaughter had been restrained.

17.1: The Lord instructed Moses,
17.2: "Tell the Israelites to take a rod - one from each ancestral house, that is, one from every leader, for a total of twelve rods. Write each tribal name on his rod.
17.3: You are also to write Aaron's name on the tribe of Levi, since there is to be one rod for every leader of their ancestors's tribes.2
17.4: "Then lay them there in the tent of meeting in front of the Ark of the Covenant where I'll meet with you.
17.5: The rod that belongs to the man whom I'll choose will burst into bloom. That's how I'll put a stop to the complaints of the Israelites, who are complaining against you."17
17.6: So Moses spoke to the Israelites, and each of the tribe leaders gave him a rod, one for each leader, according to their ancestral tribes, for a total of twelve rods. Aaron's rod was one of them.
17.7: Then Moses laid out the rods in the Lord's presence, inside the tent of the testimony.
17.8: The next morning, Moses went to the tent of the testimony and the rod of Aaron of the tribe of Levi had burst into bloom! It sprouted buds, bloomed blossoms, and produced fully ripe almonds!
17.9: Then Moses took out all the rods from the Lord's presence to show all the Israelites. Everybody looked, and  then each man took his rod.
17.10: Then the Lord instructed Moses, "Return Aaron's rod before the testimony to be kept for a sign of against the rebels so that you may put an end to their complaints against me so that they won't die."
17.11: So Moses did exactly what the Lord had commanded him to do.
17.12: Then the Israelites said to Moses, "We're sure to die! We're all going to perish - all of us! 13Anyone who comes near or approaches the Lord's tabernacle is to die. Are all of us going to die?"

18.1: Later, the Lord said to Aaron, "You, your sons, and your father's tribe with you are to bear the iniquity of the sanctuary. Also, you and your sons with you are to bear the iniquity of your priesthood.
18.2: "Moreover, bring your brothers from your father's tribe of Levi with you, so they may join you and minister to you while you and your sons with you stand in the presence of the tent of the testimony.
18.3: They are to take care of your concerns and all the responsibilities involved with the tent. "But they're not to approach the holy vessels or the altar. That way, neither you nor they will die.
18.4: They are to join you to maintain services related to the tent of meeting, for all the responsibilities ivolved with the tent. But no unauthorized person is to approach you.
18.5: "Take care of the sanctuary and the services of the altar so that there won't be any more wrath on the Israelites.
18.6: Notice that I've taken your brothers, the Levites, from among the Israelites, giving them to you as a gift from the Lord to perform the service of the tent of meeting.
18.7: Now you and your sons with you are to maintain your priestly duties and all matters that concern the altar and what is housed within the veil. You are to perform these service. I'm giving you the priesthood as a gift of service, but any unauthorized person who approaches is to be put to death."
18.8: Then the Lord told Aaron, "Look! I am indeed placing you in charge of my raised offerings and the holy things concerning the Israelites. Because of your anointing, I'm giving you and your sons a prescribed portion forever.
18.9: "This is what is to belong to you from consecrated offerings spared from the fire： all of their offerings, grain offerings, sin offerings, and trespass offerings that they render to me. They're to be considered most sacred to you and your sons.
18.10: You may eat them as consecrated gifts. Every male may eat them. They're sacred for you.
18.11: "The raised offering and wave offerings presented by the Israelites are yours, too. I've given them to you, to your sons, and to your daughters as a prescribed apportionment forever. Everyone who is clean in your household may eat it.
18.12: All the best oil, wine, grain, and first fruits that they give to the Lord are to belong to you. Everyone who is clean in your household may eat it.
18.13: "The first ripe fruits of every thing that the land produces and that they bring to the Lord are yours, too. Everyone who is clean in your household may eat it.
18.14: "Every devoted thing in Israel is yours, too.
18.15: Everything that opens the womb, any living thing that they bring to the Lord - whether from human beings or animals - are for you. Just be sure that you redeem the first born of people and the first born of unclean animals.
18.16: "Those that can be redeemed, you are to redeem at the age of one month, based on your estimate - for five shekels of silver, according to the shekel of the sanctuary, that is, for 20 gerahs.
18.17: But you are not to redeem the first born of a cow, sheep, or a female goat. They are holy. You are to sprinkle their blood on the altar and burn their fat for an offering made by fire, a pleasing aroma to the Lord.
18.18: "Their meat is to belong to you, just as the breast wave offering and the right thigh is yours.
18.19: I'm giving you, your sons, and your daughters as a prescribed portion forever all the raised offerings of the consecrated things that the Israelites offer to the Lord. It's a salt covenant forever before the Lord with you and your descendants with you."
18.20: "Then the Lord instructed Aaron, "You are not to have any inheritance in the land, nor are you to have any portion among the people. I am your portion and your inheritance among the Israelites.
18.21: As to the descendants of Levi, certainly I've given all the tithes in Israel as their inheritance in return for their services that they perform at the tent of meeting.
18.22: Therefore the Israelites need no longer come to the tent of meeting, so they won't suffer the consequences of their sin and die.
18.23: The Levites are to perform the service of the tent of meeting and they are to bear their iniquity. This is to be a statute forever, throughout your generations, that they are not to receive an inheritance among the Israelites,
18.24: because I've given to the Levites the tithes that the Israelites bring to the Lord as raised offering. Therefore I told them that, unlike the Israelites, they won't receive an inheritance."1823
18.26: Tell the Levites that when they receive tithes from the Israelites the tithes that I've given you from them as an inheritance, you are to offer a tenth of it it as a raised offering for the Lord.
18.27: Your raised offerings are to be accounted for you as though it were grain from threshing floors and full produce from wine vats.
18.28: "You are to offer a raised offering to the Lord from all your tithes that you receive from the Israelites. Give Aaron the priest the raised offering of the Lord
18.29: out of all the most consecrated offerings that you receive, that is, all the raised offerings of the Lord, with all its best and the most holy parts of it.
18.30: Tell them that when they bring the best from it, as far as the Levites are concerned it is to be considered like produce from the threshing floors and wine vats.
18.31: You and your household may eat it anywhere, because it's a reward to you in return for your services at the tent of meeting.
18.32: You won't sin by offering the best of it, and you are not to profane the sacred things of the Israelites, so that you won't die."

19.1: The Lord told Moses and Aaron,
19.2: "This is the ordinance of the law that the Lord commanded that the Israelites be told： They are to bring you a spotless red heifer, without physical defect, that has never been fitted with a yoke.
19.3: They are to deliver it to Eleazar the priest, and it is to be brought outside the camp and slaughtered in his presence.
19.4: "Then Eleazar the priest is to take blood from it with his finger and sprinkle the blood in front of the tent of meeting.
19.5: The entire heifer is to be incinerated in his presence, including its skin, its flesh, its blood, and its dung.
19.6: Then the priest is to take some cedar wood, hyssop, and scarlet material and throw it into the middle of the burning heifer.
19.7: The priest is to wash his clothes and bathe himself with water, after which he may enter the camp, but he is to remain unclean until evening.
19.8: "Whoever takes part in the burning is to wash his clothes and bathe himself in water and is to remain unclean until the evening.
19.9: "Then someone who is unclean is to gather the ashes of the heifer and lay them outside the camp in a clean place. This is to be done for the community of Israel to use for water of purification from sin.
19.10: "Whoever gathers the ashes of the heifer is to wash his clothes and is to remain unclean until the evening. "This ordinance is to remain for the benefit of both the Israelites and the resident aliens who live among them."
19.11: "Whoever comes in contact with the body of a dead person is to remain unclean for seven days.
19.12: He is to purify himself on the third day and he will be clean on the seventh day. But if he can't purify himself on the third day then he can't be clean on the seventh day.
19.13: "Anyone who comes in contact with a dead person, that is, with the corpse of a human being who has died, but who does not purify himself, defiles the Lord's tabernacle. That person is to be eliminated from Israel, because the water of impurity wasn't sprinkled on him. He remains unclean and his uncleanness will remain with him.
19.14: This is the procedure to follow when a man dies in his tent. Every one who enters the tent and every one in it is to remain unclean for seven days.
19.15: Every open vessel that has no covering fastened around it is to be considered unclean.
19.16: "Whoever is out in an open field and touches the body of someone who was killed by a sword, or a dead body, or someone's bones, or a grave, he is to be considered unclean for seven days.
19.17: Now as for the unclean, they are to take ashes from the burning sin offering, and pour running water on it inside a vessel.
19.18: A clean person is to take some hyssop, dip it in water, and then sprinkle it on the tent, on every vessel, and on whoever was there; that is, on whoever touched the bones, the killed person, or the dead body, including whoever dug the grave.
19.19: "And the clean person is to sprinkle the unclean person on the third day and seventh day and then he is to purify himself on the seventh day, wash his clothes, and bathe with water. He is to be considered clean at evening.
19.20: "The person who is unclean but who doesn't purify himself is to be eliminated from contact with the assembly, since he has defiled the Lord's  sanctuary and the water of impurity wasn't sprinkled on him. He is to be considered unclean
19.21: as a continuing reminder to them.192"Whoever sprinkles the water of impurity is to wash his clothes, and whoever comes in contact with the water of impurity is to remain unclean until evening.
19.22: Furthermore, anything that the unclean person touches is to be considered unclean and the person who touches him is to be considered unclean until the evening.

20.1: The entire community of the Israelites entered the Zin wilderness during the first month. The people stayed in Kadesh. Miriam died and was buried there.
20.2: But there was no water for the community, so they gathered together against Moses and Aaron.
20.3: As the people argued with Moses, they told him, "We wish that we had died when our relatives died in the Lord's presence!
20.4: Why did you bring the assembly of the Lord into this wilderness -  So we and our cattle could die here -
20.5: Why did you take us out of Egypt and bring us to this terrible place -  There's no place to plant seeds, fig trees, vines, or pomegranates! And there's no water to drink!"
20.6: Then Moses and Aaron went into the presence of the community at the entrance of the tent of meeting and fell on their faces. Then the glory of the Lord appeared to them.
20.7: The Lord told Moses,
20.8: "Take the rod, gather the community together, and then you and your brother Aaron are to speak to the rock right before their eyes. It will release water. As you bring water to them from the rock, the community and the cattle will be able to drink.
20.9: So Moses took the rod in the Lord's presence, just as he had commanded.
20.10: Then Moses and Aaron gathered the community together in front of the rock. "Pay attention, you rebels!" Moses told them. "Are we to bring you water from this rock?"
20.11: Then Moses raised his hand and struck the rock twice with his rod. Lots of water gushed out, and both the community and their cattle were able to drink.
20.12: But the Lord rebuked Moses and Aaron, telling Moses： "Because you both didn't believe me, because you didn't consecrate me as holy in the presence of the Israelites, you won't be the ones to bring this congregation into the land that I'm about to give them.
20.13: Because the Israelites argued with the Lord and he was set apart among them, this place was called the Meribah Springs.
20.14: Later, Moses sent messengers from Kadesh to the king of Edom with this message： "This is you're your relative Israel says： 'You know all the hardships we've encountered.
20.15: Our ancestors went down to Egypt, where we lived for many years. But the Egyptians treated us and our ancestors viciously.
20.16: Then we cried to the Lord and he heard our voice, sending us a messenger who brought us out of Egypt. "'Now look! We've arrived in Kadesh, a city at the extreme end of your territory.
20.17: Permit us now to pass through your land. We won't pass through your fields or vineyards, and we won't drink waters from your wells. We'll keep to the King's Highway without turning either right or left until we have passed through your territory."
20.18: But Edom replied, "You are not to pass through my land. If you do, I'll come out and start a war with you."
20.19: Then the Israelites replied, "Permit us to travel on the highway. If we and our cattle drink your water, we'll pay the price you ask. Only please let us walk through, and nothing more."
20.20: But still he replied. "No. You're not to pass through." Then Edom went out to meet Moses with a vast army and a lot of military might.
20.21: That's how Edom refused Israel passage through their territory. So Israel turned away from there.
20.22: They traveled from Kadesh, and then the entire community of the Israelites arrived at Mount Hor.
20.23: Then the Lord told Moses and Aaron at Mount Hor, near the territory of Edom,
20.24: "Aaron is to be gathered to his people, since he is not to enter the land that I'm about to give the Israelites. After all, you both rebelled against my command at the Meribah Springs.
20.25: So take Aaron and his son Eleazar and ascend Mount Hor.
20.26: Remove Aaron's vestments and place them on his son Eleazar, because Aaron is to be gathered to his people and die there."
20.27: So Moses did just what the Lord had commanded. They ascended Mount Hor right in front of the entire community.
20.28: As Moses was stripping Aaron's garments from him and clothing Aaron's son Eleazar with them, Aaron died there on top of the mountain. Afterwards, Moses and Eleazar came down from the mountain.
20.29: When the entire community saw that Aaron had died, they mourned in memory of Aaron for 30 days.20

21.1: When the Canaanite king of Arad, who lived in the Negev, heard that Israel was coming along the Atharim caravan route, he fought against Israel and took some of them captive.
21.2: Then Israel made this vow in the Lord's presence： "If you give these people into our control, we intend to devote their cities to total destruction."
21.3: When the Lord heard what Israel had decided to do, he delivered the Canaanites to them, and Israel exterminated them and their cities. They named the place Hormah.
21.4: After this, they traveled from Mount Hor along the caravan route by way of the Sea of Reeds and went around the land of Edom. But when the people got impatient because it was a long route,
21.5: the people complained against the Lord and Moses. "Why did you bring us out of Egypt to die in the wilderness?" they asked. "There's no food and water, and we're tired of this worthless bread."
21.6: In response, the Lord sent poisonous serpents among the people to bite them. As a result, many of people of Israel died.
21.7: Then the people approached Moses and admitted, "We've sinned by speaking against the Lord and you. Pray to the Lord, that he'll remove the serpents from us." So Moses prayed in behalf of the people.
21.8: Then the Lord instructed Moses, "Make a poisonous serpent out of brass and fasten it to a pole. Anyone who has been bitten looks at it will live.
21.9: So Moses made a bronze serpent and fastened it to a pole. If a poisonous serpent bit a person and he looked at it, he lived.
21.10: After this, the Israelites traveled and encamped at Oboth.
21.11: Then they traveled from Oboth and encamped at Iye Abarim, in the wilderness which is in the vicinity of Moab's eastern border.
21.12: From there, they traveled and encamped in the valley of Zered.
21.13: Then from there they traveled to the other side of Arnon and camped in the wilderness that borders the territory of the Amorites. (Arnon borders Moab between Moab and the Amorites,
21.14: which is why the book of the Wars of the Lord reads, "Waheb and Suphah and the wadis of the Arnon,
21.15: and the slope of the valleys, that extends to the dwelling places of Ar and the borders of Moab."
21.16: From there they traveled to the Well of Beer, where the Lord had said instructed Moses, "Gather the people together and I'll give you water.
21.17: That's also where Israel sang this song： Rise up, well! Sing to it!
21.18: It's the well that the leaders dug, the one carved out by the nobles of the people with their scepters and staffs. Then the moved on in the wilderness from there to Mattanah,
21.19: then from Mattanah to Nahaliel, from Nahaliel to Bamoth,
21.20: and from Bamoth to the valley of Moab where their fields are, and from there to the top of Mount Pisgah, that looks down toward the open desert.
21.21: Later, Israel sent messengers to Sihon, king of the Amorites, who conveyed this request：
21.22: "Permit us to pass through your land. We won't trespass in your fields or vineyards. We won't drink water from any well, and we'll only travel along the King's Highway until we've passed through your territory."
21.23: Instead of letting Israel pass through his territory, Sihon mustered his entire army and marched out to meet them in the wilderness. He arrived at Jahaz and attacked Israel.
21.24: But Israel defeated him in battle and took possession of all his lands from Arnon to Jabbok, including the Ammonites, even though the border of the Ammonites was strong.
21.25: So Israel captured all of those cities, occupied all the Amorite cities in Heshbon, and all its towns.
21.26: Heshbon was the capital city of Sihon, king of the Amorites, who fought against the previous king of Moab and captured all his land from his capital city to Arnon.
21.27: Therefore the ones who speak in proverbs say： Come to Heshbon and let it be built! Let the city of Sihon be established!
21.28: A a fire has gone out from Heshbon, And a flame from the city of Sihon. It consumed Ar of Moab and the lords of the high places who lived in Arnon.
21.29: Woe to you, Moab! You are destroyed, you people of Chemosh! He has given up his sons as fugitives and his daughters have gone into captivity to Sihon, king of the Amorites.
21.30: We've fired at them. Heshbon has perished as far as Dibon. We've destroyed them as far as Nophah even as far as Medeba.
21.31: So Israel lived in Amorite territory.
21.32: Then Moses sent out explorers to scout Jazer. They captured its towns and drove out the Amorites who were there.
21.33: Then they turned toward Bashan. However, Og, the king of Bashan mustered his army and went out to attack them at Edrei.
21.34: The Lord told Moses, "You are not to fear him, because I'm going to deliver him, his entire army, and his land into your control. Do to him just what you've done to Sihon, king of the Amorites, who used to live in Heshbon."
21.35: So they attacked him, his sons, and his entire army, until there wasn't even a single survivor left. Then they took possession of his land.

22.1: The Israelites continued their travels, eventually encamping on the plains of Moab beside the Jordan River opposite Jericho.
22.2: Zippor's son Balak saw everything that Israel had done to the Amorites.
22.3: As a result, Moab greatly feared the people, because they were so numerous. Because a sense of impending doom was afflicting the Moabites as they faced the Israelites,
22.4: the Moabites said to the elders of Midian, "This horde of people is about to lick up everything around us, like an ox licks up the green ground." At that time, Zippor's son Balak was the king of Moab.
22.5: He sent messengers to Beor's son Balaam in Pethor, near the Jordan River, the land where the descendants of his people originated, to summon his aid. He said, "Look! A group of people have escaped from Egypt. They cover the surface of the whole earth, and are sitting here right in front of me.
22.6: So come right now and curse this people for me, because there are too many of them for me to handle. Perhaps I'll be able to strike them down and drive them out of the land, since I know that whomever you bless is blessed and whomever you curse is cursed."
22.7: So the elders of Moab and Midian left to visit Balaam, bringing an honorarium with them, and communicated Balak's concerns to him.
22.8: In answer, Balaam told them, "Stay here for the night and I'll bring back a message to you, depending on what the Lord says to me." So the officers of Moab stayed with Balaam overnight.
22.9: God visited Balaam and asked him, "Who are these men with you?"
22.10: Then Balaam said to God： Zippor's son Balak, king of Moab, sent them to me and said,
22.11: "Look! A group of people have escaped from Egypt. They cover the surface of the whole earth! So come right now and curse them for me. Perhaps I'll be able to fight against them and drive them out."
22.12: But God said to Balaam, "Don't go with them. Don't curse the people, because they're blessed."
22.13: So Balaam got up the next morning and told Balak's officials, "Go back to your homeland, because the Lord has refused me permission to go with you."
22.14: So Balak's officials  got up, returned to Balak and reported, "Balaam refused to come with us."
22.15: In response, Balak sent more officers - higher ranking ones, at that! -
22.16: who approached Balaam with this message： "This is what Zippor's son Balak says： 'Don't let anything get in the way of your coming to me.
22.17: I'm determined to reward you generously, and I'll do  everything you tell me to do. So come right away and and curse this people for me."
22.18: Balaam responded to Balak's entourage by saying, "Even if Balak were to give me his house full of silver and gold, I won't doublecross the command of the Lord my God in even the slightest way.
22.19: Meanwhile, stay here overnight so I may learn what the Lord might say to me."
22.20: God came to visit Balaam that same night and told him, "If the men come to call on you, get up and go with them, but be sure to do only what I tell you to do."
22.21: The next morning, Balaam got up, saddled his donkey, and started to leave, accompanied by the Moabite officials.
22.22: At this, the anger of the Lord flared up against Balaam, because he was leaving. So the angel of the Lord stood in the way to oppose him. As Balaam was riding his donkey, accompanied by two of his servants,
22.23: all of a sudden the donkey saw the angel of the Lord standing in the way, with an unsheathed sword in his hand! The donkey turned off the road and went into an open field. Balaam started beating the donkey in order to turn her back to the road,
22.24: but the angel of the Lord stood on a narrow path that crossed the vineyards. It had walls on both sides of the path.
22.25: When the donkey saw the angel of the Lord, she squeezed herself so close to the wall that Balaam's foot was pressed to the wall. So he beat her again!
22.26: Then the angel of the Lord went along a little further and stood in a much narrower space, where it was impossible to turn either right or left.
22.27: When the donkey saw the angel of the Lord, she crouched down under Balaam. As a result, Balaam got so angry that he started to whip the donkey with his staff.22
22.28: That's when the Lord enabled the donkey to speak. She asked Balaam, "What did I do to you that you would beat me in the space of only three footsteps?"
22.29: "Because you're playing a dirty trick on me," Balaam answered the donkey. "If only I had a sword in my hand! I'd kill you right now!"
22.30: But in response, the donkey asked Balaam, "I'm your donkey that you've ridden on in the past without incident, am I not, and I'm the same donkey you're riding on right now, am I not -  Am I in the habit of treating you like this?" "No," he admitted.
22.31: Then the Lord enabled Balaam to see, so he observed the angel of the Lord standing in the way, with an unsheathed sword in his hand. So he bowed down and prostrated himself on his face.
22.32: Then the angel of the Lord asked him, "Why did you beat your donkey in the space of only three footsteps -  I've come to oppose you, because I say that what you're doing is perverted.
22.33: The donkey saw me and turned in front of me in the space of those three footsteps.
22.34: If she hadn't turn away from me, I would have killed you by now and left her alive!" At this, Balaam replied to the angel of the Lord, "I've sinned! I didn't know that you were standing to meet me on the road. So now, since it displeases you, let me go back."
22.35: But the angel of the Lord told Balaam, "Go with the men, but deliver only the message that I'm going to give you." So Balaam went with Balak's officials.
22.36: When Balak heard that Balaam had arrived, he went out to meet him in the city of Moab on the border of Arnon at the extreme end of his territory.
22.37: Balak asked Balaam, "Didn't I repeatedly send for you to summon you -  Why didn't you come to me -  I can pay you well, can't I?"
22.38: Balaam answered Balak, "Well, I'm here now. I've come to you, but I can't just say anything, can I -  I'll speak only what God puts in my mouth to say."
22.39: So accompanied by Balaam and Balak's officials, Balak traveled to Kiriath-huzoth,
22.40: where he sacrificed oxen and sheep.
22.41: The next day, Balak brought Balaam up to Bamoth-baal, where he could see part of the community of Israel.

23.1: Balaam told Balak, "Build for me here seven altars and prepare here for me seven bulls and seven rams.
23.2: So Balak did just as Balaam instructed. Balak and Balaam offered a bull and a ram on each altar.
23.3: Then Balaam instructed Balak, "Stand by your offering and leave me alone by myself. Perhaps the Lord will come to meet me. I'll tell you whatever he reveals to me." And so he went to a high place,
23.4: where the Lord met with Balaam, who told to him, "I've prepared seven altars and offered bulls and rams on an altar."
23.5: Then the Lord gave Balaam this message. "Return to Balak and speak to him."
23.6: So Balaam returned to where Balaak had been standing, that is, next to his offerings, accompanied by all the Moabite officials.
23.7: Then Balaam uttered this prophetic statement： "King Balak of Moab brought me from Aram, from the eastern mountains, and told me, 'Come and curse Jacob for me. Come and curse Israel.'
23.8: But how can I curse those whom God hasn't cursed -  How can I denounce those whom the Lord has not denounced -
23.9: I saw them from the top of the rocks. I watched them from the hills Truly this is a people that lives by itself and doesn't matter among the nations.
23.10: Who can count the dust of Jacob -  Who can number the dust of Israel -  Let me die the death of the righteous, and may I end up like him."
23.11: "What are you doing to me?" Balak asked Balaam. "I brought you to curse my enemies, not pronounce a blessing!"
23.12: But in response, Balaam asked, "Shouldn't I be careful to communicate only what the Lord puts in my mouth?"
23.13: "Come with me to another place where you can see them," Balak replied. "You'll only see a portion of them, because you won't be able to see them completely. Come and curse them from there for me."
23.14: So Balak took him to the field of Zophim, and from there to the top of Mount Pisgah, where he built seven altars and then offered a bull and a ram on each altar.
23.15: Then he said to Balak, "Stand by your offering while I go alone to meet the Lord."14
23.16: Then the Lord met with Balaam and gave a message to him. "Now go back to Balak and speak to him."
23.17: So Balaam returned to where Balak had been standing, that is, next to his offerings, accompanied by the Moabite officials. "What did the Lord say?" Balak asked him.
23.18: In response, Balaam uttered this prophetic statement:23 "Stand up, Balak, and pay attention! Listen to me, you son of Zippor!
23.19: God is not a human male - he doesn't lie, nor is he a human being - he never vacilates. Once he speaks up, He's going to act, won't he -  Once he makes a promise, He'll fulfill it, won't he -
23.20: Look! I've received a blessing, and so I will bless. I won't withdraw it.
23.21: He has not responded to iniquity in Jacob or gazed at mischief in Israel. The Lord his God is with them, And the triumphant cry of a king is among them.
23.22: From Egypt God brought them - his strength was like a wild ox!
23.23: No Satanic plan against Jacob nor divination against Israel can ever prevail. When the time is right, it shall be asked about Jacob and Israel, 'What has God accomplished?'
23.24: Look! The people are like a lions. Like the lion, he rises up! He does not lie down again until he has consumed his prey and drunk the blood of the slain."
23.25: Then Balak said to Balaam, "Don't curse them or bless them!"
23.26: Didn't I tell you," Balaam responded to Balak, "that I'll say whatever that the Lord tells me to say?"
23.27: So Balak exhorted Balaam, "Let's go right now! I'll take you to another place. Maybe God will  agree to have you a curse from there for me."
23.28: So Balak took Balaam to the top of Mount Peor, which overlooks the open wilderness.
23.29: Balaam told Balak, "Build seven altars for me right here. Then prepare seven bulls and seven rams."
23.30: Balak did just what Balaam had instructed  - he offered a bull and a ram on each altar.

24.1: When Balaam noticed that the Lord was pleased that Balaam was blessing Israel, he didn't behave as he had time after time before, that is, to practice divination. Instead, he turned with his face to the wilderness,
24.2: looked up, and saw Israel encamped in their respective tribal order. Just then, the spirit of God came upon him.
24.3: Balaam uttered this prophetic statement： "A declaration by Beor's son Balaam, a declaration by the strong, blind man.
24.4: A declaration from one who hears what God has to say, who saw the vision that the Almighty revealed, who keeps stumbling, with open eyes.
24.5: Jacob, your tents are so fine, as well as your dwelling places, Israel!
24.6: They're spread out ike valleys, like gardens along river banks, like aloe planted by the Lord, or like cedars beside water.
24.7: He will pour water from his buckets; and his descendants will stream forth like abundant water. His king will be more exalted than Agag when he exalts his own kingdom.
24.8: God is bringing them out of Egypt, with the strength of an ox. He'll devour enemy nations, break their bones, and impale them with arrows.
24.9: He crouches, laying low like a lion. Who would awaken him -  Those who bless you are blessed, and those who curse you are cursed.
24.10: Balak flew into a rage and he started hitting his fists together. "I called you to curse my enemies," he yelled at Balaam. "But look here! You've blessed them three times!
24.11: Now get out of here! I had promised you that I would definitely honor you, but now the Lord has kept me from doing that!"
24.12: But Balaam asked Balak, "I told your messengers,
24.13: 'Even if Balak gives me his palace full of silver and gold, I won't doublecross the command of the Lord and do anything - where good or evil - on my own initiative, because I'm going to say whatever the Lord says.'
24.14: Meanwhile, since I have to return to my people, come and listen while I tell you what this people will be doing to your people in the last days."13
24.15: Then Balaam uttered this prophetic statement： "The declaration by Beor's son Balaam, a declaration by the strong, blind man.
24.16: A declaration from one who hears what God has to say, who knows what the Most High knows, who saw the vision that the Almighty revealed, who keeps stumbling with open eyes.
24.17: I can see him, but not right now. I observe him, but from a distance. A star streams forth from Jacob; a scepter arises from Israel. He will crush Moab's forehead, along with all of Seth's descendants.24
24.18: Edom will be a conquered nation and Seir will be Israel's defeated foe, while Israel performs valiantly.
24.19: He will rule over Jacob, annihilating those who survive in the city.
24.20: Next, Balaam looked directly at Amalek and then uttered this prophetic statement： Even though Amalek is an international leader, his future is permanent destruction.
24.21: Balaam also uttered this prophetic statement about the Kenites： Your dwelling places are stable, because your nest is carved in solid rock.
24.22: Nevertheless, Kain will be incinerated. How long will it take until Asshur takes you hostage -
24.23: Finally, he uttered this prophetic statement： "Ah, who can live, unless God makes it happen -
24.24: Ships under control of Kittim will devastate Asshur and Eber, until they are permanently destroyed.
24.25: Then Balaam got up, returned to his country, and Balak went on his way.

25.1: While Israel remained encamped in Shittim, the people began to commit sexual immorality with Moabite women,
25.2: who also invited the people to the sacrifices of their gods. So the people ate and worshipped their gods.
25.3: The people joined the Baal-Peor cult. As a result, the anger of the Lord flared up against Israel,
25.4: so the Lord told Moses, "Take all the leaders of the people and execute them in broad daylight for the Lord, so the Lord's burning anger may be withdrawn from Israel."
25.5: Then Moses ordereed the judges of Israel, "Each one of you is to execute the men in his own tribe who joined the Baal-Peor cult."
25.6: That very moment, one of the Israelites arrived, bringing to his brothers one of the Midianite women, right in front of Moses and the entire community of Israel, while they were weeping at the entrance of the tent of meeting!
25.7: When Eleazar's son Phinehas, grandson of Aaron the priest saw this, he jumped up from the middle of the community, grabbged a javelin in his hand,
25.8: followed the Israelite man inside his tent and impaled the two of them - the Israel man and the woman - right through both of them and into her abdomen. Then the plague infecting the Israelites was brought to a halt. Nevertheless,- 24,000 people died because of the plague.
25.11: "Eleazar's son Phinehas, grandson of Aaron the priest, has turned my wrath away from Israel. Because his zealousness reflected my own zeal for them, I didn't consume Israel in my jealousy.
25.12: Therefore, I'm certainly going to be giving him my covenant of peace,
25.13: for him and for his descendants after him, too, a covenant of perpetual priesthood, because he was zealous for his God and made atonement for the Israelites."
25.14: Now the name of the Israelite man who was slain, along with the Midianite woman, was Salu's son Zimri, a leader from the tribe of Simeon.
25.15: The name of the woman who was slain, that is, the Midianite woman, was named Cozbi. She was the daughter of Zur, a leader of one of the ancestral houses of Midian.
25.18: because they've acted deceitfully, bringing trouble to you when in this incident at Peor with Cozbi, daughter of a Midian prince, who was killed during the plague that came about because of the incident at Peor."
