37.26: I'll make a secure covenant with them, one that will last forever. I will establish them, make them increase, and will place my sanctuary in their midst forever.
37.27: I will pitch my tent among them and will be their God. They will be my people,
37.28: and the nations will know that I, the Lord, am the sanctifier of Israel when I place my sanctuary in their midst forever."'"

38.1: The word of the Lord came to me：
38.2: "Son of Man, turn your attention toward Gog, from the land of Magog, the chief noble of Meshech, and Tubal. Prophesy this against him:
38.3: 'This is what the Lord God says： "Watch out! I'm coming after you, Gog, chief ruler of Rosh, Meshech, and Tubal.
38.4: I'm going to turn you around, put hooks into your jaws, and bring you out - you and your whole army - along with you're your horses and cavalry riders, all of them richly attired, a magnificent company replete with buckler and shield, and all of them wielding battle swords.
38.5: Persia, Cush, and Lybia will be accompanying them, all of them equipped with shields and helmets.
38.6: Gomer with all its troops, and the household of Togarmah from the remotest parts of the north with all its troops-many people will accompany you.
38.7: Be prepared. Yes, prepare yourself-you and all of your many batallions that have gathered together around you to protect you.
38.8: "Many days from now - in the latter years-you will be summoned to a land that has been restored from violence. You will be gathered from many nations to the mountains of Israel, which formerly had been a continual waste, but which will be populated with people who have been brought back from the nations. All of them will be living there securely.
38.9: You'll arise suddenly, like a tornado, coming like a windstorm to cover the land, you and all your soldiers with you, along with many nations.
38.10: "This is what the Lord God says： 'This is what's going to happen on that day you begin your invasion： You'll be thinking in your heart, making evil plans,
38.11: and boasting, "I'm going to invade a land comprised of open country that is at rest, living in confidence, all of whose inhabitants will be living securely with neither fortification nor bars on their doors.
38.12: I'm going to confiscate anything I can put my hands on. I'll attack the restored ruins and the people who have been gathered together from the nations, who are acquiring livestock and other goods, and who live at the center of the world's attention.
38.13: Businessmen based in Sheba, Dedan, Tarshish, and all of its growling lions will ask you, "Are you coming for war spoils?Have you assembled your armies to carry off silver and gold, and to gather lots of war booty?'
38.14: "Therefore, Son of Man, prophesy to Gog and tell him, 'This is what the Lord God says： "When the day comes on which my people are living securely, won't you be aware of it -
38.15: You'll come in from your home in the remotest parts of the north. You'll come with many nations, all of them riding along on horses. You'll be a hugh, combined army.
38.16: You'll come up to invade my people Israel like a a storm cloud to cover the land. In the last days, Gog, I'll bring you up to invade my land so that the world will learn to know me when I show them how holy I am before their very eyes."'"
38.17: "This is what the Lord God says： 'Surely you're the one about whom I spoke years ago in the writings of my servants, Israel's prophets, aren't you?They predicted back then that I would bring you up after many years, didn't they -
38.18: So it will be that on that day, when Gog invades the land of Israel,' declares the Lord God, 'my zeal will ignite my anger.
38.19: In my zealousness and in my burning anger, on that day there will be a massive earthquake throughout the land of Israel.
38.20: I'm going to shake the fish in the sea, the birds in the sky, the wild beasts, all the creatures that crawl on the earth, and every single human being who lives on the surface of the earth. Mountains will collapse, as will their mountain passages, and every wall will fall to the ground.
38.21: Then I'll call for war against Gog on top of every mountain,' declares the Lord God, 'and every weapon of war will be turned against their fellow soldier.
38.22: I'll judge them with disease and bloodshed. I'll shower him, his soldiers, and the vast army that accompanies him a torrential flood, hailstones, fire, and sulfur.
38.23: I will exalt myself and demonstrate my holiness, making myself known to many people, who shall learn that I am the Lord.'"

39.1: "Now as for you, Son of Man, prophesy against Gog and tell him, '"This is what the Lord God has to say： 'Watch out, Gog, you leader of the head of Meshech and Tubal!
39.2: I'm going to turn you around, drag you along to your destruction, and bring you up from the farthest parts of the north, and carry you to the mountains of Israel.
39.3: There I will strike your bow from your left hand and your arrows from your right, causing your fall.
39.4: You will collapse on the mountains of Israel, along with all of your soldiers and the nations that have accompanied you. There the raptors, vultures, and wild animals will feed on you.
39.5: You will fall dead in the open fields, because I have ordered this to happen,' declares the Lord God.
39.6: 'I'm also going to set fire to Magog, along with those who are settled down and at home in the islands. That's when they'll learn that I am the Lord.
39.7: I'll make my holiness and reputation known in the midst of my people Israel, and I won't let my holiness be profaned anymore. The nations will learn that I, the Lord, am holy in the midst of Israel.
39.8: Pay attention! It's coming and will certainly happen,' declares the Lord God. 'This shall be the day about which I've spoken!'"'"
39.9: "After all this happens, the people who live in the cities of Israel will be kindling fires for seven years, using small shields, large shields, bows, arrows, clubs, personal weapons, and spears to do so.
39.10: They won't need to cut down trees from the fields nor gather firewood from the forests, because they will light fires with the weapons as they plunder the plunderers and loot the looters!" declares the Lord God.
39.11: "When all of this happens, I'm going to set aside a grave site for Gog in Israel's Traveler's Valley, near the approach to the Dead Sea. She will block off everyone who tries to bypass it. There they will bury Gog, and rename the area "Valley of Gog's Gang".
39.12: "The house of Israel will be burying them for seven months in order to purify the land.
39.13: Everyone in the land will be involved in the burials, and this will serve as a reminder for them that I have glorified myself," declares the Lord God.
39.14: "Men will be assigned to travel continuously throughout the land, exploring for seven full months as they go about burying the bodies that remain from the battle on the surface of the ground, so that the land may be sterilized.
39.15: As scouts go searching throughout the land, whenever they see someone's bones, they will place a sign beside the remains until the remains have been buried in the Valley of Gog's Gang.
39.16: They'll also name the city that is there 'Hamonah,' as they purify the land.
39.17: "Now as for you, Son of Man, this is what the Lord God has to say： 'Tell all of the birds and wild beasts to come and gather together and participate in the sacrifice that I'm going to make for you. This great sacrifice will take place on the mountains of Israel, where you'll be eating flesh and drinking blood.
39.18: You'll eat the flesh of mighty men and drink the blood of the world's princes, drinking the blood of these rams, lambs, goats, bulls, all of them fattened as if they're from Bashan, fit for slaughter.
39.19: You'll eat until you're fat and glutted. You'll drink blood until you're drunk from the sacrifice that I'm going to make for all of you.
39.20: You'll be fully satiated at my table, dining on horse flesh, horsemen, elite soldiers, and every kind of warrior," declares the Lord God.
39.21: "I'm going to display my glory among the people, and every nation will see the judgment that I administer by my own hand among them.
39.22: The house of Israel will learn that I am the Lord their God from that day forward!
39.23: The nations will also learn that because of Israel's sin the house of Israel went into captivity due to their unfaithful behavior against me. As a result, I hid my presence from them, turned them over to the control of their enemies, and they died by violence.
39.24: It was because of their defilement and transgression that I treated them this way by hiding my presence from them."
39.25: "Therefore this is what the Lord God has to say： 'I'm going to restore the resources of Jacob and show mercy to the entire house of Israel. I'll be zealous for my own reputation and for my holiness.
39.26: They'll forget their shame and all of their unfaithfulness by which they behaved so unfaithfully toward me. They will live on their land in confidence, not in fear.
39.27: When I bring them back from the nations and gather them together from the lands that belong to their enemies, I will demonstrate my holiness through them right in front of the eyes of the world,
39.28: and they will learn that I am the Lord their God, who sent them into exile and who gathered them back to their land. I will not leave any of them remaining in exile.
39.29: I'll no longer hide my presence from them again when I pour out my Spirit on the house of Israel," declares the Lord God.

40.1: At the beginning of year 25 of our captivity, on the tenth day of the fourteenth year after the destruction of Jerusalem-on that very day-the Lord grabbed me in his hand and took me there.
40.2: God brought me in a series of visions to the land of Israel and placed me on top of a very high mountin, where to the south there was something that looked like the outline of a city.
40.3: That's where he took me. All of a sudden, there was a man whose appearance resembled glowing bronze. He had a measuring rod and line in his hand as he stood in the city gate.
40.4: This is what the man told me： "Son of Man, watch carefully, listen closely, and remember everything I'm going to be showing you, because you've been brought here to be shown what you're about to see. Be sure that you tell the house of Israel everything that you observe."
40.5: All of a sudden, we were at the exterior wall that completely surrounded the temple. The man whom I had observed held a measuring rod that was ten and a half feet long as measured in cubits that were a cubit and a handbredth long. As he measured the thickness of the wall, he measured out one rod. Its height was also one rod.
40.6: Then he went over to the gate that faced toward the east, ascended its steps, and measured its thresholds. One threshold measured one rod and the other one measured one rod.
40.7: Each guardhouse measured one rod long and one rod wide, and the distance between each guardhouse was five cubits. The threshold of the gate near the vestibule facing away from the temple entrance measured one rod.
40.8: Next he measured the vestibule of the gate facing away from the temple entrance at one rod.
40.9: He measured the vestibule of the gate inside at eight cubits and the doorjambs at two cubits. (The vestibule at the gate faced away from the temple.)
40.10: Gate guardhouses stood facing east, numbereding three on each side, each of them of equal size to the door jamb; that is, having the same measurement on each side.
40.11: He measured the width of the gateway at ten cubits, and the length of the gate at thirteen cubits.
40.12: The retaining wall in front of the guardhouses measured one cubit wide. It stood one cubit from the wall to the guardhouses, which were six cubits square.
40.13: He measured the gate from the roof of the guardhouses to the roof of another at 25 cubits from doorway to opposite doorway.
40.14: Then he measured the open air porch at 60 cubits from the doorjamb of the courtyard the encompassed the gate.
40.15: The distance from the front entrance gate to the vestibule of the inner gate measured 50 cubits.
40.16: Latticed windows faced the guardhouses, their side pillars within the gate all around, and also for the porches. Windows were placed all around inside, and the side pillars were engraved with palm trees.
40.17: Next, he brought me into the outer court, where chambers and a paved area had been constructed all around the courtyard, with 30 chambers facing the pavement.
40.18: The pavement to the side of the gates corresponded to the length of the gates.
40.19: He also measured the width from the front lower gate to the front of the exterior inner court at 100 cubits to the east and to the north.
40.20: Next, he measured the length and width of the outer north-facing gate to the courtyard.
40.21: It was equipped with three guardhouses on each side. Its side pillars and porches had measurements identical to the first gate： 50 cubits long and 25 cubits wide.
40.22: Its windows, porches, and palm tree ornaments had measurements identical to the east-facing gate. Reached by seven ascending steps, its porch lay to the front of the steps.
40.23: From a gate that stood opposite the northern gate he measured 100 cubits, as well as from the eastern gate.
40.24: Then he led me toward the south, where there was a gate with side pillar and porch measurements identical to the others.
40.25: The gate and its porches contained windows all around, identical to the other windows. The length of the porch was 50 cubits and its width was 25 cubits.
40.26: Seven steps led up to it, with a porch in front of them. Palm tree ornaments were engraved on its side pillars, one on each side.
40.27: The inner court contained a south-facing gate measuring 100 cubits from gate to gate toward the south.
40.28: Next, he brought me to the inner courtyard by way of the south-facing gate. He measured the south-facing gate as having measurements identical to the others.
40.29: The measurements of its guardhouses, its side pillars, and its porches were identical to the others. The gate and its porches contained windows all around. The length of the porch was 50 cubits and its width was 25 cubits.
40.30: Porches lay all around, measuring 25 cubits long and 5 cubits wide,
40.31: leading to the outer courtyard. Palm tree ornaments were engraved on its side pillars. The stairway leading to it contained eight steps.
40.32: Then he brought me into the inner east-facing courtyard, where he measured the gate, identical to the others.
40.33: The measurement of its guardhouses, side pillars, and porches was identical to the others. The gate and its porches contained windows all around. The length of the porch was 50 cubits and its width was 25 cubits,
40.34: leading to the outer courtyard. Palm tree ornaments were engraved on its side pillars. The stairway leading to it contained eight steps.
40.35: Next, he brought me to the north-facing gate, where he measured the gate, identical to the others.
40.36: The measurement of its guardhouses, side pillars, and porches was identical to the others. The gate and its porches contained windows all around. The length of the porch was 50 cubits and its width was 25 cubits,
40.37: leading to the outer courtyard. Palm tree ornaments were engraved on its side pillars. The stairway leading to it contained eight steps.
40.38: There was a chamber with a doorway by the side pillars next to the gate where they prepare the burnt offerings.
40.39: In the porch leading in front of the gate there were two tables on either side for slaughtering burnt offerings, sin offerings, and guilt offerings,
40.40: and on the outer side, approaching the northern gateway, there were two tables, as well two tables as on the opposite side of the porch in front of the gate.
40.41: Thus there were four tables on each side in front of the gate, for a total of eight tables for use in slaughtering the offerings.
40.42: There were four tables carved from stone for the burnt offering, each one and a half cubits long, one and a half cubits high, and one cubit high, on which the instruments are laid for slaughtering burnt offerings and sacrifices.
40.43: Double hooks, a single handbredth in length, were installed all around in this portion of the temple area.
40.44: From outside leading into the inner gate there were chambers for the choir. One was beside the north gate facing the south, and another was at the south gate facing the north.
40.45: The angel told me, "This south-facing chamber is for the priests who maintain the temple,
40.46: and the north-facing chamber is for the priests who maintain the altar. These are Zadok's descendants, who, as descendants of Levi approach the Lord to minister directly to him."
40.47: He measured the court in the form of a square at 100 cubits long and 100 cubits wide. The altar stood in front of the temple.
40.48: Next, he brought me to the temple porch and measured the side pillars at five cubits on each side. The width of the gate measured three cubits on each side.
40.49: The porch was 20 cubits long and eleven cubits wide. The stairway by which it was ascended was equipped with columns attached to its side pillars, one on each side.

41.1: Next he brought me to the temple and measured its door jambs at six cubits wide on each side of the structure.
41.2: The entrance was ten cubits wide and its door jambs were five cubits wide on each side. He measured the length of the nave at 40 cubits and its width at 20 cubits.
41.3: Then he went inside and measured the door jambs at two cubits wide and the doorway at six cubits high. The doorway was seven cubits wide.
41.4: He measured its length at 20 cubits, its width at 20 cubits in front of the structure, and then he told me, "This is the most holy area."
41.5: Next, he measured the temple walls at six cubits high and the wide of the side chambers at four cubits around all four sides of the temple.
41.6: The side chambers consisted of three stories, each above the other, with 30 chambers in each story. The side chambers extended out from the wall that faced the inside of the chambers where the chambers were fastened together, but the chamber walls were not fastened directly into the temple walls itself.
41.7: The side chambers surrounding the temple were wider at each successive story, because the surrounding structure ascended by proportional increments as it rose, making ascent to the highest story by going up successively from the lowest.
41.8: I observed a raised platform that surrounded the temple, and the foundations of the side chambers were a full six cubits deep.
41.9: The outer wall of the side chambers was five cubits thick, and there was an empty space between the temple's side chambers
41.10: and its outer chambers 20 cubits in width, surrounding the temple on each side.
41.11: The side chamber door ways facing the free space contained a single north-facing doorway and a second south-facing doorway. The width of the free space was five cubits all around the perimeter.
41.12: The building that faced the west side of the courtyard was 70 cubits wide, and the building's wall was five cubits thick all around. It was 90 cubits long.
41.13: Then he measured the temple. It was 100 cubits long, and the courtyard, its building, and its walls were 100 cubits long.
41.14: The front of the temple and its east-facing courtyard were each 100 cubits long.
41.15: Next, he measured 100 cubits as the length of the structure toward the front of the courtyard that stood behind it, where it housed a gallery on each side of it. Then he measured the temple and the inner porticos of the courtyard,
41.16: the thresholds, the shielded windows, and the surrounding three-storied galleries that stood opposite. From the ground to the shielded windows, they were paneled with wood all around,
41.17: including up to the doorway, up to the temple (both within and without) and all around both sides of the inner wall, according to his measurement.
41.18: There were carved cherubim and palm trees, alternating with palm tree between cherub, and each cherub had two faces,
41.19: with a human face looking toward the palm tree on one side and a young lion's face looking toward the palm tree on the other side. These carvings extended all the way around the temple,
41.20: from the ground to above the doorway, as well as on the walls of the main sanctuary.
41.21: The door posts of the main sanctuary were square. Each door post was identical in appearance to any other.
41.22: The altar was made of wood, three cubits high and two cubits long. Its corners, base, and wides were of wood. He told me, "This table stands in the Lord's presence."
41.23: The nave and the sanctuary each were equipped with double doors.
41.24: Each door had two sections mounted on hinges, for a total of two sections for one door and two sections for the other.
41.25: The doors of the nave had carvings engraved on them, consisting of cherubim and palm trees identical to those on the walls. The front of the exterior porch was equipped with a wooden threshold.
41.26: Shielded windows and palm trees were visible on both sides; that is, on the sides of the porch, the side chambers of the temple, and on its thresholds.

42.1: Then he brought me to the outer, north-facing courtyard into the chamber that stood opposite the structure that was facing north.
42.2: It stood 100 cubits long and 50 cubits wide, with a door in the middle.1
42.3: Opposite the 20 cubits that pertained to the inner court, and opposite the paved area that pertained to the outer court, there were three stories of galleries that faced each other.
42.4: In front of the chambers there was an inner walkway ten cubits wide and 100 cubits wide, the openings to which were on the north.
42.5: The upper chambers were narrower, since the galleries required more space than did the lower and middle portions of the building.
42.6: The three part structure had no columns, unlike the courts, which is why the upper chambers were offset from the ground upward, more so than the lower and middle chambers.
42.7: The outer wall by the side of the chambers toward the outer court and facing the chambers was 50 cubits long.
42.8: While the chambers in the outer court were 50 cubits in length, the chambers facing the temple were 100 cubits long.
42.9: Below these chambers, as one might enter from the outer court, was the east side entrance.
42.10: There were chambers built into the thick part of the wall of the court facing the east; that is, facing the separate area toward the front of the building,
42.11: with a passage way in front of them, similar in appearance to the chambers that were on the north, proportional to their length and width, with all of their exits according to their arrangements and doorways.
42.12: Corresponding to the chamber doorways facing the south was an opening at the beginning of the passage; that is, the passage in front of the corresponding part of the wall facing east as one might enter.
42.13: Then he told me, "The north and south chamber, which are opposite the courtyard are consecrated areas where the priests who approach the Lord will eat consecrated offerings and lay the consecrated grain offerings, sin offerings, and guilt offerings, because the area is holy.
42.14: When the priest enters, they will not enter the out court from the sanctuary without having removed their garments worn during their time of ministry, because they are holy. They will clothe themselves with alternate garments, and then they will approach the area reserved for the people."
42.15: After he had finished measuring the inner temple, he brought me out by way of the east facing gate and measured it all around.
42.16: He measured the east side at 500 reeds, according to the length of the measuring stick,
42.17: the north side at 500 reeds, according to the length of the measuring stick,
42.18: the south side at 500 reeds, according to the length of the measuring stick,
42.19: and the west side at 500 reeds, according to the length of the measuring stick.
42.20: He measured a wall that encompassed all four sides, 500 hundred long and 500 wide, dividing between the sacred and the common.

43.1: Next, he brought me to the east facing gate,
43.2: and the glory of the God of Israel was coming from the east. His voice sounded like roaring water, and the land shimmered from his glory.
43.3: His appearance in the vision that I was having was similar to what I observed in the vision where he had come to destroy the city, and also like the visions that I saw by the Chebar River. I fell flat on my face
43.4: while the glory of the Lord entered the tample by way of the east-facing gate.
43.5: Just then, the Spirit lifted me up and carried me into the inner courtyard, where the glory of the Lord was filling the temple!
43.6: I heard someone speaking to me from the temple, and a man appeared, standing beside me!
43.7: "Son of Man," the Lord God told me, "This is where my throne is, where I place the soles of my feet, and where I will live among the Israelites forever. The house of Israel will no longer defile my holy name-neither they nor their kings - by their unfaithfulness, by the lifeless idols of their kings on their funeral mounds,
43.8: by their setting up their threshold too close to my threshold and their door post too close to my door post, with a wall between me and them. After all, they have defiled my holy name by their loathsome things that they did, so I devoured them in my anger.
43.9: But now let them send their unfaithfulness and lifeless idols of their kings far away from me, and I will live among them forever."
43.10: "And now, Son of Man, describe the temple to the house of Israel. They will be ashamed because of their sin. They will measure its pattern.
43.11: If they are ashamed of everything that they've done, you are to reveal to them the design of the temple, its structure, its exits and entrances, its plans, its ordinances, and all of its regulations. Write it down where they can see it and remember all of its designs and regulations, so they will implement them.
43.12: This is to be the regulation for the temple： the entire area on top of the mountain is to be considered wholly consecrated. This is to be the law of the temple."11
43.13: "Here are the measurements of the altar in cubits that were a cubit and a handbreadth long： its base is a cubit long and a cubit wide, and its border around the edge at one handbreadth is to be the height of the altar.
43.14: From the base on the ground to its lower edge is to be two cubits, with its width to be one cubit. From the lesser ledge to the larger edge is to be four cubits. Its width is to be one cubit.13
43.15: "The hearth is to be four cubits high, and four horns are to extend upwards from the hearth.
43.16: "The hearth is to be twelve cubits long and twelve cubits wide; that is, it will be a four-sided square.
43.17: It is to have a ledge fourteen cubits long by fourteen cubits wide around the four sides. Its border is to be half a cubit and its base is to be a cubit all around, with its steps facing east."
43.18: Then he said to me, "This is what the Lord God says： 'These are the regulations for the altar, starting the day that it is constructed for presenting burnt offerings and sprinkling blood.
43.19: You are to present to the Levitical priests, Zadok's descendants, who will approach me to serve me, a young bull for a sin offering,' declares the Lord God.
43.20: 'You are to take some of its blood and put it on the four horns of the altar, on its four corners of the ledge, and on the border that surrounds it, thus cleansing it and making an atonement for it.
43.21: You are also to present a bull for a sin offering, incincerating it in the appointed place at the temple, outside the sanctuary.
43.22: "The second day following commencement of offerings, you are to offer a male goat without defect for a sin offering to cleanse the altar the same way they cleansed it with the bull.
43.23: After you've finished the cleansing, you are to present a young bull without defect and a ram from the flock without defect.
43.24: You are to present them in the Lord's presence, and the priests are to throw salt on them and then present them as a burnt offering to the Lord.
43.25: "Every day for a week, you are to prepare a goat for a sin offering, a young bull, and a ram from the flock, each without defect.
43.26: For a seven day period they are to make atonement for the altar, purifying it and consecrating it.
43.27: When they will have completed this period, from the eighth day following the priests are to offer your burnt offerings on the altar, along with your peace offerings, and I will accept you,' declares the Lord God."

44.1: Then the Lord God brought me back by way of the east-facing outer gate of the sanctuary. But it was shut.
44.2: The Lord said to me, "This gate is to remain shut. It will not be opened. No man is to enter in by it, because the Lord God of Israel entered in by it, so it is to remain shut.
44.3: The Regent Prince will be seated there, as Regent Prince, and will dine in the Lord's presence, entering by way of the portico of the gate and exiting by the same way."
44.4: Then he brought me by way of the north-facing gate to the front of the temple. As I looked, the glory of the Lord filled the Lord's temple, and I fell flat on my face!
44.5: Then the Lord told me, "Son of Man, watch carefully, listen closely, and remember everything I'm going to be telling you about all the statutes pertaining to the Lord's temple and all of its laws. Pay careful attention to the entrance to the temple, along with all of the exits to the sanctuary."
44.6: "You are to tell the Resistance-that is, the house of Israel, 'This is what the Lord God says： "I've had enough of all of your loathsome behavior, you house of Israel!
44.7: You kept on bringing in foreigners, those who were both uncircumcised in heart and flesh, to profane my sanctuary by being inside my temple, and by doing so you've emptied my covenant, all the while offering my food - the fat and the blood - in addition to all of your other loathsome things.
44.8: Furthermore, you haven't observed the requirements of my holy things. Instead, you placed foreigners in charge of my sanctuary."'
44.9: "This is what the Lord God says, 'No foreigner who is both uncircumcised in heart and flesh, of all the foreigners who are among the Israelites is to enter my sanctuary.
44.10: But the Levites, who went far away from me when Israel abandoned me, who left me to follow their idols, are to bear the punishment of their iniquity.
44.11: Nevertheless, they are to serve in my sanctuary, overseeing the gates of the temple, ministering to the temple, slaughtering the burnt offerings and the sacrifices presented for the people, standing in the presence of the people, and ministering to them.
44.12: Because they kept serving them in the presence of their idols, becoming a sin-filled stumbling block to the house of Israel,'" declares the Lord God. "'I have sworn against them that they are to bear the consequences of their iniquity.
44.13: They are not to come near me to serve as a priest to me, nor approach any of my holy things, including the most holy things. Instead, they are to bear the shame of the loathsome things that they have committed.
44.14: Nevertheless, I will appoint them to take care of my temple, includings all of its service and everything that is to be done inside of it.'"
44.15: "The descendants of Zadok, Levitical priests who took care of my sanctuary when the Israelites wandered away from me, are to come near me to minister to me. They are to stand before me to offer the fat and the blood to me," declares the Lord God.
44.16: "They are to enter my sanctuary, approach my table to minister to me, and observe my requirements.
44.17: Whenever they enter at the gates of the inner court, they are to be clothed with linen garments. They are not to wear wool when they are ministering within the gates of the inner courtyard or in the temple.
44.18: Linen turbans are to be on their heads, and they are to wear linen undergarments. Also, they are not to clothe themselves with anything that makes them perspire.
44.19: "When they enter the outer courtyard, that is, the outer courtyard where the people are, they are to take off their garments in which they were ministering, lay them in the consecrated chambers, and put on different garments so they will not transfer holiness to the people through their garments.
44.20: Also, they are not to shave their heads nor let their hair grow long. Instead, they are certainly to trim the hair on their heads.
44.21: "None of the priests are to drink wine upon entering the inner courtyard.
44.22: "They are not to marry a widow or a divorced woman. Instead, they are to marry virgins from the descendants of the house of Israel, or a widow who is the widow of a priest."
44.23: "They are to teach my people how to discern what is holy compared to what is common, showing them how to discern between what is unclean and clean.
44.24: When disputes arise, they are to take their stand to judge, adjudicating matters according to my ordinances. They are to enforce my laws, my statutes, all of my appointed feasts, and they are to sanctify my Sabbaths.
44.25: "They are not to come in contact with a dead body, so as not to defile themselves, except in the case of their father, mother, son, daughter, brother, or for an unmarried sister, on whose behalf they may defile themselves.
44.26: After he is cleansed, he is to remain exempt from ministry for seven days.
44.27: On the day that he returns to the sanctuary's inner court to minister, he is to offer his own sin offering," declares the Lord God.
44.28: "Now with respect to their inheritances, I am to be their inheritance, and you are to give them no possession in Israel, since I am their possession.
44.29: They are to eat the grain offerings, sin offering, and guilt offering. Everything consecrated in Israel is to belong to them.
44.30: The first portion of all the first fruits of every kind and every offering of any kind is to be for the priests. You are to give the priest the first portion of your grain. As a result a blessing will rest on your household.
44.31: However, the priests are not to eat any bird or animal that has died a natural death or that has been torn apart."

45.1: "When you divide the land for an inheritance, you are to present a Terumah to the Lord, a consecrated portion of the land, the length of which is to be 25,000 cubits, and the width of which is to be 20,000 cubits. The entire contents of this area are to be treated as holy."
45.2: "A holy place is to be dedicated from this area in the form of a square measuring 500 by 500 cubits, with a 50 cubit buffer zone surrounding it.
45.3: From this area a measure is to be made 25,000 cubits long and 10,000 cubits wide, which is to contain the sanctuary, the holiest of holy objects.
45.4: It is to be a holy portion of the land, set aside for the priests who serve the sanctuary, who approach to serve the Lord. It is to be a place for their houses, as well as the holy place of the sanctuary.
45.5: "An area 25,000 cubits long by 10,000 cubits wide is to be set aside for use by the Levite servants of the temple, 20 parcels for their residential properties.
45.6: "The land allocation for the city is to be set at 5,000 cubits wide and 25,000 cubits long, adjacent to the sanctuary district, reserved for the entire house of Israel."
45.7: "The Regent Prince is to have a portion on both sides of the consecrated allotment for the sanctuary and the city's land allotment, adjacent to both on the west and the east sides, comparable in length to one of the portions from the west border to the east border.
45.8: This property in Israel is to belong to the Regent Prince, so my regent princes will no longer mistreat my nation. The remaining portion of the land is to be allotted to the house of Israel, that is, to its tribes."7
45.9: "This is what the Lord God says, 'Enough of you, you regent princes of Israel! Abandon your violence and destruction. Practice what is just and right instead! Stop confiscating property from my people!' declares the Lord God.
45.10: It's to be an honest scale, an honest dry measure, and an honest liquid measure for you!
45.11: "The ephah and the bath are to be of equal volume; that is, the bath is to contain one tenth of a homer and the ephah one tenth of a homer. The homer is to be the standard on which their volume measurement is to be based.
45.12: "The shekel is to weigh 20 gerahs. The mina is to be comprised of three coins weighing 20, 25, and fifteen shekels, respectively."
45.13: "Here are the standards for presenting offerings： a sixth of an ephah that is based on the standard homer of wheat, and a sixth of an ephah based on the standard homer of barley.
45.14: The olive oil quota is to be based on the bath, measured at ten baths to each homer, which is equal to one kor.
45.15: The sheep quota is to be one from each flock of 200 taken from the pastures of Israel. From all of these you are to present grain offerings, burnt offerings, and peace offerings, to make atonement for them," declares the Lord God.
45.16: "The entire nation living in the land is to present this offering to the Regent Prince in Israel.
45.17: The Regent Prince is to provide the burnt offerings, grain offerings, and drink offerings at the feasts, on the new moons and Sabbaths, and at all of the proscribed feasts of the house of Israel. He is to provide the grain offerings, burnt offerings, and peace offerings in order to make atonement for the house of Israel."
45.18: "This is what the Lord God says, 'On the first day of the first month, you are to present a young bull without defect in order to cleanse the sanctuary.
45.19: The priest is to place some of the blood from the sin offering on the door posts of the temple, on the four corners of the ledge around the altar, and on the posts of the gate leading to the inner court.
45.20: You are also to do this on the seventh day of the month, to make atonement for any person who wanders away or who sins through ignorance in order to make atonement for the temple.
45.21: "'On the fourteenth day of the first month, you are to observe the Passover as a feast for seven days. Unleavened bread is to be eaten.
45.22: On that day, the Regent Prince is to provide, both for himself and for all the people who live in the land, a bull for a sin offering.
45.23: Each day during the seven days of the feast, he is to provide a burnt offering to the Lord, consisting of seven bulls and seven rams without defect, offered each day throughout the seven days, along with a male goat offered each day as a sin offering.
45.24: "'The Regent Prince is also to present a grain offering consisting of an ephah with each bull and an ephah with each ram, along with a hin of olive oil mixed with an ephah of grain.
45.25: "'On the fifteenth day of the seven month, during a seven day feast, the Regent Prince is to present these as daily sin offerings, burnt offerings, and grain offerings mixed with oil.

46.1: "This is what the Lord God says： 'The inner, east-facing courtyard will remain shut during the six working days of the week, but on the Sabbath day it will be opened, as well as on the day of the new moon.
46.2: "'The Regent Prince will enter by way of the portico of the gate from outside and will stand at the doorframe of the gate where the priests will present the Regent Prince's burnt offerings and peace offerings. Then the Regent Prince will worship at the threshold of the gate and go out. The gate will not be closed until evening.
46.3: "'The people who live in the land will worship at the doorway of the gate on the sabbaths and new moons in the Lord's presence.
46.4: "'The burnt offering that the Regent Prince will present to the Lord on the Sabbath day will consist of six lambs without defect, a ram without defect,
46.5: a grain offering with the ram consisting of an ephah, a grain offering with the lambs consisting of whatever amount he brings with him, and a hin of oil with each ephah of grain.
46.6: "'Furthermore, each new moon there is to be a young bull without defect and six male lambs and a ram without defect.
46.7: The Regent Prince will present an ephah of grain along with the bull, an ephah of grain along with the ram, a grain offering-consisting of as much as he is able to give-and a hin of olive oil with each ephah of grain.
46.8: "'The Regent Prince will enter by way of the portico of the gate and will leave the same way he came in.
46.9: When the people who live in the land come into the Lord's presence during the feasts, whoever enters by way of the northern gate will leave by way of the southern gate, and whoever enters by way of the southern gate will leave by way of the northern gate. No one will leave by the same route that he enters, but instead will go straight out.
46.10: The Regent Prince will enter when they are coming in, and he will leave when they go out.
46.11: "'The grain offering for the festivals and appointed feasts will include an ephah with a bull, an ephah with a ram, and as much grain with the lambs as the Regent Prince brings with him, along with a hin of oil with each ephah.
46.12: "'Whenever the Regent Prince presents a voluntary offering, burnt offering, or peace offering, he will present it voluntarily to the Lord, and the east-facing gate is to be opened for him. He is to provide his burnt offering and peace offering as he does on the Sabbath. When he leaves, the gate is to be shut behind him.
46.13: "'He is to present a one year old lamb without defect for a burnt offering to the Lord in the morning every day.
46.14: In addition, he is to present a grain offering with it every morning, consisting of a sixth of an ephah mixed with and one third of a hin of oil. This grain offering is to be offered to the Lord, pursuant to a permanent ordinance.
46.15: "'They are to present the lamb offering, the grain offering, and the oil every morning as an ongoing burnt offering.'"
46.16: "This is what the Lord God says： 'If the Regent Prince gives a gift to someone, it is to remain with the man's descendants as their own inheritance.
46.17: But if he gives a gift to any of his servants, it is to belong to the servant until the Year of Release, at which time it is to be returned to the Regent Prince. His inheritance is to belong only to his sons.
46.18: The Regent Prince is not to appropriate the nation's inheritance nor take advantage of them by taking their property from them. Instead, he is to provide an inheritance for his sons from his own possessions so that my people will not be separated from their possessions.'"
46.19: Then the angel brought me in through an entrance beside the gate into the north-facing chambers dedicated to the priests. As I looked toward the rear of the far western end, I saw a place
46.20: about which he said, "This is where the priests will be boiling the guilt and sin offerings and baking the grain offerings so they don't bring them through the outer courtyard, thus diminishing the people's holiness."
46.21: Then he brought me out to the exterior courtyard and led me across to each of the four corners of the courtyard. There in each corner was an enclosed area set aside,
46.22: all of them the same size; that is, each were 40 cubits long and 30 cubits wide.
46.23: A low wall built of masonry surrounded each courtyard, with boiling places set in rows in the wall.
46.24: He told me, "This is where the ministers of the Temple will be preparing the sacrifices that will be presented by the people.

47.1: After this, he brought me back to the doorway to the temple. To my amazement, there was water flowing out toward the east from beneath the threshold of the temple. (The temple faced eastward.) The water flowed down from beneath the right side of the temple, that is, from the south-facing side where the altar was located.
47.2: Then he brought me out through the north gateway and around to the one outside that faces toward the east. To my amazement, water was trickling out from that part of the south side, too!
47.3: As the man went out toward the east, he carried a measuring line in his hand. He measured out 1,500 feet as he led me through water that was ankle-deep.
47.4: Then he measured out another 1,500 feet, where he led me through water that was knee-deep. And then he measured out another 1,500 feet, where the water was waist-deep.
47.5: When he had measured out another 1,500 feet, the water had become deep enough that I wasn't able to ford it. Instead, I would have had to swim through it.
47.6: Then, as he was bringing me back along the river bank, he asked me, "Son of Man, did you see all of this?
47.7: As we were coming back, I was amazaed to see that there were many, many trees lining both banks of the river.
47.8: He told me, "This river flows toward the eastern territories all the way down into the Arabah, and from there its water heads out toward the Dead Sea, where the sea water turns fresh.
47.9: It will support all kinds of living creatures that will thrive abundantly wherever the river flows. There will be a great many fish, because this water will flow there and turn the Dead Sea's salt water fresh. As a result, everything will live wherever the river flows.8
47.10: "A day will come when firsherman will line its banks-from Engedi to Eneglaim there will plenty of room to spread out nets. There will be all sorts of species of fish, as abundant as the fish that live in the Meditteranean Sea. There will be lots of them!
47.11: "The river delta will consist of swamps and marshes that will remain a salt water wetland preserve.
47.12: Lining each side of the river banks, all sorts of species of fruit tree will be growing. Their leaves will never wither and their fruit will never fail. They will bear fruit every month, because the water that nourishes them will be flowing from the sanctuary. Their fruit will be for food and their leaves will contain substances that promote healing."
47.13: This is what the Lord God says： "This is to be the territorial border by which you apportion the land for an inheritance among the twelve tribes of Israel, with Joseph double-portioned.
47.14: Apportion it for their inheritances, one man equally as if to his own brother, which is how I promised to give it to your ancestors. This way, the land will fall to you as an inheritance.
47.15: "This is to be the border for the land： on the north side, from the Mediterranean Sea by the Hethlon Road to the entrance of Zedad,
47.16: Hamath, Berothah, Sibraim (which lies between the border of Damascus and the border of Hamath), and Hazer-hatticon, which is on the border of Hauran.
47.17: The border shall proceed from the Mediterranean Sea to Hazer-enan (a border of Damascus), and on the north facing north will be the border of Hamath. This will be the north side.
47.18: "The eastern extremity will proceed from between Hauran and Damascus, then between Gilead, and then through the land of Israel-the Jordan River. You shall measure from the northern border to the Dead Sea. This will be the eastern perimeter.
47.19: "You will determine the southern extremity running from Tamar as far as the waters of Meribath-kadesh, then from there proceeding to the Wadi of Egypt and then to the Mediterranean Sea. This will be the southern perimeter.
47.20: "The western perimeter will be the Mediterranean Sea, from the southern most border to a location opposite the entrance to Hamath. This will be the western perimeter.
47.21: "You shall apportion this land among yourselves according to the tribes of Israel,
47.22: dividing it randomly among yourselves and among the aliens who live among you and bear children among you. You shall treat them like native-born Israelites. Among you they, too, shall be allotted an inheritance with the tribes of Israel.
47.23: Furthermore, you shall provide his inheritance there in the tribe within which the alien remains," declares the Lord God.

48.1: "These are the names of the tribes from the northernmost extremity westward along the road from Hethlon to the entrance of Hamath, Hazar-enan (a border of Damascus) northward to the coast of Hamath. The perimeter shall run east-to-west; the tribe of Dan with one portion;
48.2: running along the border of the tribe of Dan from the eastern perimeter to the western perimeter, the tribe of Asher with one portion;
48.3: running along the border of the tribe of Asher from the eastern perimeter to the western perimeter, the tribe of Naphtali with one portion;
48.4: running along the border of the tribe of Naphtali from the eastern perimeter to the western perimeter, the tribe of Manasseh with one portion;
48.5: running along the border of the tribe of Manasseh from the eastern perimeter to the western perimeter, the tribe of Ephraim with one portion;
48.6: running along the border of the tribe of Ephraim from the eastern perimeter to the western perimeter, the tribe of Reuben with one portion;
48.7: and running along the border of the tribe of Reuben from the eastern perimeter to the western perimeter, the tribe of Judah with one portion."
48.8: "Running along the border of the tribe of Judah from the eastern perimeter to the western perimeter you shall set apart the Terumah, 25,000 units wide but in east-west length equal to one of the other apportionments, from the eastern perimeter to the western perimeter, with the Temple in the m iddle of it.
48.9: The Terumah that you shall give to the Lord shall be 25,000 units."
48.10: "The holy Terumah shall be reserved for these, the priests： Toward the north, 25,000 units in length; toward the west, 10,000 units in width; toward the east, 10,000 units in width; and toward the south, 25,000 units in length. The Lord's sanctuary shall be in its midst.
48.11: It shall be for use by priests from the descendants of Zadok, who have observed the things with which I charged them and who did not wander astray when the Israelites went astray, just as the Levites wandered astray.
48.12: It shall be a Terumah for them from the allotment of the land, a most holy place, adjoining the border of the Levites."
48.13: "Alongside the border of the priests, the Levites shall be allotted 25,000 units in length and 10,000 units in width. The entire length is to be 25,000 units and its width 10,000 units.
48.14: Furthermore, they are not to sell or exchange any part of it, nor transfer this first fruits of the land, because it is holy to the Lord.
48.15: "The rest, 5,000 units wide and 25,000 units along its front, will serve as a common portion for use by the city for housing and open spaces, since the city shall be in its midst.
48.16: These shall be its dimensions： the north side, 4,500 units; the south side, 4,500 units; the east side, 4,500 units; and the west side 4,500 units.
48.17: The city shall have urban areas set aside： on the north 250 units; on the south, 250 units, on the east, 250 units; and on the west, 250 units.
48.18: "The remainder of the length that borders the holy Terumah shall be 10,000 units long eastward and 10,000 units westward It shall lie adjacent to the holy Terumah. Its harvest shall produce food for those who work in the city.
48.19: The city workers who cultivate it shall come from all the tribes of Israel.
48.20: The entire Terumah is to be 25,000 units by 25,000 units-you shall reserve it as a holy Terumah in the form of a square within the city limits."
48.21: "Now the remainder of the allotment on either side of the holy Terumah will be for the Regent Prince and for city property-over against the
48.25: Adjacent to the border of Simeon running from east to west, Issachar will retain one portion.
48.22: Except for what belongs to the Levites and the city property, which will stand in the middle of what belongs to the Regent Prince, whatever is between the border of Judah and the border of Benjamin shall belong to the Regent Prince."
48.23: "Now as to the rest of the tribes： from the east side to the west side, Benjamin will retain one portion.
48.24: Adjacent to the border of Benjamin running from east to west, Simeon will retain one portion.
48.26: Adjacent to the border of Issachar running from east to west, Zebulun will retain one portion.
48.27: Adjacent to the border of Zebulun running from east to west, Gad will retain one portion.
48.28: Adjacent to the border of Gad to the south and extending toward the south, the border shall proceed from Tamar to the waters of Meribath-kadesh, then to the Wadi of Egypt, and from there to the Mediterranean Sea.
48.29: "This is the land that you are to allocate by lottery to the tribes of Israel as their inheritance, and these are their respective divisions," declares the Lord God.
48.30: "These are the exits to the city： On the north side, 4,500 units by measurement,
48.31: shall be the gates of the city. Named after the tribes of Israel, three gates shall serve the north site： one named the Reuben Gate, one named the Judah Gate, and one named the Levi Gate.
48.32: On the east side, 4,500 units by measurement, there shall be three gates： one named the Joseph Gate, one named the Benjamin Gate, and one named the Dan Gate.
48.33: On the south side, 4,500 units by measurement, there shall be three gates： one named the Simeon Gate, one named the Issachar Gate, and one named the Zebulun Gate.
48.34: On the west side, 4,500 units by measurement, there shall be three gates： one named the Gad Gate, one named the Asher Gate, and one named the Naphtali Gate.
48.35: A perimeter shall measure 18,000 units, and the name of the city from that time on shall be： 'The Lord is There.'"

1.1: In the third year of the reign of King Jehoiakim of Judah, King Nebuchadnezzar of Babylon came to Jerusalem and laid siege to it.
1.2: Within a week, the Lord handed King Jehoiakim of Judah over to him, along with valuable objects from the house of God. Nebuchadnezzar brought them to the temple of his god in the land of Shinar and stored them in its treasure house.
1.3: The king ordered Ashpenaz, his chief officer, to bring in some Israelites of royal and noble descent.
1.4: They were to be young men without physical defect, handsome in appearance, skilled in all wisdom, quick to learn, prudent in how they used knowledge, and capable of serving in the king's palace. They were to learn the literature and language of the Chaldeans.
1.5: The king assigned them fine food and choice wine on a daily basis, ordering them to be trained for three years, at the end of which time they would enter the king's service.
1.6: Included among the people of Judah were Daniel, Hananiah, Mishael, and Azariah.
1.7: The chief officer assigned the name "Belteshazzar" to Daniel, the name "Shadrach" to Hannaniah, the name "Meshach" to Mishael, and the name "Abednego" to Azariah.
1.8: Daniel determined within himself not to become defiled by the king's menu of rich foods or by the king's wine, so he requested permission from the chief officer not to defile himself.
1.9: God granted to Daniel grace and compassion on the part of the chief officer.
1.10: The chief officer said to Daniel, "I fear his majesty the king, who has determined what you eat and drink. If he notices that your faces are more pale than the other young men in your group, I will forfeit my head to the king."1
1.11: But Daniel said to the guard whom the chief officer had appointed over Daniel, Hananiah, Mishael, and Azariah,
1.12: "Please test your servants for ten days and let us be given vegetables to eat and water to drink.
1.13: Then compare how we look with the young men who ate the king's rich food, and treat your servants in accordance with what you observe."
1.14: So he listened to what Daniel said and tested them for ten days.
1.15: At the end of ten days their appearance was better and their faces were well-nourished compared to the young men who ate the king's rich food.
1.16: So the guard took away their rich food and wine, giving them vegetables.
1.17: As for these four young men, God gave them knowledge, aptitude for learning, and wisdom. Daniel also could understand all kinds of visions and dreams.1
1.18: Then at the end of the training period that the king had established, the chief officer brought them in before Nebuchadnezzar.
1.19: When the king spoke to them, none of them compared to Daniel, Hananiah, Mishael, or Azariah as they stood before the king.
1.20: In every matter of wisdom or understanding that the king discussed with them, he found them ten times superior to all the astrologers and enchanters in his entire palace.
1.21: So Daniel remained there in service until the first year of King Cyrus.

2.1: Now during the second year of Nebuchadnezzar's reign, Nebuchadnezzar had dreams that troubled him. As a result, he couldn't sleep.
2.2: So the king gave orders to summon the diviners, the enchanters, sorcerers, and Chaldeans to reveal to the king what he had dreamed. When they came and stood before him,
2.3: the king said to them, "I have dreamed a dream and I will remain troubled until I can understand it."2
2.4: The Chaldeans responded to the king in Aramaic： "May the king live forever. Tell the dream to your servants, and we'll reveal its meaning."
2.5: In reply the king said to the Chaldeans, "Here is what I have commanded： If you don't tell me both the dream and its meaning, you'll be destroyed and your houses will be reduced to rubble.
2.6: But if you do relate the dream to me as well as its meaning, you'll receive gifts, rewards, and great honor from me. Therefore reveal the dream to me, along with its meaning."
2.7: They replied again, "Let the king tell his servants the dream, and we'll disclose its meaning."
2.8: The king responded, "I'm convinced that you're stalling for time because you're aware of what I've commanded.
2.9: So if you don't disclose the dream to me, there will be only one sentence for all of you. You have conspired together to present lies and corrupt interpretations until the situation changes. Now tell me the dream and I'll know that you can reveal its true meaning."
2.10: The Chaldeans answered the king directly, "There's not a single man on earth who can do what the king has commanded. No king, lord, or ruler has ever asked such a thing from any diviner, enchanter, or Chaldean.
2.11: Furthermore, what the king is asking is so difficult that no one can reveal it except the gods-and they don't live with human beings."
2.12: At this point, the king flew into a rage and issued an order to destroy all the advisors of Babylon.
2.13: When the order went out to kill the advisors, they searched for Daniel and his friends to kill them, too.
2.14: Daniel responded with wisdom and discretion to Arioch, the king's executioner, who had gone out to execute the advisors of Babylon.
2.15: He asked him, "Why such a harsh decree from the king? Then Arioch informed Daniel,
2.16: so Daniel went to ask Nebuchadnezzar for an appointment to see him, and it was granted him so that he could reveal the meaning to the king.
2.17: Then Daniel went home and told his friends Hananiah, Mishael, and Azariah about the king's command.
2.18: Daniel was seeking mercy, in order to ask about this mystery in the presence of the God of heaven, so that Daniel and his friends might not be executed along with the rest of the advisors of Babylon.
2.19: When the mystery was revealed to Daniel in a vision later that night, Daniel blessed the God of heaven
2.20: and said, "May the name of God be blessed forever and ever; wisdom and power are his for evermore.
2.21: It is God who alters the times and seasons, and he removes kings and promotes kings. He gives wisdom to the wise and knowledge to the discerning.
2.22: He reveals what is profoundly mysterious and knows what is in the darkness; with him dwells light.
2.23: To you, God of my ancestors, I give thanks and praise, because you have given me wisdom and power; you have now revealed to me what we asked of you by making known to us what the king commanded."
2.24: After this, Daniel went to Arioch, whom the king had appointed to execute the advisors of Babylon. He told him, "Don't destroy the advisors of Babylon. Bring me before the king and I'll explain the meaning to him."
2.25: Then Arioch quickly brought Daniel into the king's presence and informed him： "I've found a man from the Judean captives who will make known the meaning to the king."
2.26: King Nebuchadnezzar replied by saying to Daniel (whose Babylonian name is Belteshazzar), "Are you able to tell me about the dream and its meaning?
2.27: By way of answer, Daniel addressed the king： "None of the advisors, enchanters, diviners, or astrologers can explain the secret that the king has requested to be made known.
2.28: But there is a God in heaven who reveals secrets, and he is making known to King Nebuchadnezzar what will happen in the latter days. "While you were in bed, the dream and the visions that came to your head were as follows：
2.29: Your majesty, when you were in bed, thoughts came to your mind about what would happen in the future, and the Revealer of Secrets has made known to you what will take place.
2.30: As for me, this secret was made known to me, not because my own wisdom is greater than anyone else alive, but in order that the meaning may be made known to the king, and that you might understand the thoughts of your heart.
2.31: "Your majesty, while you were watching, you observed an enormous statue. This magnificent statue stood before you with extraordinary brilliance. Its appearance was terrifying.
2.32: That statue had a head made of pure gold, with its chest and arms made of silver, its abdomen and thighs made of bronze,
2.33: its legs made of iron, and its feet made partly of iron and partly of clay.
2.34: "As you were watching, a rock was quarried-but not with human hands-and it struck the iron and clay feet of the statue, breaking them to pieces.
2.35: Then the iron, the clay, the bronze, the silver, and the gold were broken in pieces together and became like chaff from a summer threshing floor that the breeze carries away without leaving a trace. Then the rock that struck the statue grew into a huge mountain and filled the entire earth.
2.36: "This was the dream, and we'll now relate its meaning to the king.
2.37: You, your majesty, king of kings-to whom the God of heaven has given the kingdom, the power, the strength, and the glory,
2.38: so that wherever people, wild animals, or birds of the sky live, he has placed them under your control, giving you dominion over them all - you're that head of gold.
2.39: "After you, another kingdom will arise that is inferior to yours, and then a third kingdom of bronze will arise to rule all the earth.
2.40: Then there will be a fourth kingdom, as strong as iron. Just as all things are broken to pieces and shattered by iron, so it will shatter and crush everything.
2.41: "The feet and toes that you saw, made partly of potter's clay and partly of iron, represent a divided kingdom. It will still have the strength of iron, in that you saw iron mixed with clay.
2.42: Just as their toes and feet are part iron and part clay, so will the kingdom be both strong and brittle.
2.43: Just as you saw iron mixed with clay, so they will mix themselves with human offspring. Furthermore, they won't remain together, just as iron doesn't mix with clay.
2.44: "During the reigns of those kings, the God of heaven will set up a kingdom that will never be destroyed, nor its sovereignty left in the hands of another people. It will shatter and crush all of these kingdoms, and it will stand forever.
2.45: Now, just as you saw that the stone was cut out of the mountain without human hands-and that it crushed the iron, bronze, clay, silver, and gold to pieces-so also the great God has revealed to the king what will take place after this. Your dream will come true, and its meaning will prove trustworthy.
2.46: Then King Nebuchadnezzar fell on his face before Daniel, paid honor to him, and commanded that an offering and incense be presented on his behalf.
2.47: The king told Daniel, "Truly your God is the God of gods, the Lord of kings, and the Revealer of Secrets, because you were able to reveal this mystery."
2.48: Then the king promoted Daniel to a high position and lavished many great gifts on him, including making him ruler over the entire province of Babylon and chief administrator over the advisors of Babylon.
2.49: Moreover, Daniel requested that the king appoint Shadrach, Meshach, and Abednego administrators over the province of Babylon, while Daniel himself remained in the royal court.

3.1: Some time later, king Nebuchadnezzar built a golden statue, making it ninety feet high and nine feet wide. He set it up in the Dura Valley within the province of Babylon.
3.2: Then King Nebuchadnezzar summoned the regional authorities, governors, deputy governors, advisors, treasurers, judges, magistrates, and all of the other administrators of the provinces, ordering them to come to the dedication of the statue that he had erected.
3.3: So the regional authorities, governors, deputy governors, advisors, treasurers, judges, magistrates, and all of the other administrators of the provinces assembled to dedicate the statue that King Nebuchadnezzar had erected. They took their places in front of the statue that he had erected.
3.4: Then a herald proclaimed aloud： "People of all nations, and languages are commanded：
3.5: Whenever you hear the sound of the trumpet, the flute, the lyre, the four-stringed lyre, and the harp, playing together along with various instruments, you are to fall down and worship the golden statue that was set up by King Nebuchadnezzar.
3.6: Anyone who does not fall down and worship shall immediately be thrown into the blazing fire furnace."
3.7: Therefore, when all of the people "heard the sound of the trumpet, the flute, the lyre, the four-stringed lyre, and the harp, playing together along with various other instruments," all the "people, nations, and languages" began to fall down and worship the golden statue that King Nebuchadnezzar had set up.
3.8: Just then, certain influential Chaldeans took this opportunity to come forward and denounce the Jews.
3.9: They said to King Nebuchadnezzar, "Your majesty, live forever.
3.10: You, your majesty, issued this decree： 'Every man who hears the sound of the trumpet, the flute, the lyre, the four-stringed lyre, and the harp, playing together along with various other instruments shall fall down and worship the golden statue.
3.11: Whoever does not fall down and worship shall be thrown into a blazing fire furnace.'
3.12: "Certain influential Jewish men whom you appointed to manage the city of Babylon?Shadrach, Meshach, and Abednego?have neither paid attention to you, your majesty, nor served your gods. And they won't worship the golden statue that you set up."
3.13: Nebuchadnezzar flew into a rage and furiously ordered that Shadrach, Meshach, and Abednego be brought before him.
3.14: Nebuchadnezzar asked them, "Is it true, Shadrach, Meshach, and Abednego, that you don't worship my gods and that you don't worship the golden statue that has been set up -
3.15: Now, if you are ready at this very moment to obey 'the sound of the trumpet, the flute, the lyre, the four-stringed lyre, and the harp,' and worship the image that I have made… If you do not so worship, you will immediately have cast yourselves into the middle of the blazing fire, and what god is there who can deliver you from my power?
3.16: Shadrach, Meshach, and Abednego answered King Nebuchadnezzar, "It's not necessary for us to respond in this matter.
3.17: Your majesty, if it be his will, our God whom we serve can deliver us from the blazing fire furnace, and he will deliver us from you.
3.18: But if not, rest assured, your majesty, that we won't serve your gods, and we won't worship the golden statue that you have set up."
3.19: Out of control with rage, Nebuchadnezzar's facial expression changed toward Shadrach, Meshach, and Abednego, and he ordered that the furnace be heated seven times hotter than usual.
3.20: Then he issued orders to his elite guard to bind Shadrach, Meshach, and Abednego with ropes and throw them into the blazing fire furnace.
3.21: So the elite guard tied them up fully clothed, still wearing their robes, tunics, and turbans, and threw them into the blazing fire furnace,
3.22: because the king's command was so drastic. Since the furnace was blazing hot, its flames killed those who threw Shadrach, Meshach, and Abednego into the blazing fire.
3.23: Bound firmly with ropes, these three men Shadrach, Meshach, and Abednego fell into the blazing fire furnace.
3.24: Astonished, King Nebuchadnezzar stood up in terror, and asked his advisers, "Didn't we throw three men into the fire, bound firmly with ropes? In reply they said to the king, "Yes, your majesty."
3.25: "Look!" he told them, "I see four men walking untied and unharmed in the middle of the fire, and the appearance of the fourth resembles a divine being."
3.26: Then Nebuchadnezzar approached the opening of the blazing fire furnace. He shouted out, "Shadrach, Meshach, and Abednego, servants of the Most High God, come out and come here!" So Shadrach, Meshach, and Abednego came out of the fire.
3.27: The satraps, the viceroys, the governors, and the royal advisors gazed at those men and saw that the fire had no effect on their bodies-not a hair on their head was singed, their clothes were not burned, and they did not smell of fire.
3.28: Nebuchadnezzar spoke up and announced： 'Blessed be the God of Shadrach, Meshach and Abednego! He sent his angel to deliver his servants who trusted in him. They disobeyed the king's command and were willing to risk their lives in order not to serve or worship any god except their own God.
3.29: So I decree that people from any nation or language who say anything against the God of Shadrach, Meshach and Abednego will be destroyed and their house reduced to rubble, because there is no other god who can save like this.'283
3.30: Then the king promoted Shadrach, Meshach, and Abednego within the province of Babylon.29

4.1: An Official Statement from Nebuchadnezzar the King To the people of all nations and languages who live on earth. Peace and prosperity to you!
4.2: It gives me great pleasure to tell about the signs and wonders that the Most High God has done for me.
4.3: How great are his signs! How powerful are his wonders! His kingdom is an eternal kingdom, and his dominion lasts from generation to generation.
4.4: I, Nebuchadnezzar, was resting in my home and prospering in my palace.
4.5: I had a dream that made me afraid. The thoughts that went through my mind while in bed and the visions in my head terrified me.
4.6: So I gave an order to bring in all of the advisors of Babylon so they would tell me the interpretation of the dream.
4.7: Then the diviners, enchanters, Chaldeans, and astrologers came in, and I told them the dream. But they could not reveal its interpretation to me.
4.8: Eventually, Daniel appeared before me. (He is called Belteshazzar, in accordance with the name of my god, and the spirit of the holy gods is within him.) I told him my dream：
4.9: "Belteshazzar, chief of the diviners, since I know that the spirit of the holy gods is within you, and no mystery too difficult for you, explain to me the vision of my dream that I saw, along with its interpretation.
4.10: This is what I saw in the visions of my head while I was in bed： I was looking and-listen carefully! - I saw a tree in the middle of the earth, the height of which was very great.
4.11: The tree grew large, became strong, and its top reached the sky. It could be seen to the ends of the earth.
4.12: Its foliage was beautiful, its fruit bountiful, and its food sufficient for everyone. The animals of the field found shade under it, the birds of the sky lived in its branches, and every creature was fed from it.
4.13: "Then I saw in the visions of my head while I was in bed-and take careful notice! - I saw a holy observer descend from heaven.
4.14: He called out aloud： 'Cut down the tree and cut off its branches. Strip off its foliage and scatter its fruit. Let the animals get out from under it, and let the birds leave its branches.
4.15: Nevertheless, leave the stump and its roots in the ground, but bind it with iron and bronze in the field grass. Let him be drenched with dew from the sky, and let him graze with the animals in the grass of the earth.
4.16: Let his mind be changed from that of a man, and let him be given the mind of an animal until seven seasons of time pass by for him.
4.17: This order is announced by the observers, and the holy ones declare the verdict, so that the living may know that the Most High is sovereign over human kingdoms and grants them to whomever he desires, and he places the least important of men over them.'14
4.18: "This is the dream that I, King Nebuchadnezzar, saw. Belteshazzar, tell me its meaning, since none of the advisors in my kingdom can tell me its interpretation. But you are able to do so because the spirit of the holy gods is in you."
4.19: Then Daniel (also known as Belteshazzar) was greatly troubled for a while and was terrified by his thoughts. The king said, "Belteshazzar, don't let the dream or its meaning terrify you." Belteshazzar responded, "Your majesty, if only the dream were about your enemies and its meaning about those who oppose you!
4.20: The tree that you saw, which grew large and strong until its top reached the sky and became visible to the whole earth
4.21: with beautiful leaves and abundant fruit-enough food for everyone-and under which wild animals of the field found shelter and the birds of the air had nests in its branches -
4.22: it's you, your majesty! You've become great and strong, your greatness has grown to the heavens, and your dominion reaches to the distant parts of the earth.
4.23: "Your majesty saw a holy observer descending from heaven and saying, 'Cut down the tree and destroy it, but leave the stump in the ground, along with its roots, bound with iron and bronze in the field grass. Let him be soaked with the dew of the sky and live with the wild animals of the field until seven seasons pass over him.'
4.24: "This is the meaning, your majesty, and this is the decree that the Most High has issued against his majesty, the king:
4.25: You'll be driven from people, and you'll live among wild animals of the field. You'll eat grass like cattle and be soaked with the dew of the sky while seven years pass you by-until you realize that the Most High is sovereign over human kingdoms and grants them to whomever he desires.
4.26: Just as it was ordered for you to leave the stump of the tree in the ground along with its roots, so your kingdom will be restored to you when you realize that Heaven rules over everything.
4.27: Therefore, your majesty, may my advice be acceptable to you： Stop your sinning, do what's right, and put a stop to your wickedness by showing kindness to the oppressed. Perhaps your tranquility will continue."
4.28: All of this happened to King Nebuchadnezzar.
4.29: About a year later, as the king was walking on the roof of the royal palace of Babylon,
4.30: he commented to himself, "Isn't Babylon great?I've built a royal palace in it by my own might and power, for the sake of my majesty."
4.31: As the words were being spoken by the king, a voice came forth from heaven： "King Nebuchadnezzar, this is declared to you： 'The kingdom has been taken from you!
4.32: You're to be driven away from people. You're to live with the wild animals of the field. You are to be made to eat grass like cattle, and seven years will pass you by until you realize that the Most High is sovereign over human kingdoms and grants them to whomever he desires.'
4.33: The decree was fulfilled against Nebuchadnezzar immediately. He was driven away from people to eat grass like cattle, and his body was drenched with dew from the sky, until his hair grew like eagles' feathers and his nails like birds' claws."
4.34: "When that period of time was over, I, Nebuchadnezzar, lifted my eyes to heaven and my sanity returned to me. I blessed the Most High, praising and honoring the one who lives forever： For his sovereignty is eternal, and his kingdom continues from generation to generation.
4.35: All who live on the earth are nothing compared to him. He does what he wishes with the heavenly armies and with those who live on earth. No one can hold back his power or say to him, 'What did you do?
4.36: At that moment I recovered my sanity, and my honor and majesty returned to me, for the sake of my kingdom. My advisors and officials sought me out, my throne was restored, and even more greatness than I had before was added to me.
4.37: "In conclusion, I, Nebuchad­nezzar, praise, exalt, and give glory to the King of heaven： For everything he does is true, his ways are just, and he is able to humble those who walk in pride."

5.1: King Belshazzar put on a great feast for a thousand of his officials. He joined all one thousand of them in getting drunk.
5.2: Under the influence of wine, Belshazzar ordered that the gold and silver vessels his grandfather Nebuchadnezzar had taken from the temple in Jerusalem be brought in so the king, his officials, his wives, and his mistresses could drink from them.
5.3: As ordered, they brought in the gold vessels that had been taken from the sanctuary of God's temple in Jerusalem, and the king, his officials, his wives, and mistresses drank from them.
5.4: As they drank the wine, they praised gods of gold, silver, bronze, iron, wood, and stone.
5.5: At that moment, humanlike fingers of a hand appeared near the lamp stand of the royal palace and wrote on the plaster of the wall.
5.6: While the king watched the back of the hand as it was writing, his facial expression changed. Utterly frightened, he lost control of his bowels and his knees knocked together.
5.7: The king cried out to bring in the enchanters, Chaldeans, and astrologers. He announced to the advisors of Babylon, "Whoever can read this writing and tell me its meaning will be clothed in purple, have a gold chain placed around his neck, and will become the third highest ruler in the kingdom."
5.8: Then all the king's advisors came in, but they were unable to read the writing or tell the king what it meant.
5.9: So King Belshazzar became even more frightened, and his facial expression showed it. His officials also were thrown into confusion.
5.10: Hearing the voices of the king and his officials, the queen entered the banquet hall. "Your majesty, live forever," the queen said. "Don't be frightened by your thoughts or allow your facial expression to show it.
5.11: There's a man in your kingdom in whom dwells the spirit of the holy gods. During your grandfather's reign, he was found to have insight, intelligence, and wisdom, like that of the gods. Your grandfather, King Nebuchadnezzar-your kingly predecessor-appointed him to be chief administrator over the magicians, enchanters, Chaldeans, and astrologers,
5.12: because he was found to have an extraordinary spirit, knowledge, and understanding, along with an ability to interpret dreams, explain riddles, and solve difficult problems. His name is Daniel, whom the king renamed Belteshazzar. Call for Daniel, and he will reveal the meaning of the writing."
